topics.title: Design and implement a lexical analyzer using C language to recognize all valid tokens in the input program, ignoring redundant spaces, tabs, newlines, and comments
Write a lex program to display the number of lines, words, and characters in an input text
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL607 - SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a lex program to display the number of lines, words, and characters in an input text

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Lex** is a lexical analyzer generator tool used in System Programming and Compiler Design. It takes a specification file (with extension `.l`) containing **regular expressions** (patterns) paired with **actions written in C**, and produces a C source file (`lex.yy.c`) containing a complete lexical scanner. This scanner, when compiled with a C compiler, produces an executable that can read an input stream, identify tokens matching the given patterns, and execute the corresponding C code.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCSL607 – Systems Lab)**, this experiment is the classical "**Word Count Utility**" problem, analogous in logic to the Unix `wc` command. The objective is to write a Lex program that:

- Counts the **number of lines** (newline characters `\n`).
- Counts the **number of words** (maximal contiguous sequences of non-whitespace characters).
- Counts the **number of characters** (every single character, including whitespace and newlines).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Vocabulary You Must Know**
> - **Token**: A logically grouped sequence of characters matched by a single Lex rule.
> - **Pattern (Regular Expression)**: The left-hand side of a Lex rule; tells *what* to match.
> - **Action (C Code)**: The right-hand side of a Lex rule; tells *what to do* when the pattern matches.
> - **yytext**: A global `char *` pointing to the currently matched text.
> - **yyleng**: An `int` holding the length of the currently matched text.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are a **postman sorting letters in a mailroom**:
- The **incoming mail** is the input text.
- You have a **rulebook** (the Lex `.l` file) that says: *"If you see a letter marked 'URGENT', put it in the red tray; if it's a 'BILL', put it in the yellow tray; otherwise, drop it in the general tray."*
- Each time you handle a letter, you write a tally mark on a specific counter (lines, words, characters).
- The **lexical analyzer** is you — you keep reading mail and applying rules until the bag is empty.

In our program:
- **Newline character** → increment *line counter* (like a paragraph break in a book).
- **A "word"** → a continuous run of letters/digits with no spaces or tabs around it (like a single word in a sentence).
- **Every single character** → increment the *character counter* (think: every letter, space, and punctuation mark counts as one).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment tests three KTU Course Outcomes —
> **CO1** (Apply knowledge of mathematics & engineering to system software problems),
> **CO2** (Design system components using Lex/Yacc),
> and **CO5** (Use modern tools like `flex` and `gcc` to conduct experiments and analyze results).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Lex Program (Three Sections)

A Lex program (`.l` file) has a strict three-part structure separated by `%%`:

| Section | Delimiters | Purpose | Key Syntax |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Declarations** | `%{ ... %}` | Global C variables, `#include`, constants | Wrapped C code, copied verbatim into the generated `.c` file |
| **Rules** | `%%` ... `%%` | Pattern → Action pairs | `pattern { action; }` |
| **Auxiliary Functions** | After second `%%` | `main()`, `yywrap()`, helper functions | Regular C functions |

## 2.2 The Lex Processing Pipeline

The Lex compiler (commonly `flex` on Linux) performs the following transformations:

$$\text{\texttt{program.l}} \xrightarrow{\text{flex}} \text{\texttt{lex.yy.c}} \xrightarrow{\text{gcc}} \text{\texttt{a.out (executable)}}$$

1. **Lex reads** the `.l` file.
2. **Translates each pattern** to a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA).
3. **Emits C code** implementing the DFA into `lex.yy.c`.
4. **GCC compiles** this file (linked with `-lfl` on some systems).
5. **The executable** reads input via `yyin` and runs the `yylex()` scanner.

## 2.3 Pattern Matching Strategy for Word Count

The single most important conceptual point: **Lex picks the longest match, and on ties, the rule listed first wins.**

For our problem, the patterns are:

$$\text{Word} \rightarrow \text{any non-whitespace sequence}$$

$$\text{Line boundary} \rightarrow \text{newline character}$$

$$\text{Whitespace} \rightarrow \text{spaces and tabs}$$

$$\text{Any other character} \rightarrow \text{a catch-all rule for counting characters}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why a catch-all rule `.` is needed**: If you do not include a final rule, characters like punctuation (`.`, `,`, `!`) will be silently **discarded** (Lex's default action is to copy the matched text to output only when `ECHO` is used, and unmatched input is discarded). To count *every* character, the catch-all is essential.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| Concept | Symbol / Syntax | Meaning | Counted as |
|---|---|---|---|
| Newline | `\n` | End of a line | +1 line |
| Word pattern | `[^ \t\n]+` | One or more characters that are NOT space, tab, or newline | +1 word |
| Whitespace | `[ \t]+` | One or more spaces or tabs | counted in characters (length added) |
| Catch-all | `.` | Any single character not matched by other rules | +1 character |
| `yytext` | global `char *` | Pointer to matched string | — |
| `yyleng` | global `int` | Length of `yytext` | added to character count |
| `yywrap()` | user-defined | Returns 1 when input ends | ends `yylex()` |
| `ECHO` | macro | Default action: `fprintf(yyout, "%s", yytext);` | prints matched text |
| `yyin` | `FILE *` | Input stream (default: `stdin`) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Pitfall:** Use `\vert` style in formulas above (e.g., `$\vert x \vert$`) because the `|` character breaks markdown table cells. In your exam scripts, write `|x|` plainly.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same Lex pattern-matching engine powers:

- **Compiler front-ends** (GCC's lexer, Clang's lexer).
- **Text-processing utilities** (`grep`, `awk`, `sed` use similar finite-automata techniques).
- **Syntax highlighters** in IDEs (VS Code, Sublime Text).
- **Network packet inspectors** and protocol analyzers.
- **Data sanitization tools** that strip malformed input.

Mastering this experiment gives you the foundational skill of *declarative pattern-based programming*, which scales from counting words to detecting SQL injection attacks in cybersecurity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Program Construction & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Reference Lex Program (Lab-Ready)

```lex
%{
/* ============================================================
   PROGRAM : Word Count using Lex
   COURSE  : PCCSL607 - Systems Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)
   AIM     : Display the number of lines, words and characters
             in an input text.
   TOOLS   : flex, gcc (Linux) / Lex (Windows)
   ============================================================ */
#include <stdio.h>

/* Global counters — visible to action blocks */
int line_count      = 0;
int word_count      = 0;
int character_count = 0;
%}

/* No helper definitions needed here, but the section must exist */

%%
                /* ---- TRANSLATION RULES ---- */

\n              { line_count++; 
                  character_count += yyleng; }

[ \t]+          { character_count += yyleng; }    /* spaces & tabs */

[^ \t\n]+       { word_count++; 
                  character_count += yyleng; }    /* a 'word'  */

.               { character_count += yyleng; }    /* any other char */
%%

/* ---- AUXILIARY FUNCTIONS ---- */

int yywrap() {
    /* Return 1 to signal end-of-input to yylex() */
    return 1;
}

int main() {
    printf("===== KTU Word Count Utility =====\n");
    printf("Type your text. Press Ctrl+D (Linux) "
           "or Ctrl+Z (Windows) to finish:\n\n");

    yylex();   /* Drive the scanner until yywrap() returns 1 */

    printf("\n===== STATISTICS =====\n");
    printf("Lines      : %d\n", line_count);
    printf("Words      : %d\n", word_count);
    printf("Characters : %d\n", character_count);
    return 0;
}
```

## 3.2 Exhaustive Line-by-Line Walkthrough

### Section 1 — Declarations (`%{ ... %}`)

**Line-by-line reasoning:**

- `#include <stdio.h>` → required for `printf()` used in `main()`.
- `int line_count = 0, word_count = 0, character_count = 0;` → three **mutable global counters** are declared so that the *action blocks* (which become parts of the generated `yylex()` function) can update them. Local variables in `main()` would be invisible inside the action blocks.
- The `%{ ... %}` wrapper tells Flex: *"Copy the enclosed C code verbatim to the top of the generated `lex.yy.c`."*

### Section 2 — Rules (`%% ... %%`)

Each row is a **pattern → action** pair. Flex applies the **maximal-munch** rule.

**Rule 1: `\n`**
$$\text{Pattern: } \backslash n \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Matches one newline character}$$
$$\text{Action: } \texttt{line\_count++; character\_count += yyleng;}$$
Since `\n` has length 1, this adds exactly 1 to the line counter and 1 to the character counter.

**Rule 2: `[ \t]+`**
$$\text{Pattern: } [\, \backslash t\,] + \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{One or more spaces/tabs}$$
$$\text{Action: } \texttt{character\_count += yyleng;}$$
- The `+` quantifier means *one or more*. A single space and ten spaces will be matched as one token.
- We **do not** increment `word_count` here — spaces are not words.
- `yyleng` lets us add the exact number of characters in one go, not character-by-character.

**Rule 3: `[^ \t\n]+`**
$$\text{Pattern: } [\, \widehat{\, \backslash t \backslash n \,}\,] + \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Anything that is NOT a space, tab, or newline}$$
$$\text{Action: } \texttt{word\_count++; character\_count += yyleng;}$$
- The `^` inside `[...]` is the **negation** operator.
- `"Hello, world!"` is split into the tokens `Hello,` and `world!` because the space is excluded.
- Each such maximal run increments the word count **once** — this is precisely how Unix `wc -w` works.

**Rule 4: `.`**
$$\text{Pattern: } . \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Any single character not already matched}$$
$$\text{Action: } \texttt{character\_count += yyleng;}$$
- The dot matches **any character except newline** by default.
- This is a safety net for punctuation like `.`, `,`, `!`, `(`, `)` that isn't covered by Rules 1–3.
- Without this rule, punctuation would be silently discarded.

### Section 3 — Auxiliary Functions

**`yywrap()`**: When `yylex()` reaches end-of-file, it calls this function. Returning `1` (non-zero) tells Lex to stop scanning. In a multi-file scenario, you could `return 0` after opening the next file.

**`main()`**: Initializes the scanner by calling `yylex()`. The scanner runs in a tight loop:
- Read input buffer.
- Find longest match.
- Execute corresponding action.
- Repeat until EOF.
After `yylex()` returns, we print the final tallies.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sample Input Trace

Let us trace the input string `"Hi all.\nWelcome to KTU.\n"` (note: 2 newlines, 3 words, 21 characters).

| Step | Input Remaining | Token Matched | yyleng | line_count | word_count | character_count |
|---:|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | `Hi all.\nWelcome to KTU.\n` | `Hi` (word) | 2 | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | ` all.\nWelcome to KTU.\n` | ` ` (whitespace) | 1 | 0 | 1 | 3 |
| 3 | `all.\nWelcome to KTU.\n` | `all` (word) | 3 | 0 | 2 | 6 |
| 4 | `.\nWelcome to KTU.\n` | `.` (catch-all) | 1 | 0 | 2 | 7 |
| 5 | `\nWelcome to KTU.\n` | `\n` (newline) | 1 | 1 | 2 | 8 |
| 6 | `Welcome to KTU.\n` | `Welcome` (word) | 7 | 1 | 3 | 15 |
| 7 | ` to KTU.\n` | ` ` (whitespace) | 1 | 1 | 3 | 16 |
| 8 | `to KTU.\n` | `to` (word) | 2 | 1 | 4 | 18 |
| 9 | ` KTU.\n` | ` ` (whitespace) | 1 | 1 | 4 | 19 |
| 10 | `KTU.\n` | `KTU` (word) | 3 | 1 | 5 | 22 |
| 11 | `.\n` | `.` (catch-all) | 1 | 1 | 5 | 23 |
| 12 | `\n` | `\n` (newline) | 1 | 2 | 5 | 24 |

**Final Output:**
$$\text{Lines} = 2, \quad \text{Words} = 5, \quad \text{Characters} = 24$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Verification:** The Unix command `wc input.txt` should yield identical counts if your Lex program is correct. Always test against `wc` for lab-record validation.

## 3.4 Compilation & Execution Procedure (For Lab Record)

```bash
# Step 1: Save the program
nano wordcount.l

# Step 2: Generate the C scanner
flex wordcount.l        # produces lex.yy.c

# Step 3: Compile to executable
gcc lex.yy.c -o wordcount -lfl    # -lfl may not be needed in some distros

# Step 4: Run interactively OR with a file
./wordcount                       # type input, press Ctrl+D
./wordcount < input.txt           # redirect a file
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lex Compilation Pipeline (Build Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[wordcount.l - Lex Source] --> B[flex Compiler]
    B --> C[lex.yy.c - Generated C Source]
    C --> D[gcc Compiler]
    D --> E[wordcount - Executable]
    E --> F[Input Text Stream]
    F --> G[Statistics Output]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The `.l` file is the human-written specification.
- `flex` translates regular expressions into C code for a DFA-based scanner.
- `gcc` compiles the C code to a native executable.
- The executable consumes input and emits the three counts.

## 4.2 Lex Scanner Runtime Architecture (Internal Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Buffer yyin stdin] --> B[yylex Scanner Engine]
    B --> C{Pattern Match Phase - Maximal Munch}
    C -->|Newline n found| D[Action: lines++ chars++]
    C -->|Whitespace space tab| E[Action: chars += yyleng]
    C -->|Word sequence non-whitespace| F[Action: words++ chars += yyleng]
    C -->|Other char catch-all| G[Action: chars += yyleng]
    D --> H{Check yywrap}
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H -->|Return 0 - More Input| B
    H -->|Return 1 - EOF| I[Exit yylex]
    I --> J[main prints Lines Words Chars]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The `yylex()` function runs in a loop.
- For each token, it consults the rule table and selects the **longest match**.
- The corresponding C action updates the global counters.
- `yywrap()` acts as a gatekeeper: returning 1 means stop; returning 0 means more input remains.

## 4.3 Pattern-to-Counter Mapping (Data Flow View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pattern: backslash n] --> R1[Rule 1]
    P2[Pattern: bracket space tab bracket plus] --> R2[Rule 2]
    P3[Pattern: bracket caret space tab n bracket plus] --> R3[Rule 3]
    P4[Pattern: dot] --> R4[Rule 4]
    R1 --> C1[Counter: line_count]
    R2 --> C2[Counter: character_count]
    R3 --> C3[Counter: word_count]
    R3 --> C4[Counter: character_count]
    R4 --> C5[Counter: character_count]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Only Rule 1 touches the `line_count` counter.
- Only Rule 3 touches the `word_count` counter.
- Rules 1, 2, 3, and 4 all contribute to `character_count`, ensuring every character is tallied exactly once.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** 
*What is Lex? Mention the three sections of a Lex program.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Lex is a tool (specifically, a lexical analyzer generator) that converts a specification file containing regular expressions and C actions into a C program that performs lexical analysis (tokenization) on an input stream.
- **Three sections (2 marks):**
  1. **Declarations section** — enclosed in `%{ ... %}`, contains C code (variables, includes) copied verbatim to the output.
  2. **Rules section** — enclosed between two `%%` markers, contains `pattern { action }` pairs.
  3. **Auxiliary functions section** — plain C code placed after the second `%%`, typically includes `main()` and `yywrap()`.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** 
*Write a Lex pattern to match: (i) a word, (ii) a newline, (iii) a sequence of one or more spaces/tabs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Token | Lex Pattern | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| Word | `[^ \t\n]+` | One or more chars that are NOT space, tab, or newline |
| Newline | `\n` | The literal newline character |
| Spaces/tabs | `[ \t]+` | One or more characters from the set `{space, tab}` |

The `+` quantifier (1 mark) ensures we match the *maximal* sequence, not character-by-character.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

> **(a)** *With a neat diagram, explain the working of the Lex compiler.* **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** *Write a Lex program that reads a C source file and counts the number of `printf` statements. Display the total at the end.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a) — Lex Compiler Working (7 marks)**

The Lex compiler converts a `.l` specification into an equivalent C program implementing a **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)**.

**Step-by-step working:**

1. **Input phase** *(1 mark)*: Lex reads the `.l` file, separating the three sections (declarations, rules, auxiliary).
2. **Pattern compilation** *(2 marks)*: Each regular expression on the LHS of a rule is converted into an **NFA (Non-deterministic Finite Automaton)** using Thompson's construction. All NFAs are then merged into one master NFA with a new start state.
3. **Subset construction** *(2 marks)*: The NFA is converted to a **DFA** via the subset algorithm, eliminating nondeterminism.
4. **Minimization** *(1 mark)*: The DFA is minimized by merging equivalent states, reducing the number of states for efficiency.
5. **Code emission** *(1 mark)*: Flex emits C code (`lex.yy.c`) that simulates the DFA. Each DFA state has a switch-case emitting the corresponding action. The function `yylex()` is the main scanner driver.

**Block Diagram (textual for answer sheet):**

```
   .l file  ──►  Pattern → NFA  ──►  NFA → DFA  ──►  Minimize DFA
                                                       │
                                                       ▼
                                            Emit C code (lex.yy.c)
                                                       │
                                                       ▼
                                          gcc compile → a.out
```

---

#### **Solution to (b) — Lex Program to Count `printf` Statements (7 marks)**

```lex
%{
#include <stdio.h>
int printf_count = 0;
%}

%%
"printf"      { printf_count++; ECHO; }
.|\n          { ECHO; }
%%

int yywrap() { return 1; }

int main() {
    yylex();
    printf("\nTotal printf statements: %d\n", printf_count);
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of the program (for full marks):**

- The pattern `"printf"` *(literal string in quotes)* matches the exact token `printf` as a unit *(2 marks)*.
- `printf_count++` increments the global counter each time the pattern matches *(1 mark)*.
- `ECHO` is a built-in Lex macro equivalent to `fprintf(yyout, "%s", yytext);` — it preserves the original source on the output *(1 mark)*.
- The catch-all rule `.|\n` (any char OR newline) ensures every other character is echoed so that no input is lost, mirroring the input on stdout *(1 mark)*.
- `yywrap()` returning 1 ends scanning at EOF *(1 mark)*.
- The final count is printed after `yylex()` returns *(1 mark)*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to write the pattern as `printf` (without quotes). Without quotes, Lex interprets it as a *regular expression* (which in this case happens to work but is technically incorrect). For multi-character literals, **always use double quotes**, e.g., `"printf"`. Similarly, students often forget `ECHO`, causing the entire input to be discarded and losing 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

> **(a)** *Explain the difference between `yytext` and `yyleng` with an example.* **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** *Write a Lex program that displays the number of lines, words, and characters in an input text. Explain each rule.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a) — `yytext` vs `yyleng` (7 marks)**

| Property | `yytext` | `yyleng` |
|---|---|---|
| Type | `char *` (pointer) | `int` |
| Value | Points to the matched text in the input buffer | Length of the matched text in characters |
| Memory | Located in the internal input buffer | A simple integer variable |
| Usage | `printf("%s", yytext);` to print the match | `chars += yyleng;` for counting |
| Null-terminated? | Yes | N/A |

**Worked example (4 marks):**

Suppose the input contains the word `"Hello"` and the pattern `[a-zA-Z]+` matches it.

- After the match:
$$\text{yytext} = \text{`"Hello"'} \quad (\text{pointer to characters H, e, l, l, o, }\backslash 0)$$
$$\text{yyleng} = 5$$

**Code demonstration:**
```c
printf("Matched: %s\n", yytext);   /* Output: Matched: Hello */
printf("Length : %d\n", yyleng);   /* Output: Length : 5      */
```

- `yytext` is essential when you need to *inspect the content* (e.g., to look up a symbol table entry).
- `yyleng` is essential when you need to *count characters* or skip ahead by a known amount.

---

#### **Solution to (b) — Lines, Words, Characters Program (7 marks)**

**Full program** (as developed in Section 3.1) carries 4 marks. The remaining 3 marks go to the **explanation of each rule**:

**Rule 1: `\n  { line_count++; character_count += yyleng; }`** *(1 mark)*
- Matches a newline character; increments the line counter by 1.
- Adds the newline's length (1) to the character counter.

**Rule 2: `[ \t]+  { character_count += yyleng; }`** *(1 mark)*
- Matches one or more consecutive spaces or tabs as a single token (efficient).
- Adds the matched length to the character counter; **does not** count as a word.

**Rule 3: `[^ \t\n]+  { word_count++; character_count += yyleng; }`** *(1 mark)*
- Matches any maximal run of non-whitespace characters.
- The negation `^` inside `[...]` excludes spaces, tabs, and newlines.
- Each match represents one word.

**Rule 4: `.  { character_count += yyleng; }`** *(1 mark)*
- Catch-all rule for any single character not covered above (e.g., punctuation).
- Without this rule, unmatched characters are silently discarded.

**Auxiliary functions:** *(1 mark for both)*
- `yywrap()` returns 1 to terminate `yylex()` at EOF.
- `main()` calls `yylex()` and prints the final counts.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A frequent error is using `\n+` instead of `\n` for the line rule. Using `\n+` collapses multiple consecutive newlines into a single match, undercounting blank lines. Always use the single-character pattern `\n` for accurate line counting. Another trap: students often omit the catch-all `.` rule, causing punctuation to vanish and lowering the character count — losing 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Lex is a pattern-action scanner generator**, not a parser; it tokenizes but does not understand grammar.
- [x] A `.l` file has **three sections**: declarations (`%{ ... %}`), rules (`%% ... %%`), and C functions (after the second `%%`).
- [x] `flex wordcount.l` generates `lex.yy.c`; compile with `gcc lex.yy.c -o wordcount`.
- [x] **Line counter** uses pattern `\n` with action `line_count++`.
- [x] **Word counter** uses pattern `[^ \t\n]+` with action `word_count++`.
- [x] **Character counter** must be updated in *every* rule using `character_count += yyleng;`.
- [x] **Catch-all rule `.`** is mandatory to prevent silent character loss.
- [x] `yytext` is a `char *` to the matched text; `yyleng` is its length.
- [x] `yywrap()` returning 1 tells Lex that input is exhausted.
- [x] **Rule precedence**: Lex picks the **longest** match; on ties, the **first** listed rule wins.
- [x] **Pattern quoting**: Multi-character literals (e.g., keywords) must be in double quotes: `"printf"`.
- [x] **Global variables** must be declared in the `%{ ... %}` block so action code can access them.
- [x] **Quantifiers**: `+` (one or more), `*` (zero or more), `?` (zero or one).
- [x] **Character classes**: `[abc]` matches any one of `a`, `b`, `c`; `[^abc]` matches anything *except* them.
- [x] Always cross-verify your Lex output with the Unix `wc` command before submitting the lab record.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generate a YACC specification to recognize a valid arithmetic expression that uses operators and parenthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCSL607 - SYSTEMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Generate a YACC specification to recognize a valid arithmetic expression that uses operators and parenthesis ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **YACC (Yet Another Compiler Compiler)** is a parser generator tool that takes a context-free grammar specification written in **Backus-Naur Form (BNF)** and automatically generates a C-language parser using the **LALR(1) (Look-Ahead Left-to-Right with one token look-ahead)** parsing algorithm. It is the standard UNIX tool for building the **syntax analysis (parsing) phase** of a compiler. For this experiment, YACC is used to *recognize a valid arithmetic expression* — i.e., the parser must: 1. Accept any syntactically legal infix arithmetic expression built from `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `(`, `)` and integer operands. 2. Reject any malformed input (mismatched brackets, dangling operators, illegal tokens). 3. Optionally **evaluate** the expression during parsing (synthesized attribute evaluation). > [!IMPORTANT] > **YACC is the parser (grammar) generator; LEX/FLEX is the tokenizer (lexer) generator.** They work together: LEX produces tokens, YACC consumes them to build a parse tree. In KTU labs they are almost always paired. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Strict Math Teacher" Imagine a very strict math teacher who follows a fixed set of rules: - "You may not write two operators in a row" (no `++3` or `*/5`). - "Every opening parenthesis `(` must have a matching `)`." - "Multiplication is solved before addition" (precedence). - "Operators of equal weight are evaluated left to right" (left-associativity). The **LEX program** is like the teacher's *reading glasses* — it identifies the raw symbols (digits, `+`, `*`, `(`, etc.). The **YACC program** is the *set of grammar rules* in the teacher's mind that decides whether what was read is a valid sentence. The student (input) either gets a green tick (parsed) or a red cross (syntax error with line number). ### 1.3 Why YACC and not a Hand-Written Parser? | Hand-written recursive descent | YACC-generated LALR(1) parser | | :--- | :--- | | Verbose, error-prone for big grammars | Concise BNF-style specification | | No automatic conflict detection | Built-in **shift/reduce** & **reduce/reduce** conflict reports | | Re-implement for every grammar | Re-generate by recompiling `.y` file | | Difficult to maintain precedence/associativity | Declarative `%left` / `%right` / `%nonassoc` | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The experiment tests your ability to (a) write the `.y` file, (b) write the paired `.l` (LEX) file, (c) compile using `yacc -d` and `lex`, and (d) demonstrate with valid/invalid inputs in the record. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 The Architecture of a YACC Program A YACC specification file (`arith.y`) is divided into three sections separated by the **`%%`** delimiter: ``` ───────── ────────────── ───── Section 1 Section 2 Section 3 (Declarations)(Grammar (C-Code Rules) Routines) ───────── ────────────── ───── %% %% %% ``` - **Section 1 — Declarations:** C code (`%{ ... %}`), token declarations (`%token`), precedence/associativity (`%left`, `%right`, `%nonassoc`), and type declarations (`%union`, `%type`). - **Section 2 — Grammar Rules:** Production rules of the form `nonterminal : body1 | body2 | ... ;` Each body can carry a **semantic action** `{ ... }` that computes the value of the left-hand side (LHS) nonterminal. - **Section 3 — Auxiliary Routines:** C functions such as `main()`, `yyerror()`, and any user-defined helpers. These are copied verbatim into the generated `y.tab.c`. ### 2.2 Token Categories Used in This Experiment | Token | Lex Regular Expression | Returned Token Name | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Integer literal | `[0-9]+` | `NUMBER` (with `yylval` = numeric value) | | `+` | `\+` | `'+'` (character literal) | | `-` | `\-` | `'-'` | | `*` | `\*` | `'*'` | | `/` | `\/` | `'/'` | | `(` | `\(` | `'('` | | `)` | `\)` | `')'` | | Whitespace | `[ \t]` | *(ignored, no return)* | | Newline | `\n` | `'\n'` (statement terminator) | | Any other char | `.` | `yytext[0]` (single-char token) | ### 2.3 Operator Precedence & Associativity — The Heart of Arithmetic Parsing YACC resolves the classic ambiguity of the "dangling else" / "dangling operator" problem through **declarative precedence**. Lower declaration = **lower** precedence (binds less tightly). All operators declared on the **same line** share **equal precedence** and the declared associativity. For arithmetic expressions the standard declaration block is: ```c %left '+' '-' /* lowest precedence — addition/subtraction */ %left '*' '/' /* higher precedence — multiplication/division */ %right 'U' /* (optional) unary minus — highest */ ``` - `2 + 3 * 4` is parsed as `2 + (3 * 4)` because `*` is declared *above* `+`, hence binds tighter. - `10 - 4 - 3` is parsed as `(10 - 4) - 3 = 3` because `'-'` is `%left` (left-associative). - `8 / 2 / 2` parses as `(8 / 2) / 2 = 2` for the same reason. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** Examiners *love* asking: *"Why is your grammar not ambiguous? How did you resolve the shift-reduce conflict?"* The answer is *always* — the `%left`/`%right` declarations. Without them, `yacc` would print `warning: shift/reduce conflict` and parse the expression right-to-left, which is wrong for `-` and `/`. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Notation | Meaning | Order | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Left-associative | `%left` | `a-b-c = (a-b)-c` | Same precedence, left-to-right | | Right-associative | `%right` | `a^b^c = a^(b^c)` | Same precedence, right-to-left | | Non-associative | `%nonassoc` | `a==b==c` is a syntax error | No chaining allowed | | Semantic value (LHS) | `$$` | Value being computed for LHS nonterminal | — | | Semantic value (i-th RHS symbol) | `$1`, `$2`, `$3` … | Value carried up from the i-th symbol in the rule | — | | Start symbol | First LHS in Section 2 | Root of the parse tree | — | | Lookahead | 1 token (LALR(1)) | Used to decide shift vs reduce | — | > [!WARNING] > Avoid using the vertical bar `|` inside a `$$` expression when writing on paper — it will be confused with the grammar alternation bar. Use words like *"the value of $1 plus the value of $3"* when transcribing. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compiler Construction:** Front-ends of GCC, Clang, and most language translators use LALR parser generators (Bison, the GNU YACC, is the de-facto open-source replacement). - **Expression Evaluators in Calculators & Spreadsheets:** Excel formula parsers, scientific calculators, and CAS engines (Mathematica's underlying engine) all use precedence-climbing or LALR techniques. - **Configuration & Query Languages:** SQL parsers, JSON-with-comments tools, and even hardware description languages (Verilog, VHDL parsers) leverage YACC-style BNF. - **Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs):** Modern frameworks like **ANTLR** (LL(*)*) and **Bison** (LALR(1)) trace their lineage to YACC. ## 3. Step-by-Step Code, Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 The Grammar (Production Rules) The Context-Free Grammar (CFG) for the arithmetic expression language is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{stmt} & \rightarrow \text{ expr } \backslash\text{n} \\ \text{expr} & \rightarrow \text{ expr } + \text{ expr } \;\vert\; \text{ expr } - \text{ expr } \\ & \rightarrow \text{ expr } * \text{ expr } \;\vert\; \text{ expr } / \text{ expr } \\ & \rightarrow \text{ ( expr ) } \;\vert\; \text{ NUMBER} \end{aligned} $$ The grammar is **ambiguous** on its own (no precedence), so the `%left` directives in the `.y` file resolve the ambiguity — this is *the* classical demonstration of why YACC needs precedence declarations. ### 3.2 The Paired LEX File — `arith.l` ```c %{ #include "y.tab.h" /* pulls in token definitions from yacc -d */ #include /* for atoi() */ %} /* No auxiliary declarations needed */ /* Regular definitions and rules */ %% [0-9]+ { yylval = atoi(yytext); return NUMBER; } [ \t] ; /* skip whitespace */ \n return '\n'; /* statement terminator */ . return yytext[0]; /* single-char operators + ( ) */ %% /* No auxiliary functions in LEX */ ``` **Explanation of each rule:** - `[0-9]+` — One or more digits. `atoi()` converts the matched string to an integer which is stored in `yylval` (the default value-carrier between LEX and YACC). Returns the token `NUMBER`. - `[ \t]` — Whitespace. The **empty action** `;` means the matched text is discarded and scanning continues. - `\n` — Newline. Returned as the literal character `'\n'` so that YACC can use it as a statement terminator. - `.` — Catch-all single character. Returns the character itself, which works for `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `(`, `)` because they are all single-byte tokens. ### 3.3 The YACC File — `arith.y` ```c %{ #include #include int yylex(void); /* LEX-generated scanner */ void yyerror(const char *s); /* error routine */ %} /* ----- Token Declarations ----- */ %token NUMBER /* ----- Precedence (low → high) and Associativity ----- */ %left '+' '-' %left '*' '/' /* ----- Grammar Rules ----- */ %% stmt : expr '\n' { printf("Result = %d\n", $1); } ; expr : expr '+' expr { $$ = $1 + $3; } | expr '-' expr { $$ = $1 - $3; } | expr '*' expr { $$ = $1 * $3; } | expr '/' expr { if ($3 == 0) { yyerror("Division by zero"); $$ = 0; } else $$ = $1 / $3; } | '(' expr ')' { $$ = $2; } | NUMBER { $$ = $1; } ; %% /* ----- Auxiliary Routines ----- */ void yyerror(const char *s) { fprintf(stderr, "Syntax Error: %s\n", s); } int main(void) { printf("Enter an arithmetic expression (Ctrl-D to exit):\n"); return yyparse(); /* generated parser entry point */ } ``` ### 3.4 Line-by-Line Semantic Analysis | Line / Rule | What `$$`, `$1`, `$2`, `$3` mean | Type of the values | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `expr '+' expr { $$ = $1 + $3; }` | `$$` = result of *this* expr rule. `$1` = value of left sub-expr. `$3` = value of right sub-expr. `$2` is the `'+'` token — no value. | `int` (YACC's default for `yylval`) | | `'(' expr ')' { $$ = $2; }` | `$$` takes the value of the inner expr. `$1` and `$3` are the brackets, no value. | `int` | | `NUMBER { $$ = $1; }` | `$$` is set to the integer `atoi()` produced in the LEX action. | `int` | | `stmt : expr '\n' { printf(... $1 ...); }` | The whole expression's value is `$1` (the only non-token symbol). It is printed when the statement reduces. | `int` | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **default `yylval` type is `int`**. If you want to support *real numbers (floats)* you must declare `%union { int ival; double dval; }` and annotate each token/rule with its type, e.g., `%token NUMBER` and `expr : ... { $$ = $1 + $3; }` where `expr` is declared `%type `. For the KTU base experiment, **integers are sufficient** and avoid union complexity. ### 3.5 Step-by-Step Build and Run Procedure ```bash # Step 1: Generate the parser (y.tab.c and y.tab.h) from arith.y yacc -d arith.y # Step 2: Generate the scanner (lex.yy.c) from arith.l lex arith.l # Step 3: Compile everything together gcc lex.yy.c y.tab.c -o arith -ll # -ll links the libfl LEX runtime # Step 4: Run ./arith ``` > [!NOTE] > On modern Linux, `lex` may already be `flex` (a compatible FOSS replacement). The library flag is `-lfl` on some systems. If you get `undefined reference to yywrap`, append `%option noyywrap` at the top of the `.l` file. ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sample Inputs and Expected Outputs | # | Input | Expected Output | Reason | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | `2+3\n` | `Result = 5` | Simple addition. | | 2 | `10-4-3\n` | `Result = 3` | Left-associative subtraction: `(10-4)-3`. | | 3 | `2+3*4\n` | `Result = 14` | Precedence: `2+(3*4)`, not `(2+3)*4`. | | 4 | `(2+3)*4\n` | `Result = 20` | Parentheses override precedence. | | 5 | `8/2/2\n` | `Result = 2` | Left-associative division: `(8/2)/2`. | | 6 | `((7-3)*(2+1))\n` | `Result = 12` | Nested parentheses work. | | 7 | `2++3\n` | `Syntax Error` | Two operators in a row is not a valid expression. | | 8 | `(2+3\n` | `Syntax Error` | Unmatched parenthesis. | | 9 | `5/0\n` | `Syntax Error: Division by zero` (but parser succeeds, semantic error) | Caught in the action, not the grammar. | | 10 | `12+3*4-6/2\n` | `Result = 21` | `12 + (3*4) - (6/2) = 12 + 12 - 3 = 21`. | ### 3.7 Detailed Trace of the Parse for `2+3*4` (Shift/Reduce Walk) The input is streamed as tokens: `NUMBER(2)`, `'+'`, `NUMBER(3)`, `'*'`, `NUMBER(4)`, `'\n'`. The parser maintains a **stack** and consults the next token (look-ahead) at every step. | Step | Stack (top on right) | Look-ahead | Action | | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | | 1 | `$` | `2` | Shift `NUMBER(2)`, push value 2. | | 2 | `$ 2` | `+` | Reduce `NUMBER → expr` (by `expr : NUMBER`). Push `expr` with value 2. | | 3 | `$ expr` | `+` | Shift `'+'`. | | 4 | `$ expr +` | `3` | Shift `NUMBER(3)`. | | 5 | `$ expr + 3` | `*` | Reduce `NUMBER → expr`. | | 6 | `$ expr + expr` | `*` | **Decision point:** shift or reduce `expr+expr`? Look-ahead `*` has *higher* precedence than `+` (declared later in `%left`), so **shift** the `*`. | | 7 | `$ expr + expr *` | `4` | Shift `NUMBER(4)`. | | 8 | `$ expr + expr * 4` | `\n` | Reduce `NUMBER → expr`. | | 9 | `$ expr + expr * expr` | `\n` | Reduce `expr * expr → expr` (semantic: `$$=3*4=12`). | | 10 | `$ expr + expr` | `\n` | Reduce `expr + expr → expr` (semantic: `$$=2+12=14`). | | 11 | `$ expr` | `\n` | Shift `'\n'`. | | 12 | `$ expr \n` | EOF | Reduce `expr '\n' → stmt`, print `Result = 14`. | | 13 | `$` | EOF | **Accept!** | The final `$$` after the last reduction is **14** — exactly the correct mathematical answer. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End Compilation Pipeline (LEX → YACC → Binary) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["arith.l
LEX source"] B["arith.y
YACC source"] C["lex.yy.c

Parser in C"] E["y.tab.h
Token IDs header"] F["arith
Executable binary"] G["User Input
e.g. 2+3*4"] H["Output
Result = 14"] A -- "lex / flex" --> C B -- "yacc -d" --> D B -- "yacc -d" --> E C -- "gcc includes y.tab.h" --> F D -- "gcc compiles" --> F E -- "gcc includes" --> F G -- "stdin" --> F F -- "stdout" --> H style A fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style B fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366 style F fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006600 style H fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000 ``` ### 4.2 Parse Tree for the Expression `(2+3)*4` This tree is built by the LALR(1) parser; each internal node is a grammar production, and the leaves are the input tokens. ```mermaid graph TD S["stmt"] E1["expr

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]** `[3 Marks]` > **Q.** What is YACC? Mention any two differences between YACC and LEX. **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** - **YACC (Yet Another Compiler Compiler)** is a parser generator that takes a grammar in BNF/Backus-Naur Form and generates a C parser using the LALR(1) parsing algorithm. *(1 mark)* - Two differences: *(2 marks)* 1. **Purpose:** LEX is a *lexer/scanner generator* — it converts input characters into *tokens*. YACC is a *parser generator* — it takes those tokens and checks whether they form a *valid syntactic structure* (a sentence of the grammar). 2. **Output:** LEX produces a finite automaton (transition table) that recognises regular expressions. YACC produces a push-down automaton (parse stack + LALR tables) that recognises context-free grammars. 3. **Input language:** LEX rules are *regular expressions*. YACC rules are *production rules* of the form `A : B C D ;`. > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]** `[3 Marks]` > **Q.** Define *shift-reduce conflict* in YACC. How is it resolved in an arithmetic-expression grammar? **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** - A *shift-reduce conflict* occurs when the parser, holding a handle on top of the stack and looking at the next input token, cannot uniquely decide whether to **shift** the token onto the stack or **reduce** the handle by some production. YACC reports this as `yacc: warning: … shift/reduce conflict`. *(1 mark)* - In the arithmetic-expression grammar, the conflict occurs with input like `2+3*4` after seeing `expr + expr` and looking at `*`: do we reduce `2+3` first, or shift `*`? *(1 mark)* - It is resolved **declaratively** by the precedence and associativity lines `%left '+' '-' / %left '*' '/'` in the declaration section. Because `*` has higher precedence, the parser *shifts* `*`, binding it tightly to `3` and `4`. *(1 mark)* --- ### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]** `[14 Marks]` > **(a)** Write a YACC specification to recognize and evaluate arithmetic expressions consisting of integers, the binary operators `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, and parentheses `(` `)`. The expression is terminated by a newline. State the precedence and associativity rules used. `[7 Marks]` > **(b)** Write the corresponding LEX program. Show the steps to compile and run, and demonstrate three sample inputs (one valid with parentheses overriding precedence, one triggering a syntax error, and one demonstrating left-associativity of subtraction). `[7 Marks]` #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — File `arith.y`:** *(5 marks)* ```c %{ #include int yylex(void); void yyerror(const char *s); %} %token NUMBER %left '+' '-' %left '*' '/' %% stmt : expr '\n' { printf("Result = %d\n", $1); } ; expr : expr '+' expr { $$ = $1 + $3; } | expr '-' expr { $$ = $1 - $3; } | expr '*' expr { $$ = $1 * $3; } | expr '/' expr { if ($3 == 0) yyerror("Divide by zero"); else $$ = $1 / $3; } | '(' expr ')' { $$ = $2; } | NUMBER { $$ = $1; } ; %% void yyerror(const char *s) { fprintf(stderr, "Syntax Error: %s\n", s); } int main(void) { printf("Enter expression:\n"); return yyparse(); } ``` **Step 2 — Precedence/associativity statement:** *(2 marks)* - `+` and `-` are declared *first* → **lowest precedence**. - `*` and `/` are declared *second* → **higher precedence** (bind more tightly). - Both lines use `%left` → **left-associative** (so `a-b-c = (a-b)-c` and `a/b/c = (a/b)/c`). - The production `expr : expr op expr` is **ambiguous on its own**; the precedence declarations resolve all conflicts — YACC will report *zero* conflicts at compile time. --- #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — File `arith.l`:** *(2 marks)* ```c %{ #include "y.tab.h" #include %} %% [0-9]+ { yylval = atoi(yytext); return NUMBER; } [ \t] ; \n return '\n'; . return yytext[0]; %% ``` **Step 2 — Build steps:** *(2 marks)* ```bash yacc -d arith.y # produces y.tab.c and y.tab.h lex arith.l # produces lex.yy.c gcc y.tab.c lex.yy.c -o arith -ll ./arith ``` **Step 3 — Three sample runs:** *(3 marks — 1 mark each)* | Input | Output | What it demonstrates | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `(2+3)*4` | `Result = 20` | **Parentheses override precedence** — multiplication is forced to happen on the sum first, yielding `5*4=20` instead of `2+(3*4)=14`. | | `2++3` | `Syntax Error` | **Invalid input** — two operators in a row. The parser cannot reduce or shift to a valid handle, so `yyerror` is called. | | `10-4-3` | `Result = 3` | **Left-associativity** — parses as `(10-4)-3 = 3`, NOT `10-(4-3) = 9`. This is enforced by `%left '-'`. | --- > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B1 (Internal Choice Alternative)]** `[14 Marks]` > **(a)** Explain the role of semantic actions in a YACC specification. Using the production `expr : expr '+' expr`, describe how the value of the left-hand side `$$` is computed from `$1` and `$3`. Why is `$2` not used in the addition? `[7 Marks]` > **(b)** Modify the YACC specification of Q.A1 to additionally support a **unary minus** operator (e.g., `-5+3`, `-(2+3)*4`). Show the precedence declaration, the new grammar rule, and verify the output for the inputs `-5+3` and `-(2+3)*4`. `[7 Marks]` #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks - **Role of semantic actions:** They are the C code blocks `{ ... }` attached to each production in Section 2 of the YACC file. They execute *exactly when* the parser reduces that production. They are used to **build the parse tree, compute values, generate intermediate code, emit error messages, or update symbol tables** — i.e., they give the parser a *meaning* (semantics) for each syntactically valid phrase. *(3 marks)* - **For `expr : expr '+' expr { $$ = $1 + $3; }`:** When this rule is reduced, the parser has already computed the integer values of the *left* sub-expression (call it `L`) and the *right* sub-expression (call it `R`). The semantic action assigns their sum to `$$`, which becomes the value of the *new* `expr` node being constructed. So if the input is `2+3*4`, when the parser finally reduces the `+`, the LHS of the `+` is `expr → 2` (value 2) and the RHS is `expr → expr*expr` (value 12), giving `$$ = 2 + 12 = 14`. *(2 marks)* - **Why `$2` is not used:** `$2` corresponds to the *second symbol on the right-hand side* of the production, which is the **token `'+'`** itself. Tokens do not carry numeric values in this grammar (no `%union` is declared, so `yylval` is never assigned for the `+` character). Even if it did, adding a non-numeric token to itself is meaningless. Only the two *expression operands* contribute numerical values. *(2 marks)* #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks **Precedence block changes** — Unary minus must bind *tighter* than `*` and `/`: *(2 marks)* ```c %left '+' '-' %left '*' '/' %right 'U' /* 'U' is a fictitious token marking a unary-minus occurrence */ ``` **New grammar rule for unary minus:** *(3 marks)* ```c expr : '-' expr %prec 'U' { $$ = -$2; } | expr '+' expr { $$ = $1 + $3; } | expr '-' expr { $$ = $1 - $3; } | expr '*' expr { $$ = $1 * $3; } | expr '/' expr { if ($3 == 0) yyerror("Divide by zero"); else $$ = $1 / $3; } | '(' expr ')' { $$ = $2; } | NUMBER { $$ = $1; } ; ``` The directive **`%prec 'U'`** overrides the default precedence of this particular production with that of the token `'U'`, which is `%right` and declared *above* `*` and `/`, ensuring `-5+3` is parsed as `(-5)+3 = -2`, not `-(5+3) = -8`. **Verification:** *(2 marks)* | Input | Parse Tree | Output | | :---: | :--- | :---: | | `-5+3` | `((-5) + 3)` | `Result = -2` | | `-(2+3)*4` | `(-(2+3)) * 4 = (-5)*4` | `Result = -20` | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deducting Mistakes** > 1. **Forgetting `%left` / `%right` lines** → YACC prints `shift/reduce conflict` warnings and your output becomes *wrong* (e.g., `2+3*4` returns `20` instead of `14`). Examiner will give 0 for the precedence part. *(Lose up to 2 marks)* > 2. **Confusing `$1`, `$2`, `$3` indexing** → `$i` counts *all* symbols on the RHS including tokens. In `expr : expr '+' expr`, `expr` is `$1`, `'+'` is `$2`, `expr` is `$3`. Writing `$$ = $1 + $2` is a common mistake and produces garbage. *(Lose up to 1 mark)* > 3. **Missing the `'\n'` rule** → The parser will read multiple expressions without ever printing a result. Always end the top-level production with a newline token so each line is independently evaluated. *(Lose up to 1 mark)* > 4. **Not including `#include "y.tab.h"` in the LEX file** → Tokens like `NUMBER` will be undeclared, giving cryptic compile errors. *(Lose up to 1 mark)* > 5. **Using `yytext` after it has been overwritten** → `atoi(yytext)` must be called *inside* the action, immediately. Storing `yytext` in a global pointer and using it later is a classic bug. > 6. **Omitting the `void yyerror(const char *s)` function** → Linker error: `undefined reference to yyerror`. Always define it (even if it just prints). --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **YACC** generates an **LALR(1) parser** from a **BNF grammar**; **LEX** generates a **scanner** from **regular expressions**. They are a pair. - A YACC file has **three sections** separated by `%%`: *Declarations*, *Grammar Rules*, *C Routines*. - Use **`%token NAME`** to declare non-character tokens and **`%left` / `%right` / `%nonassoc`** to declare precedence (lower in the file = lower precedence; same line = same precedence with declared associativity). - Arithmetic grammar template: `expr : expr op expr | ( expr ) | NUMBER` — *must* be accompanied by `%left` directives or you will get shift/reduce conflicts. - Semantic action placeholders: `$$` = LHS value, `$1` `$2` … `$N` = values of the 1st, 2nd, … Nth RHS symbols. - Top-level rule (`stmt`) should consume `'\n'` so each input line is a self-contained evaluation. - Common build command: `yacc -d file.y && lex file.l && gcc y.tab.c lex.yy.c -o out -ll`. - Errors fall into two categories: **syntax errors** (caught by the parser → `yyerror`) and **semantic errors** (e.g., division by zero — must be checked in the action). - Real-world equivalents: **Bison** (GNU), **BYACC** (Berkeley), **ANTLR** (LL(*)*, Java/Cpp). - **Default `yylval` is `int`** — for floats use `%union { int ival; double dval; }` and tag every token/rule with its field (``, ``). - Always **declare `yyerror` in the C prolog** (`void yyerror(const char *s);`) and **define it in Section 3** to avoid linker errors. - **Unary operators** use the `%prec` directive to inherit precedence from a fictitious token declared at the desired level. - KTU record deliverables: the `.y` source, the `.l` source, a build script or build steps, and a minimum of **3 valid + 2 invalid** I/O demonstrations with outputs.

topics.title: Implementation of Calculator using LEX and YACC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL607 - SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation of Calculator using LEX and YACC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **LEX/YACC-based Calculator** is a software application that uses a *lexical analyzer* generated by **LEX (Lexical Analyzer Generator)** to tokenize arithmetic input expressions, and a *parser/evaluator* generated by **YACC (Yet Another Compiler-Compiler)** to interpret the grammar and evaluate the expression according to defined precedence and associativity rules.

The implementation is typically split into **three source files**:
1. **`calc.l`** — The **LEX specification file** containing token declarations, regular expressions, and C actions executed when each pattern matches.
2. **`calc.y`** — The **YACC grammar file** containing token declarations, precedence rules, type definitions (`%union`/`%token`/`%type`), grammar productions, and semantic actions.
3. **`calc.c` / `main()`** — A driver program that repeatedly calls `yyparse()` until input ends or an error occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Mapping (CO2):**
> This experiment maps to **CO2: *Implement lexical analyzer and parser using LEX and YACC tools***. Students must be able to write both the `.l` and `.y` files from scratch, compile them using the `lex`/`yacc` (or `flex`/`bison`) toolchain, and trace token-to-parse-tree evaluation for any given input expression.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World View)

Think of a **human waiter at a restaurant who speaks two languages**:

- **LEX** is like the waiter **reading your handwritten order** ("2 pizzas + 1 coke") and translating it into a structured list of items: `NUMBER: 2`, `WORD: pizzas`, `OPERATOR: +`, `NUMBER: 1`, `WORD: coke`. The waiter doesn't understand the *meaning* — only the *shape* of the words.

- **YACC** is like the **kitchen manager** who takes the structured list, understands the *grammar of an order* (you can't say "+ 2 pizzas" — that's invalid), and computes the final bill by following a recipe of rules: "multiply each item by its quantity, then sum totals."

Together, they turn **"3 + 4 * 2"** into the number **11** (not 14!) because YACC knows `*` binds tighter than `+` — exactly like the manager knows the dessert comes *after* the main course, not before.

### 1.3 Key Tools & Reserved Identifiers

| Tool / Identifier | Role | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| **LEX / Flex** | Generates `lex.yy.c` (the DFA-based scanner) | Eric Schmidt & Mike Lesk, 1975 |
| **YACC / Bison** | Generates `y.tab.c` (the LALR parser) | Stephen C. Johnson, AT&T Bell Labs, 1973 |
| `yylex()` | Function called by parser to fetch next token | **yy** = "yet yet" convention |
| `yyparse()` | Function that drives the entire parser | Auto-generated |
| `yylval` | Variable holding the *value* of the current token | Union type from `%union` |
| `yytext` | Pointer to the matched input string | Set by `yylex()` |
| `yyin` | Input file pointer (defaults to `stdin`) | Global `FILE *` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern Linux labs (Ubuntu/Fedora used by KTU), use **Flex** and **Bison** (GNU replacements). Commands are `flex calc.l` and `bison -d calc.y` (the `-d` flag generates `y.tab.h` containing token macros that the `.l` file `#include`s).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a LEX/YACC Application

A LEX/YACC calculator follows a strict **3-stage pipeline**:

**Stage 1 — Lexical Analysis (Tokenization):**
The LEX-generated scanner reads the raw input character stream and groups characters into **lexemes** (the matched substrings) mapped to **token classes** (the categories). For a calculator, the token classes are minimal:

| Token Class | Lexeme Examples | Regular Expression (LEX) |
|---|---|---|
| `NUMBER` | `42`, `3.14`, `0`, `.5` | `[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?` |
| `PLUS` | `+` | `\+` |
| `MINUS` | `-` | `-` |
| `MUL` | `*` | `\*` |
| `DIV` | `/` | `/` |
| `LPAREN` | `(` | `\(` |
| `RPAREN` | `)` | `\)` |
| `NEWLINE` | `\n` | `\n` |
| (whitespace) | ` `, `\t` | `[ \t]+` |

**Stage 2 — Syntax Analysis (Parsing):**
The YACC-generated LALR(1) parser consumes the token stream and builds a **parse tree** (or directly evaluates via semantic actions). Grammar rules for a simple calculator are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S   &\rightarrow E\ \text{NEWLINE} \\
E   &\rightarrow E\ \text{PLUS}\ T \mid E\ \text{MINUS}\ T \mid T \\
T   &\rightarrow T\ \text{MUL}\ F \mid T\ \text{DIV}\ F \mid F \\
F   &\rightarrow \text{LPAREN}\ E\ \text{RPAREN} \mid \text{NUMBER}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the classic **arithmetic grammar** that gives `*` and `/` higher precedence than `+` and `-` and enforces **left-associativity**.

**Stage 3 — Evaluation (Semantic Action):**
Each grammar rule has a C code block attached. When the parser reduces by a rule, the action executes. The value is propagated upward via `$$` (the result of the current production) and `$1`, `$2`, `$3` (the values of symbols on the right-hand side).

### 2.2 Operator Precedence and Associativity

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the grammar enforce precedence?**
> The way the grammar is *layered* (E for +/- level, T for */ level, F for parentheses/atoms) creates an implicit precedence. An expression like `3 + 4 * 5` must be parsed as `3 + (4 * 5)`, not `(3 + 4) * 5`. The `T` non-terminal "binds tighter" because it's reached only after the `E` level is closed.

| Operator | Precedence Level | Associativity | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `+`, `-` | **Lowest** (1) | Left | `a - b - c` = `(a - b) - c` |
| `*`, `/` | **Medium** (2) | Left | `a / b / c` = `(a / b) / c` |
| `(`, `)` | **Highest** (3) | N/A | Grouping overrides everything |

### 2.3 The `%union`, `%token`, and `%type` Declarations

These are **mandatory** in the `.y` file when you want to pass numeric values between LEX and YACC:

- **`%union { int ival; float fval; }`** — Declares a C union that will be the *type* of `yylval`. Since `yylval` must be able to hold different token types, a union is used.
- **`%token <ival> NUMBER`** — Declares `NUMBER` as a token whose semantic value is an **integer** in the `ival` field of the union.
- **`%type <fval> E T F`** — Declares the non-terminals `E`, `T`, `F` as carrying **floating-point** values.
- **`%left PLUS MINUS`** and **`%left MUL DIV`** — Declares tokens and their precedence (later = higher). `LEFT` specifies left-associativity, which is used by YACC's conflict resolver to break shift/reduce conflicts in our favor.

### 2.4 The Semantic Value Flow (`$$` and `$n`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of YACC Actions:**
> If a production has `n` symbols on the right-hand side, the semantic values are accessed as `$1`, `$2`, ..., `$n` (one-indexed from the left). The *result* of the reduction is stored in `$$` (double dollar). This is how values "flow" up the parse tree.

**Example:** For the production `E : E PLUS T`, the action is:
```c
{ $$ = $1 + $3; }
```
Here, `$1` is the value of the left `E`, `$3` is the value of `T`, and `$$` is the new value of the resulting `E` (left `E` + `T`).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Compilation & Execution Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exact sequence** KTU expects you to write in your lab record. Memorize it.

| Step | Command | Purpose | Output File |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `bison -d calc.y` | Generate parser + header | `y.tab.c`, `y.tab.h` |
| 2 | `flex calc.l` | Generate scanner | `lex.yy.c` |
| 3 | `gcc lex.yy.c y.tab.c -o calc -ll` | Compile & link | `calc` (executable) |
| 4 | `./calc` | Run interactively | Prompts for input |
| 5 | `Ctrl + D` | Send EOF | Exits program |

> [!WARNING]
> On some systems, the math library is required: use `gcc lex.yy.c y.tab.c -o calc -ll -lm` if you use functions like `pow()` or `sqrt()`.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The LEX/YACC pattern is the **backbone of every compiler and interpreter in industry**:
- **GCC's parser** for C/C++ uses a hand-written recursive-descent, but its early versions used YACC.
- **PHP's Zend Engine**, **Python's CPython parser**, and **Ruby's MRI** all use parser generators derived from YACC technology (LALR/GLR).
- **Database query engines** (PostgreSQL's `gram.y`) use Bison.
- **Network protocol analyzers** (Wireshark dissectors) use LEX/YACC to parse packet grammars.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION_3: Step-by-Step Code Walkthrough & Analytical Trace

### 3.1 Complete Source Code (Three Files)

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **complete, runnable, KTU-expected implementation**. Type it exactly, compile using the steps in Section 2.5, and test with expressions like `2+3*4`, `(2+3)*4`, `10/3`, `2.5*4`.

#### File 1: `calc.l` (The LEX Specification)

```c
%{
#include "y.tab.h"
#include <stdio.h>
%}

/* Recognize integers and floats */
%%
[0-9]+          { yylval.ival = atoi(yytext); return NUMBER; }
[0-9]+\.[0-9]+  { yylval.fval  = atof(yytext); return FNUM;   }
[ \t]           { /* skip whitespace */       }
\n              { return '\n';                }
\+              { return PLUS;                }
\-              { return MINUS;               }
\*              { return MUL;                 }
\/              { return DIV;                 }
\(              { return LPAREN;              }
\)              { return RPAREN;              }
.               { printf("Unknown char %c\n", *yytext); }
%%

int yywrap() { return 1; }
```

**Line-by-Line Explanation:**

- **`%{ ... %}`** — LEX embedded code block. This C code is copied verbatim to the top of `lex.yy.c`. We `#include "y.tab.h"` so that the token macros (`NUMBER`, `PLUS`, etc.) declared in the YACC file are visible here.
- **`[0-9]+`** — Matches one or more digits. `atoi(yytext)` converts the matched string `"42"` to the integer `42`, stores it in `yylval.ival`, and the `return NUMBER;` sends the token to the parser.
- **`[0-9]+\.[0-9]+`** — Matches floating-point numbers like `3.14`. We use a *separate token* `FNUM` so we can store it as a `float` in `yylval.fval`.
- **`[ \t]`** — Matches spaces/tabs but the action is empty (just skip).
- **`\n`** — Newline is special: it's the **end-of-expression marker**. We return it as character `'\n'` (a literal single character, not a token macro).
- **`\+`** — The backslash is required in LEX regular expressions for metacharacters like `+`, `*`, `(`, `)`, `?`. Without it, `+` means "one or more" (Kleene plus).
- **`yywrap()`** — Returns `1` to tell LEX "no more input files, stop." Required at the end of every LEX file.

#### File 2: `calc.y` (The YACC Grammar)

```c
%{
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
int yylex(void);
int yyerror(const char *s);
%}

/* --- Declare the union to hold semantic values --- */
%union {
    int   ival;
    float fval;
}

/* --- Token declarations (with their union field) --- */
%token <ival> NUMBER
%token <fval> FNUM
%token PLUS MINUS MUL DIV LPAREN RPAREN

/* --- Non-terminals carry float values --- */
%type <fval> E T F

/* --- Precedence: lowest first, left associative --- */
%left PLUS MINUS
%left MUL DIV
%left LPAREN
%left UMINUS

/* --- Grammar rules --- */
%%
S    : E '\n'        { printf("= %g\n", $1); }
     ;

E    : E PLUS T      { $$ = $1 + $3; }
     | E MINUS T     { $$ = $1 - $3; }
     | T             { $$ = $1;    }
     ;

T    : T MUL F       { $$ = $1 * $3; }
     | T DIV F       { $$ = $1 / $3; }
     | F             { $$ = $1;    }
     ;

F    : LPAREN E RPAREN  { $$ = $2; }
     | NUMBER           { $$ = (float)$1; }
     | FNUM             { $$ = $1; }
     | MINUS F  %prec UMINUS  { $$ = -$2; }
     ;
%%

/* --- Main driver --- */
int main(void) {
    printf("Simple Calculator (Ctrl+D to exit)\n> ");
    return yyparse();
}

int yyerror(const char *s) {
    fprintf(stderr, "Error: %s\n", s);
    return 0;
}
```

**Line-by-Line Explanation of Key Productions:**

- **`S : E '\n' { printf("= %g\n", $1); }`**
  The *start symbol* `S` accepts an expression `E` followed by a newline. When the rule is reduced, we **print the result** in `%g` format (compact float). `$1` is the float value of `E`.

- **`E : E PLUS T { $$ = $1 + $3; }`**
  Recursive rule that handles left-associative chains: `1+2+3` becomes `(1+2)+3`. The action adds the left `E` to `T` and stores the result in `$$`.

- **`E : T`**
  Base case: an `E` can be a single `T`. This is what allows the recursion in the previous rule to terminate.

- **`F : MINUS F %prec UMINUS`**
  Handles **unary minus** (e.g., `-5`, `--x`). The `%prec UMINUS` directive forces this rule to have the precedence of the dummy `UMINUS` token (declared in the precedence block *after* `LPAREN`), so unary minus binds tighter than `*` and `/`. Without this, the parser would conflict on expressions like `-3*4`.

### 3.2 Trace of a Sample Expression: `3 + 4 * 2`

Let's trace **tokenization** (LEX stage) first:

| Input | Token | yylval |
|---|---|---|
| `3` | `NUMBER` | `ival = 3` |
| ` ` | (skipped) | — |
| `+` | `PLUS` | — |
| ` ` | (skipped) | — |
| `4` | `NUMBER` | `ival = 4` |
| ` ` | (skipped) | — |
| `*` | `MUL` | — |
| ` ` | (skipped) | — |
| `2` | `NUMBER` | `ival = 2` |
| `\n` | `'\n'` | — |

Now the **parser trace** (YACC stage) using the grammar:

1. Parser sees `NUMBER 3` → shifts to stack, state expects `E`.
2. Parser sees `PLUS` → by rule `E : T`, reduce `NUMBER` to `F` ($$\$=3), then `F` to `T` ($$\$=3), then `T` to `E` ($$\$=3). Stack: `[E=3]`. Token: `PLUS`.
3. Shift `PLUS` onto stack. Stack: `[E=3, PLUS]`.
4. Parser sees `NUMBER 4` → shift. Stack: `[E=3, PLUS, NUMBER=4]`. Token: `MUL`.
5. `NUMBER` → `F` ($$\$=4), then `F` → `T` ($$\$=4). Stack: `[E=3, PLUS, T=4]`.
6. `MUL` shifts in. Stack: `[E=3, PLUS, T=4, MUL]`.
7. Parser sees `NUMBER 2` → shift. Stack: `[E=3, PLUS, T=4, MUL, NUMBER=2]`. Token: `\n`.
8. `NUMBER` → `F` ($$\$=2), then `F` → `T` ($$\$=2). Stack: `[E=3, PLUS, T=4, MUL, T=2]`.
9. Apply rule `T : T MUL F`. Action: `$$ = $1 * $3 = 4 * 2 = 8`. Stack: `[E=3, PLUS, T=8]`.
10. Apply rule `E : E PLUS T`. Action: `$$ = $1 + $3 = 3 + 8 = 11`. Stack: `[E=11]`.
11. Apply rule `S : E '\n'`. Action: `printf("= 11\n")`. ✓

**Output:** `= 11` (correct because `*` has higher precedence).

### 3.3 Error Recovery in YACC

The `yyerror` function is called by the parser when it encounters a syntax error. The default `yyparse()` then calls `yyerror`, prints "syntax error", and calls `exit(1)`. For a robust calculator that recovers after an error, you can use the **error token**:

```c
E : E PLUS T     { $$ = $1 + $3; }
  | E MINUS T    { $$ = $1 - $3; }
  | error '\n'   { yyerrok; printf("Try again.\n> "); }
  | T            { $$ = $1;    }
  ;
```

When the parser sees garbage, it discards tokens until it finds `\n`, prints an error, and continues.

### 3.4 Compilation & Execution Output

```
$ bison -d calc.y
$ flex calc.l
$ gcc lex.yy.c y.tab.c -o calc -ll
$ ./calc
Simple Calculator (Ctrl+D to exit)
> 3+4*2
= 11
> (3+4)*2
= 14
> 10/3
= 3.33333
> 2.5*4
= 10
> -3*4
= -12
> Ctrl+D
$
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION_4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Compilation & Execution Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["calc.l source file"] --> B["flex calc.l"]
    C["calc.y source file"] --> D["bison -d calc.y"]
    B --> E["lex.yy.c"]
    D --> F["y.tab.c"]
    D --> G["y.tab.h token macros"]
    G --> H["#include in calc.l"]
    E --> I["gcc compiler link"]
    F --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["calc executable binary"]
    J --> K["User input stream stdin"]
    K --> L["yylex scanner reads chars"]
    L --> M["Tokens: NUMBER PLUS MUL etc"]
    M --> N["yyparse LALR parser"]
    N --> O{"Parse tree complete?"}
    O -- "Yes, hit newline" --> P["Run semantic actions"]
    P --> Q["printf = result"]
    O -- "No, error" --> R["yyerror called"]
    R --> S["error message or recovery"]
    Q --> T["Loop back to read next line"]
    S --> T
    T --> L
```

### 4.2 Tokenization Pipeline (LEX Internal View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input character stream: 3 + 4 * 2 newline"] --> B["LEX DFA Scanner"]
    B --> C{"Match longest pattern"}
    C -- "Digit run" --> D["Token: NUMBER, value=3"]
    C -- "Plus sign" --> E["Token: PLUS"]
    C -- "Multiply sign" --> F["Token: MUL"]
    C -- "Newline char" --> G["Token: newline sentinel"]
    D --> H["yylval.ival = 3"]
    E --> I["yylval unused"]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> J["Token queue fed to yyparse"]
    I --> J
```

### 4.3 Grammar Reduction Tree for `3 + 4 * 2`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["S: start symbol"] --> N1["E newline"]
    N1 --> E1["E"]
    N1 --> NL["'\\n'"]
    E1 --> E1A["E"]
    E1 --> PLUS["PLUS"]
    E1 --> T1["T"]
    E1A --> Tprev["T"]
    Tprev --> F1["F"]
    F1 --> NUM1["NUMBER: 3"]
    T1 --> T1A["T"]
    T1 --> MUL["MUL"]
    T1 --> F2["F"]
    T1A --> Fprev["F"]
    Fprev --> NUM2["NUMBER: 4"]
    F2 --> NUM3["NUMBER: 2"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this tree bottom-up:** Start at the leaves (`NUMBER 3`, `NUMBER 4`, `NUMBER 2`). The `*` multiplies `4 * 2 = 8` first (innermost reduction). Then `+` adds `3 + 8 = 11`. This mirrors operator precedence.

### 4.4 Data Flow Between LEX and YACC (yylval Bridge)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEX["LEX File calc.l"]
        L1["Pattern: [0-9]+"]
        L2["Action: yylval.ival = atoi yytext"]
        L3["return NUMBER"]
    end
    subgraph UNION["yylval union in y.tab.h"]
        U1["ival: int field"]
        U2["fval: float field"]
    end
    subgraph YACC["YACC File calc.y"]
        Y1["%union declares ival and fval"]
        Y2["%token NUMBER type ival"]
        Y3["Grammar rule: E : E PLUS T $$ = $1 + $3"]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L2 -- "writes to" --> U1
    L3 -- "sends token to" --> Y3
    U1 -- "read by $1 $3 in action" --> Y3
    Y2 --> Y1
    Y1 --> UNION
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the role of LEX in a compiler? List any four token patterns used in a LEX specification for a simple calculator.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
LEX (Lexical Analyzer Generator) is a tool that generates a **scanner/lexer** from a specification of regular expressions. It converts a stream of input characters into a stream of **tokens**, which are the smallest meaningful units for the parser. The four token patterns for a calculator are:
1. **`[0-9]+`** → matches integers (e.g., `42`)
2. **`[0-9]+\.[0-9]+`** → matches floating-point numbers (e.g., `3.14`)
3. **`[ \t\n]`** → matches whitespace (skipped)
4. **`\+|\-|\*|\/`** → matches the four arithmetic operators

*(1 mark for defining LEX, 2 marks for the four patterns — KTU marking scheme.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the significance of the `$$` and `$n` notation in YACC semantic actions with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In YACC, semantic actions are C code blocks attached to grammar productions. The notation **`$$`** refers to the **value of the left-hand side non-terminal** (the result of the reduction), while **`$1`, `$2`, ..., `$n`** refer to the **values of the symbols on the right-hand side**, indexed from left to right.

**Example:** For the production `E : E PLUS T { $$ = $1 + $3; }`, the values flow as: `$1` is the left `E`, `$2` is the `PLUS` token (no value), `$3` is the right `T`. The action computes `$$ = $1 + $3` and assigns it to the new `E`. This is how evaluation is propagated up the parse tree.

*(1 mark for definition of $$, 1 mark for $n, 1 mark for the example.)*

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU's End Semester Evaluation (ESE) for lab courses typically includes a 14-mark descriptive question. Per the **internal choice** rule, two alternatives are provided. Pick one.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Option 1**

**(a)** Write a complete LEX specification `calc.l` for a simple calculator that recognizes integers, the four arithmetic operators, parentheses, and ignores whitespace. Explain each section of the file. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete YACC specification `calc.y` that defines a grammar for arithmetic expressions with correct precedence and associativity, supports unary minus, and prints the result. Show the compilation commands. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

```c
%{
#include "y.tab.h"
#include <stdio.h>
%}

%%
[0-9]+        { yylval.ival = atoi(yytext); return NUMBER; }
[ \t]         { /* ignore whitespace */ }
\n            { return '\n'; }
\+            { return PLUS; }
\-            { return MINUS; }
\*            { return MUL; }
\/            { return DIV; }
\(            { return LPAREN; }
\)            { return RPAREN; }
.             { return yytext[0]; }
%%

int yywrap(void) { return 1; }
```

**Explanation (distribution of 7 marks):**
- **`%{ ... %}` definitions section** (1 mark): C code copied verbatim — here we include the YACC header.
- **`%%` separator** (1 mark): Marks the start of rules.
- **Rules section** (3 marks): Each pattern-action pair; `yylval.ival = atoi(yytext)` shows how values are stored.
- **User subroutines section** (1 mark): The `yywrap()` function.
- **C code for matching and returning tokens** (1 mark).

**(b) [7 marks]**

```c
%{
#include <stdio.h>
int yylex(void);
int yyerror(const char *s);
%}

%union { int ival; float fval; }
%token <ival> NUMBER
%token <fval> FNUM
%token PLUS MINUS MUL DIV LPAREN RPAREN
%type <fval> E T F
%left PLUS MINUS
%left MUL DIV
%left UMINUS

%%
S    : E '\n'           { printf("= %g\n", $1); }
     ;
E    : E PLUS T         { $$ = $1 + $3; }
     | E MINUS T        { $$ = $1 - $3; }
     | T                { $$ = $1;    }
     ;
T    : T MUL F          { $$ = $1 * $3; }
     | T DIV F          { $$ = $1 / $3; }
     | F                { $$ = $1;    }
     ;
F    : LPAREN E RPAREN  { $$ = $2; }
     | NUMBER           { $$ = (float)$1; }
     | MINUS F %prec UMINUS { $$ = -$2; }
     ;
%%

int main(void) { return yyparse(); }
int yyerror(const char *s) { fprintf(stderr, "Error: %s\n", s); return 0; }
```

**Compilation (1 mark for commands):**
```bash
bison -d calc.y
flex calc.l
gcc lex.yy.c y.tab.c -o calc -ll
./calc
```

**Explanation of marks:**
- `%union`, `%token`, `%type` declarations (2 marks)
- Precedence directives with `%left` and `%prec UMINUS` (2 marks)
- Grammar productions with semantic actions and `$$`/`$n` usage (2 marks)
- Compilation commands (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Option 2**

**(a)** Explain the **tokenization** and **parsing** stages of a LEX/YACC calculator. How does the value of a `NUMBER` token get from `calc.l` to the semantic action in `calc.y`? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the grammar:
```
E → E + T | T
T → T * F | F
F → ( E ) | number
```
Trace the parse for the input `5 - 2 - 1` and show that left-associativity gives the result `2` (not `4`). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Tokenization Stage (3 marks):**
- The **LEX scanner** (`yylex()`) reads raw characters from input.
- It applies the **longest match** rule to determine the next token.
- For each matched pattern, the corresponding **C action** is executed. This action sets `yylval` and returns a token code (e.g., `NUMBER`, `PLUS`).

**Parsing Stage (2 marks):**
- The **YACC parser** (`yyparse()`) consumes tokens one at a time.
- It uses an **LALR(1) parse table** to decide whether to **shift** (push token onto stack) or **reduce** (apply a grammar rule and run its action).
- When the start symbol is fully reduced, parsing is complete.

**Value Transfer Mechanism (2 marks):**
- The LEX action sets `yylval.ival = atoi(yytext)`. The `yylval` variable is of type `%union`, defined in `calc.y`.
- When `yylex()` returns the token, `yylval` contains the value. YACC stores it in the parse stack.
- When the parser reduces by a rule, the values from the stack become accessible as `$1`, `$2`, etc., in the action.

**(b) [7 marks]**

Trace for `5 - 2 - 1` (left-associative, meaning `(5 - 2) - 1`):

| Step | Stack | Remaining Input | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `$` | `5 - 2 - 1` | Shift `5` |
| 2 | `$ 5` | `- 2 - 1` | Reduce `5 → F` ($$=5) |
| 3 | `$ F=5` | `- 2 - 1` | Reduce `F → T` ($$=5) |
| 4 | `$ T=5` | `- 2 - 1` | Reduce `T → E` ($$=5) |
| 5 | `$ E=5` | `- 2 - 1` | Shift `-` |
| 6 | `$ E=5 -` | `2 - 1` | Shift `2` |
| 7 | `$ E=5 - 2` | `- 1` | Reduce `2 → F` ($$=2) |
| 8 | `$ E=5 - F=2` | `- 1` | Reduce `F → T` ($$=2) |
| 9 | `$ E=5 - T=2` | `- 1` | **Reduce `E → E - T`** ($$=5-2=3) |
| 10 | `$ E=3` | `- 1` | Shift `-` |
| 11 | `$ E=3 -` | `1` | Shift `1` |
| 12 | `$ E=3 - 1` | (end) | Reduce `1 → F` ($$=1) |
| 13 | `$ E=3 - F=1` | (end) | Reduce `F → T` ($$=1) |
| 14 | `$ E=3 - T=1` | (end) | **Reduce `E → E - T`** ($$=3-1=**2**) |

**Final result: 2**, confirming left-associativity. If it were right-associative, the parse would give `5 - (2 - 1) = 4`, which is incorrect for subtraction.

*(3 marks for the table, 2 marks for the calculation logic, 2 marks for the final answer with explanation.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `-d` flag in Bison:** `bison -d calc.y` is what generates `y.tab.h`. Without `-d`, the `calc.l` file's `#include "y.tab.h"` will fail with *"file not found"*. **−2 marks** if missed.
> 2. **Missing backslash escape in LEX patterns:** Writing `+` instead of `\+` makes LEX interpret it as "one or more" (Kleene plus), so `+` will match `a++b` wrong. Always use `\`, `\*`, `\(`, `\)`, `\.`.
> 3. **Wrong precedence order in `%left`:** Tokens declared *later* have *higher* precedence. Writing `%left MUL DIV` *before* `%left PLUS MINUS` will break operator precedence — students commonly swap this. **−2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting `yywrap()`:** Without it, LEX will look for a non-existent `yywrap` in some libraries and fail to link. **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing `$$` with `$0`:** There is **no `$0`**. The left-hand side is always `$$`, and right-hand side symbols are `$1, $2, ...`. A student who writes `$0 + $2` will get 0.
> 6. **Forgetting to type-cast in semantic actions:** If `$1` is `int` (from `NUMBER`) and you assign it to a `float` non-terminal, you must cast: `$$ = (float)$1;` to avoid compiler warnings.
> 7. **Wrong `printf` format:** Use `%g` or `%f` for floats, not `%d` (which is for integers and will print garbage).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **LEX** generates a **scanner** (`yylex`); **YACC** generates a **parser** (`yyparse`).
- ✅ Three files are typical: `calc.l`, `calc.y`, and a `main()` driver. The driver can also be inside `calc.y` after the second `%%`.
- ✅ **LEX pattern** `[0-9]+` matches integers; `\.` is the escaped period for decimals.
- ✅ **YACC grammar** uses **layered non-terminals** (`E` for +/-, `T` for */, `F` for atoms) to enforce precedence implicitly.
- ✅ **`%union`** holds the type of `yylval`. **`%token <type>`** assigns a token a union field. **`%type <type>`** assigns a non-terminal a union field.
- ✅ **`%left PLUS MINUS`** and **`%left MUL DIV`** declare left-associativity and order of precedence (later = higher).
- ✅ **`$$` is the LHS value; `$n` is the nth symbol's value** in a production.
- ✅ **`%prec UMINUS`** is used to make unary minus bind tighter than `*` and `/`.
- ✅ Compilation pipeline: `bison -d calc.y` → `flex calc.l` → `gcc lex.yy.c y.tab.c -o calc -ll` → `./calc`.
- ✅ The `-d` flag in Bison **is mandatory** to generate the token header file.
- ✅ **Longest match** rule in LEX: if multiple patterns match, the longest wins; if tied, the one declared *first* wins.
- ✅ **Shift-reduce conflicts** are resolved by precedence/associativity directives; **reduce-reduce conflicts** indicate genuine grammar ambiguity.
- ✅ **Error recovery** uses the special `error` token in YACC productions.
- ✅ The **start symbol** in YACC is the non-terminal on the left of the *first* rule in the rules section.
- ✅ `yyerror()` is mandatory; the user must define it (else linker error).
- ✅ `yywrap()` returning 1 means "no more input files"; returning 0 would tell LEX to open `yyin` again.
- ✅ For the **calculator experiment**, the canonical grammar gives `*`/`/` higher precedence than `+`/`-` and enforces **left-associativity** for both.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convert the BNF rules into YACC form and write code to generate abstract syntax tree
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCSL607 - SYSTEMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Convert the BNF rules into YACC form and write code to generate abstract syntax tree ## 1.
