Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Backus-Naur Form (BNF)** is a formal mathematical notation for describing the **syntax** of context-free grammars. It was originally developed by **John Backus** and **Peter Naur** in the late 1950s to specify the syntax of **ALGOL 60**. A BNF rule consists of a *non-terminal* on the left side and a *derivation* (sequence of terminals and non-terminals) on the right side, separated by the symbol `::=`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **YACC (Yet Another Compiler Compiler)** is a *parser generator* tool developed by **Stephen C. Johnson** at Bell Labs in 1973. It takes a grammar specification written in a BNF-like notation (`.y` file) and automatically generates the **`y.tab.c`** C source file — a LALR(1) parser that detects syntactic structure in the input token stream produced by **LEX**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** is a condensed, hierarchical **tree representation** of the source program's syntactic structure. Unlike a parse tree, an AST **eliminates redundant syntactic information** (parentheses, punctuation, derivation rules) and represents each construct as a *node* containing only the essential semantic data (operator and operands). ### 1.2 The Trinity of Compilation (The Three Pillars) | Tool | Role | Input | Output | |---|---|---|---| | **LEX** | Lexical Analyzer (Tokenizer) | Source code (`.l`) | Token stream | | **YACC** | Syntax Analyzer (Parser) | Grammar (`.y`) | Parse tree / AST | | **GCC** | Code Generator | AST / Intermediate code | Executable binary | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen" > [!TIP] > **Think of BNF as a Recipe Book, YACC as the Head Chef, and AST as the Plated Dish Diagram.** > > - The **BNF recipe** says: *"A Burger must contain a bun, a patty, and a topping."* > - The **YACC chef** reads the order ticket (token stream) and *verifies* it follows the recipe structure, then **builds a plate** (AST) that records *what was used* (operators/operands) and *how it was combined* (tree hierarchy). > - The final **AST plate** is what the next kitchen station (semantic analyzer / code generator) uses to cook the actual meal (machine code). ### 1.4 Why AST Instead of Parse Tree? A parse tree contains every derivation step (including non-terminals and intermediate productions), making it **verbose and hard to traverse**. An AST abstracts away this noise, leaving only the **essential computational structure** — making it ideal for subsequent compilation phases. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a BNF Rule A BNF rule is written as: $$\text{Non-Terminal} \; ::= \; \alpha_1 \mid \alpha_2 \mid \dots \mid \alpha_n$$ where: - **Non-Terminal** (left side) = a syntactic variable (e.g., ``) - $\alpha_i$ (right side) = a sequence of **terminals** (tokens) and **non-terminals** - The symbol `::=` means "is defined as" - The symbol $\mid$ separates alternative productions (logical OR) ### 2.2 Anatomy of a YACC Specification File (`.y`) A YACC file has **three distinct sections**, separated by the special token `%%`: ``` ───────────────────────────────────── SECTION 1: DECLARATIONS (C declarations, #include, #define, token IDs, %left/%right precedence) ───────────────────────────────────── %% SECTION 2: GRAMMAR RULES (Translated BNF rules + semantic actions) ───────────────────────────────────── %% SECTION 3: AUXILIARY C ROUTINES (main(), yyerror(), AST helper functions) ───────────────────────────────────── ``` ### 2.3 The Semantic Action Stack — `$$`, `$1`, `$2`, … > [!IMPORTANT] > When YACC reduces a production, the semantic action code executes. Inside this code: > - **`$$`** refers to the **value of the LHS (left-hand side) non-terminal** (the result of the rule). > - **`$1`, `$2`, `$3`, …** refer to the values of the **1st, 2nd, 3rd … RHS symbols** respectively. > > This mechanism is how YACC **builds the AST**: each rule's action constructs a *new node* linking the children's subtrees. ### 2.4 The BNF → YACC Translation Map | BNF Element | YACC Equivalent | Notes | |---|---|---| | `::=` | `:` | Definition separator | | `\|` (alternation) | New line ending in `;` | Each alternative on its own line | | Terminal in quotes `"x"` | Token name `x` (or `'x'`) | Single character terminals use quotes | | Non-terminal `` | `expr` | No angle brackets, lowercase convention | | Epsilon (empty) $\varepsilon$ | (empty RHS) | Just `:` followed by `;` | ### 2.5 The KTU Formula Sheet — AST Node Operations > [!NOTE] > All AST node operations follow the **make-node / make-leaf** pattern. Below is the cheat-sheet table for an arithmetic expression AST. | Operation | C Code Snippet | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Create leaf node** | `node = malloc(sizeof(Node)); node->type = 'i'; node->value = val;` | Make a node holding an integer operand | | **Create operator node** | `node = malloc(sizeof(Node)); node->type = op; node->left = $1; node->right = $3;` | Make a node with two children | | **Return new node** | `$$ = node;` | Assign built subtree to LHS variable | | **Link subtrees** | `node->left = $1; node->right = $3;` | Build parent-child relationships | ### 2.6 Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > AST generation via YACC is the **backbone of every modern compiler**: GCC, Clang/LLVM, javac, ghc (Haskell), rustc, and even TypeScript's tsc all use this same LALR/GLR parsing + AST paradigm. The techniques you write in a 50-line `.y` file today are **industry-scale** concepts. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Conversion, Code & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Sample BNF Grammar (Reference: Arithmetic Expressions) Consider the classic BNF for arithmetic expressions with `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, parentheses, and integer operands: ``` Goal ::= Expr Expr ::= Expr '+' Term | Expr '-' Term | Term Term ::= Term '*' Factor | Term '/' Factor | Factor Factor ::= '(' Expr ')' | ID ``` ### 3.2 Step-by-Step BNF → YACC Conversion **Step 1 — Strip angle brackets and replace `::=` with `:`:** ``` Goal : Expr Expr : Expr '+' Term | Expr '-' Term | Term Term : Term '*' Factor | Term '/' Factor | Factor Factor : '(' Expr ')' | ID ``` **Step 2 — Each alternative on a separate line, terminated with `;`:** ``` Goal : Expr ; Expr : Expr '+' Term | Expr '-' Term | Term ; Term : Term '*' Factor | Term '/' Factor | Factor ; Factor : '(' Expr ')' | ID ; ``` **Step 3 — Add semantic actions to build the AST.** Each action uses `make_node()` to allocate a node and `$$` to return it. ### 3.3 The Complete YACC Program (`.y` file) > [!IMPORTANT] > Save this as **`ast.y`** and compile with: `yacc -d ast.y && gcc y.tab.c -o ast` ```c /*============================================================== ast.y — YACC specification with AST generation Course: PCCSL607 (Systems Lab) | KTU 2024 Scheme Topic : BNF → YACC + Abstract Syntax Tree construction Grammar: Arithmetic expressions ==============================================================*/ %{ #include #include #include /* ---- AST Node Definition ---- */ typedef struct ASTNode { int type; /* 'n' = number, 'o' = operator */ int op; /* operator character (+,-,*,/) */ int value; /* for number nodes */ struct ASTNode *left; struct ASTNode *right; } ASTNode; /* ---- AST helper function prototypes ---- */ ASTNode* make_leaf(int val); ASTNode* make_node(int op, ASTNode* l, ASTNode* r); void print_ast(ASTNode* root, int level); int yylex(void); void yyerror(const char *s); %} /* ---- Token declarations ---- */ %token ID /* ---- Operator precedence (low to high) ---- */ %left '+' '-' %left '*' '/' %right UMINUS /* ---- Grammar rules with AST-building actions ---- */ %% Goal : Expr { printf("\nAbstract Syntax Tree (indented dump):\n"); print_ast($1, 0); } ; Expr : Expr '+' Term { $$ = make_node('+', $1, $3); } | Expr '-' Term { $$ = make_node('-', $1, $3); } | Term { $$ = $1; } ; Term : Term '*' Factor { $$ = make_node('*', $1, $3); } | Term '/' Factor { $$ = make_node('/', $1, $3); } | Factor { $$ = $1; } ; Factor : '(' Expr ')' { $$ = $2; } | ID { $$ = make_leaf($1); } ; %% /* ---- AST helper: create leaf node (number/identifier) ---- */ ASTNode* make_leaf(int val) { ASTNode* n = (ASTNode*)malloc(sizeof(ASTNode)); n->type = 'n'; n->value = val; n->left = NULL; n->right = NULL; return n; } /* ---- AST helper: create internal operator node ---- */ ASTNode* make_node(int op, ASTNode* l, ASTNode* r) { ASTNode* n = (ASTNode*)malloc(sizeof(ASTNode)); n->type = 'o'; n->op = op; n->left = l; n->right = r; return n; } /* ---- AST helper: pretty-print the tree sideways ---- */ void print_ast(ASTNode* root, int level) { if (root == NULL) return; print_ast(root->right, level + 1); for (int i = 0; i < level; i++) printf(" "); if (root->type == 'o') printf("[op: %c]\n", root->op); else printf("[num: %d]\n", root->value); print_ast(root->left, level + 1); } /* ---- Error reporter ---- */ void yyerror(const char *s) { fprintf(stderr, "Parse error: %s\n", s); } /* ---- Main: ties LEX tokens to YACC parser ---- */ int main(void) { printf("Enter an expression (e.g., 3+5*2):\n> "); return yyparse(); } ``` ### 3.4 Required Companion LEX File (`ast.l`) > [!NOTE] > YACC needs a LEX tokenizer to feed it tokens. Save as `ast.l`. ```c %{ #include "y.tab.h" #include %} %% [0-9]+ { yylval = atoi(yytext); return ID; } [ \t\n] { /* skip whitespace */ } . { return yytext[0]; } %% ``` ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping BNF to YACC Actions | BNF Production | YACC Rule | Semantic Action | AST Effect | |---|---|---|---| | `Expr → Expr + Term` | `Expr : Expr '+' Term` | `$$ = make_node('+', $1, $3);` | Build `+` node, children = left-Expr and right-Term | | `Expr → Term` | `Expr : Term` | `$$ = $1;` | Propagate subtree upward unchanged | | `Term → Term * Factor` | `Term : Term '*' Factor` | `$$ = make_node('*', $1, $3);` | Build `*` node | | `Factor → ( Expr )` | `Factor : '(' Expr ')'` | `$$ = $2;` | Discard parens, keep inner subtree | | `Factor → ID` | `Factor : ID` | `$$ = make_leaf($1);` | Create leaf node holding the integer | ### 3.6 Worked Example — Parsing `3 + 5 * 2` **Step-by-step reduction trace** (LALR parser behavior): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Input token stream:} \quad & \text{ID(3) `+' ID(5) `*' ID(2)} \\[4pt] \text{Reduce 1:} \quad & \text{Factor} \to \text{ID} \quad\Rightarrow\; \text{Factor} = \text{leaf}(3) \\[4pt] \text{Reduce 2:} \quad & \text{Term} \to \text{Factor} \quad\Rightarrow\; \text{Term} = \text{leaf}(3) \\[4pt] \text{Reduce 3:} \quad & \text{Expr} \to \text{Term} \quad\Rightarrow\; \text{Expr} = \text{leaf}(3) \\[4pt] \text{Read `+', shift} \\[4pt] \text{Reduce 4:} \quad & \text{Factor} \to \text{ID} \quad\Rightarrow\; \text{Factor} = \text{leaf}(5) \\[4pt] \text{Reduce 5:} \quad & \text{Term} \to \text{Factor} \quad\Rightarrow\; \text{Term} = \text{leaf}(5) \\[4pt] \text{Read `*', shift; Reduce 6:} \quad & \text{Factor} \to \text{ID} \quad\Rightarrow\; \text{leaf}(2) \\[4pt] \text{Reduce 7:} \quad & \text{Term} \to \text{Term} \ast \text{Factor} \quad\Rightarrow\; \text{Term} = \text{node}(`*`, \text{leaf}(5), \text{leaf}(2)) \\[4pt] \text{Reduce 8:} \quad & \text{Expr} \to \text{Expr} + \text{Term} \quad\Rightarrow\; \text{Expr} = \text{node}(`+`, \text{leaf}(3), \text{node}(`*`, \text{leaf}(5), \text{leaf}(2))) \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > Notice the AST correctly encodes **operator precedence**: `*` is *deeper* in the tree, meaning it is evaluated *first*. This is the core strength of AST — the tree shape **itself** represents evaluation order. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Compiler Pipeline — Where YACC & AST Fit ```mermaid flowchart LR src["Source Code
(input.txt)"] lex["LEX
.l file"] tokens["Token Stream
ID '+' ID '*' ID"] yacc["YACC
.y file"] ast["Abstract
Syntax Tree"] sem["Semantic
Analyzer"] ir["Intermediate
Representation"] codegen["Code
Generator"] exe["Executable
Binary"] src -- "characters" --> lex lex -- "yylex()" --> tokens tokens -- "yyparse()" --> yacc yacc -- "semantic actions" --> ast ast --> sem sem --> ir ir --> codegen exe classDef stage fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,color:#000 classDef data fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#047857,color:#000 class src,tokens,ast,ir,exe data class lex,yacc,sem,codegen stage ``` ### 4.2 YACC File Structure — Three Sections ```mermaid flowchart TB y["ast.y
YACC Specification File"] s1["SECTION 1
Declarations
(%{ ... %}
%token
%left / %right)"] s2["SECTION 2
Grammar Rules
(BNF + semantic actions)
separated by %%"] s3["SECTION 3
Auxiliary Routines
(main, yyerror,
AST helpers)"] out["y.tab.c + y.tab.h
(generated C parser)"] y --> s1 s1 --> s2 s2 --> s3 s3 --> out classDef sec fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 classDef out fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 class s1,s2,s3 sec class y,out out ``` ### 4.3 AST Built for Expression `3 + 5 * 2` ```mermaid flowchart TB root["[op: +]"] left1["[num: 3]"] right1["[op: *]"] left2["[num: 5]"] right2["[num: 2]"] root --> left1 root --> right1 right1 --> left2 right1 --> right2 classDef op fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000 classDef num fill:#86efac,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 class root,right1 op class left1,left2,right2 num ``` ### 4.4 Parse Tree vs Abstract Syntax Tree — Key Difference ```mermaid flowchart TB pt["Parse Tree for 3+5
(verbose, includes all non-terminals)"] pt1["Goal"] pt2["Expr"] pt3a["Expr"] pt3b["+"] pt3c["Term"] pt4a["Term → Factor → ID(3)"] pt4b["ID(5)"] pt --> pt1 --> pt2 pt2 --> pt3a & pt3b & pt3c pt3a --> pt4a pt3c --> pt4b at["AST for 3+5
(compact, no derivation noise)"] at1["[op: +]"] at2["[num: 3]"] at3["[num: 5]"] at --> at1 at1 --> at2 & at3 classDef pt fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000 classDef at fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c,color:#000 class pt,pt1,pt2,pt3a,pt3b,pt3c,pt4a,pt4b pt class at,at1,at2,at3 at ``` ### 4.5 AST Node Memory Layout (C struct) ```mermaid flowchart LR n["ASTNode struct"] f1["type : int
( 'n' or 'o' )"] f2["op / value : int
( operator char OR number )"] f3["left : ASTNode*"] f4["right : ASTNode*"] n --- f1 n --- f2 n --- f3 n --- f4 classDef box fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,color:#000 class n,f1,f2,f3,f4 box ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** --- #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]** **Differentiate between a parse tree and an abstract syntax tree. Why is AST preferred in modern compilers?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Parse Tree | Abstract Syntax Tree | |---|---|---| | **Content** | Contains *every* non-terminal and production rule | Contains only *essential* operators and operands | | **Size** | Large, verbose | Compact, minimal | | **Construction** | Generated implicitly by parser | Built explicitly via semantic actions in YACC | | **Use** | Parser debugging | Subsequent compilation phases | **Why AST is preferred (1 mark):** The AST eliminates redundant syntactic symbols (parentheses, commas, intermediate non-terminals), making it **compact**, **easier to traverse**, and **directly usable** for semantic analysis, optimization, and code generation. It encodes **operator precedence** in its structure — deeper nodes = higher precedence = evaluated first. *(3 marks: 1 for table, 1 for AST advantages, 1 for precedence insight)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]** **Explain the role of `$$`, `$1`, `$2` … `$n` in a YACC semantic action. How are they used to construct an AST node?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **`$$`** is a special YACC variable that holds the **value of the LHS non-terminal** of the current production. It is the value *returned upward* to the parent rule. - **`$1`, `$2`, …, `$n`** hold the values of the **1st, 2nd, …, nth symbol on the RHS** of the current production — they are the *incoming* values from child rules. - **AST construction (1 mark):** Inside a rule like `Expr : Expr '+' Term`, the action `$$ = make_node('+', $1, $3);` creates a new operator node with `$1` (left subtree) and `$3` (right subtree) as children, then assigns the new node to `$$`. This new node is then passed upward as `$1` or `$2` in the parent rule. --- ### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)** *As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, students answer ONE of TWO alternatives.* --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]** **(a)** *Convert the following BNF grammar into YACC form and explain each step. (7 marks)* ``` S → A B A → a A | ε B → b B c | ε ``` **(b)** *For the grammar in (a), write the complete YACC `.y` file (with declarations, rules, and auxiliary routines) that constructs an **Abstract Syntax Tree** for the input. (7 marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **(a) BNF → YACC Conversion — Step by Step (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Identify non-terminals and terminals (1 mark):** - Non-terminals: `S`, `A`, `B` - Terminals: `a`, `b`, `c` **Step 2 — Replace `::=` with `:` and remove angle brackets (1 mark):** ``` S : A B A : a A | B : b B c | ``` **Step 3 — Each alternative on a separate line ending in `;` (1 mark):** ``` S : A B ; A : a A | | /* empty */ ; B : b B c | | /* empty */ ; ``` **Step 4 — Final YACC rule block (1 mark):** ``` S : A B ; A : 'a' A | ; B : 'b' B 'c' | ; ``` **Step 5 — Identify the language generated (1 mark):** The grammar generates strings of the form $a^i b^j c^j$ where $i, j \geq 0$ (e.g., `ε`, `abc`, `abbc`, `aabc`, `aabbcc`). **Step 6 — Add `%token` declarations for terminals (1 mark):** In declarations section: `%token A_LETTER B_LETTER C_LETTER` (or directly use `'a' 'b' 'c'` as single-character tokens). **Step 7 — Add semantic actions to build AST (1 mark):** Discussed in part (b). --- **(b) Complete YACC `.y` file with AST construction (7 marks):** ```c %{ #include #include typedef struct ASTNode { char label; /* 'a', 'b', 'c', or 'S' */ struct ASTNode *left; struct ASTNode *right; } ASTNode; ASTNode* make_node(char lbl, ASTNode* l, ASTNode* r); void print_ast(ASTNode* r, int depth); int yylex(void); void yyerror(const char *s); %} %token A_TOK B_TOK C_TOK %% S : A B { $$ = make_node('S', $1, $2); printf("AST for accepted string:\n"); print_ast($$, 0); } ; A : A_TOK A { $$ = make_node('a', $1, $2); } | /* epsilon */ { $$ = NULL; } ; B : B_TOK B C_TOK { $$ = make_node('b', $1, $2); /* $3 is C, embed in right's right or discard */ $$->right = make_node('c', NULL, NULL); } | /* epsilon */ { $$ = NULL; } ; %% ASTNode* make_node(char lbl, ASTNode* l, ASTNode* r) { ASTNode* n = (ASTNode*)malloc(sizeof(ASTNode)); n->label = lbl; n->left = l; n->right = r; return n; } void print_ast(ASTNode* r, int depth) { if (!r) return; print_ast(r->right, depth + 1); for (int i = 0; i < depth; i++) printf(" "); printf("[%c]\n", r->label); print_ast(r->left, depth + 1); } void yyerror(const char *s) { fprintf(stderr, "Error: %s\n", s); } int main(void) { printf("Enter string of a's followed by b's then c's:\n> "); return yyparse(); } ``` **Companion LEX file (`ast2.l`):** ```c %{ #include "y.tab.h" %} %% a { return A_TOK; } b { return B_TOK; } c { return C_TOK; } \n { return '\n'; } [ \t] ; /* skip whitespace */ . { return yytext[0]; } %% ``` **Compilation & Execution (1 mark within 7):** ```bash yacc -d ast2.y lex ast2.l gcc y.tab.c lex.yy.c -o ast2 ./ast2 # then type: abc ``` **Sample AST produced for input `abc` (1 mark):** ```mermaid flowchart TB s["[S]"] a["[a]"] b["[b]"] c["[c]"] s --> a s --> b b --> c classDef n fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,color:#000 class s,a,b,c n ``` --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]** **(a)** *Given the BNF grammar below, convert it into YACC specification format showing **all conversion steps**. (7 marks)* ``` E → E + T | E - T | T T → T * F | T / F | F F → ( E ) | number ``` **(b)** *Write a complete YACC program (with auxiliary C functions) to **build and display an Abstract Syntax Tree** for the grammar in part (a). Show the AST that would be generated for the input `10 - 4 * 2`. (7 marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **(a) BNF → YACC Step-by-Step Conversion (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Rewrite removing `< >` and `::=` (1 mark):** ``` E → E + T | E - T | T T → T * F | T / F | F F → ( E ) | number ``` becomes: ``` E : E + T | E - T | T T : T * F | T / F | F F : ( E ) | number ``` **Step 2 — Each alternative on its own line with `;` (2 marks):** ``` E : E '+' T | E '-' T | T ; T : T '*' F | T '/' F | F ; F : '(' E ')' | number ; ``` **Step 3 — Add `%token` declaration (1 mark):** In declarations: `%token NUMBER` **Step 4 — Add operator precedence to resolve shift-reduce conflicts (1 mark):** ``` %left '+' '-' %left '*' '/' ``` (Lower precedence listed first; `*` and `/` bind tighter than `+` and `-`.) **Step 5 — Identify START symbol (1 mark):** `E` is the start symbol (declared via `%start E` or implicitly the LHS of the first rule). **Step 6 — Add semantic actions to build AST (1 mark):** Discussed in part (b). --- **(b) Complete YACC Program with AST (7 marks):** ```c %{ #include #include typedef struct Node { int nodetype; /* 0 = number, 1 = operator */ char op; /* operator char for op nodes */ int val; /* value for number nodes */ struct Node *left; struct Node *right; } Node; Node* mk_num(int v); Node* mk_op(char op, Node* l, Node* r); void show(Node* r, int lvl); int yylex(void); void yyerror(const char *s); %} %token NUMBER %left '+' '-' %left '*' '/' %% E : E '+' T { $$ = mk_op('+', $1, $3); } | E '-' T { $$ = mk_op('-', $1, $3); } | T { $$ = $1; } ; T : T '*' F { $$ = mk_op('*', $1, $3); } | T '/' F { $$ = mk_op('/', $1, $3); } | F { $$ = $1; } ; F : '(' E ')' { $$ = $2; } | NUMBER { $$ = mk_num($1); } ; %% Node* mk_num(int v) { Node* n = (Node*)malloc(sizeof(Node)); n->nodetype = 0; n->val = v; n->left = n->right = NULL; return n; } Node* mk_op(char op, Node* l, Node* r) { Node* n = (Node*)malloc(sizeof(Node)); n->nodetype = 1; n->op = op; n->left = l; n->right = r; return n; } void show(Node* r, int lvl) { if (!r) return; show(r->right, lvl+1); for (int i=0;inodetype ? "[op %c]\n" : "[num %d]\n", r->nodetype ? r->op : r->val); show(r->left, lvl+1); } void yyerror(const char *s) { fprintf(stderr, "Error: %s\n", s); } int main(void) { printf("Enter expression: "); return yyparse(); } ``` **AST for input `10 - 4 * 2` (2 marks within the 7):** ```mermaid flowchart TB root["[op: -]"] l["[num: 10]"] r["[op: *]"] l2["[num: 4]"] r2["[num: 2]"] root --> l root --> r r --> l2 r --> r2 classDef op fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000 classDef num fill:#86efac,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 class root,r op class l,l2,r2 num ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **The tree shape encodes evaluation order:** The `*` node is *deeper* (right child of `-`), so it is evaluated first → `4 * 2 = 8`, then `10 - 8 = 2`. This correctly implements **left-to-right operator precedence** without needing parentheses. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgot `%token` declaration** → YACC throws *syntax error on rule* — lose 1 mark. > 2. **Mixed up `$$` and `$1`** → AST is built upside-down; tree traversal prints wrong order — lose 2 marks. > 3. **Did not handle epsilon (`| ; |`)** → All-empty productions get rejected by parser — lose 1–2 marks. > 4. **Forgot `#include ` for `malloc`** → Segmentation fault at runtime — lose 1 mark (no execution marks). > 5. **Did not write the `yyerror()` and `main()` functions** → Linking fails — lose 1 mark. > 6. **Showed parse tree instead of AST** in the diagram → lose 1 mark; the question explicitly asks for **AST**. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **BNF** uses `::=`, `< >`, `|`; **YACC** uses `:`, no brackets, `;` at line end. - ✅ A YACC file has **three sections**: Declarations → `%%` → Rules → `%%` → Auxiliary C code. - ✅ `$$` = value of LHS; `$N` = value of the Nth RHS symbol. - ✅ AST node = `malloc()` + fill `type/op/value/left/right` + return. - ✅ **Operator precedence in YACC** = order of `%left`/`%right` (lower precedence declared *first*). - ✅ **Epsilon productions** are written as a bare `|` on a line by itself. - ✅ **Single-character terminals** use `'a'`, `'+'`, etc.; multi-character tokens need `%token` declarations. - ✅ Compile: `yacc -d file.y && gcc y.tab.c -o file` (or with LEX: `lex file.l && gcc lex.yy.c y.tab.c -o file`). - ✅ **AST** ≠ **Parse Tree**: AST is compact (no non-terminals, no parens); parse tree is verbose. - ✅ **Tree depth encodes precedence**: deeper operators evaluated first. ---

topics.title: Write a program to find First and Follow of any given grammar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL607 - SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a program to find First and Follow of any given grammar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Compiler Design, **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** are two fundamental helper functions used during the construction of **LL(1) predictive parsing tables**. Together, they allow the parser to make a deterministic, single-token look-ahead decision when expanding a non-terminal on top of the parse stack.

**Definition 1 — FIRST(α):**
For any string of grammar symbols $\alpha$ (where $\alpha \in (V \cup \Sigma)^*$), $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ is the set of terminal symbols that can begin any string derivable from $\alpha$. If $\alpha$ can derive $\epsilon$ (epsilon), then $\epsilon$ is also included in $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$.

Formally,
$$\text{FIRST}(\alpha) = \Big\{ a \in \Sigma \;\Big|\; \alpha \Rightarrow^* a\beta,\; a\beta \in (V \cup \Sigma)^* \Big\} \cup \{ \epsilon \mid \alpha \Rightarrow^* \epsilon \}$$

**Definition 2 — FOLLOW(A):**
For a non-terminal $A \in V$ (where $V$ is the set of non-terminals and $\Sigma$ is the set of terminals), $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ is the set of terminal symbols that can immediately follow $A$ in any sentential form derivable from the start symbol $S$. The end-marker **$** (representing input end) is included in $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$.

Formally,
$$\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \Big\{ a \in \Sigma \;\Big|\; S \Rightarrow^* \alpha A a \beta,\; a \in \Sigma \Big\} \cup \{ \$ \mid S \Rightarrow^* \alpha A \}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Distinction**
> - **FIRST** answers: *"What terminal can *appear first* in the expansion of this symbol?"*
> - **FOLLOW** answers: *"What terminal can *come after* this non-terminal in some valid derivation?"*
> - FIRST is computed for **both terminals and non-terminals**, but FOLLOW is defined **only for non-terminals**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card" Intuition

Imagine you are teaching a robot to cook recipes. Each non-terminal is a *recipe step* (e.g., `Sauce`, `Topping`) and each terminal is a *physical ingredient* (e.g., `tomato`, `cheese`).

- **FIRST(step)** = *"What is the very first ingredient you will touch when starting this step?"* For example, `FIRST(Sauce)` might contain `tomato` or `cream` because the sauce can start with either.
- **FOLLOW(step)** = *"What ingredient is most likely to come after this step is done?"* For example, `FOLLOW(Sauce)` might contain `cheese` because the next layer after the sauce is usually cheese.

When the robot is mid-recipe and holds a step in its hand, FIRST tells it *what to grab first*, and FOLLOW tells it *what to expect next*. Together they form the parsing table — the robot's ultimate "what do I do now?" cheat sheet.

### 1.3 Role in Compiler Architecture

```
Source Code  →  Lexical Analyzer  →  Tokens  →  Syntax Analyzer (Parser)
                                                       │
                                                       ▼
                                              FIRST & FOLLOW Sets
                                                       │
                                                       ▼
                                              LL(1) Parsing Table
                                                       │
                                                       ▼
                                                  Parse Tree / AST
```

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU's **PCCSL607 Systems Lab**, the experiment is implemented programmatically in **C / C++ / Python**. You will read productions of the form `E->E+T|T` and print the FIRST/FOLLOW sets. Always hardcode the start symbol so that `$` is correctly added to FOLLOW(start).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Rules for FIRST Sets

**Rule F1 — Terminal as first symbol:**
If $X$ is a terminal, then $\text{FIRST}(X) = \{ X \}$.

**Rule F2 — Non-terminal with epsilon-production:**
If $X \rightarrow \epsilon$ is a production, then add $\epsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.

**Rule F3 — Non-terminal with terminal RHS start:**
If $X \rightarrow a\alpha$ where $a$ is a terminal, then add $a$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.

**Rule F4 — Non-terminal with non-terminal RHS start (chained):**
If $X \rightarrow Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_k$ is a production:
- Add $\text{FIRST}(Y_1) \setminus \{\epsilon\}$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.
- If $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(Y_1)$, also add $\text{FIRST}(Y_2) \setminus \{\epsilon\}$.
- Continue similarly: if $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(Y_1) \cap \dots \cap \text{FIRST}(Y_{i-1})$, then add $\text{FIRST}(Y_i) \setminus \{\epsilon\}$.
- If $\epsilon$ is in the FIRST of **all** $Y_1 \dots Y_k$, then add $\epsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.

### 2.2 Algorithmic Rules for FOLLOW Sets

**Rule L1 — Start symbol initialization:**
Place `$` in $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$, where $S$ is the start symbol.

**Rule L2 — Production of form $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$:**
Everything in $\text{FIRST}(\beta)$ **except** $\epsilon$ is added to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

**Rule L3 — Production of form $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ or $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ where $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$:**
Everything in $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ is added to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall — Epsilon in FOLLOW?**
> $\epsilon$ is **NEVER** added to a FOLLOW set. FOLLOW always contains terminals and `$`, never the empty string. This is one of the most common errors in KTU lab records.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Rule Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V$ | Set of non-terminals (uppercase letters) | Grammar |
| $\Sigma$ | Set of terminals (lowercase / digits) | Grammar |
| $S$ | Start symbol (designated) | Grammar |
| $\epsilon$ | Empty string (epsilon) | Special |
| $\$$ | End-of-input marker | Parsing |
| $\text{FIRST}(X)$ | Set of terminals that can begin $X$ | $V \cup \Sigma$ |
| $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | Set of terminals that can follow $A$ | $V$ only |
| $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ | Standard production form | Grammar |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Predictive Parsers (LL(1))** — Used in compilers like GCC's older front-ends and many educational compilers to enable recursive-descent parsing without backtracking.
2. **Compiler Front-End Validation** — A grammar is LL(1) (and thus predictable) **iff** the FIRST/FOLLOW-derived parsing table contains no multiple entries in any cell.
3. **Grammar Engineering** — Helps identify ambiguity and left-recursion in newly designed DSLs (Domain-Specific Languages) for tools, databases, and AI configuration systems.
4. **Static Code Analysis Tools** — Linters and IDEs use similar set-based analyses to predict token sequences in partially-parsed code.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Reference Grammar (Worked Example)

Consider the classic arithmetic expression grammar used in KTU examinations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow E + T \mid T \\
T &\rightarrow T * F \mid F \\
F &\rightarrow ( E ) \mid \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $V = \{E, T, F\}$, $\Sigma = \{+, *, (, ), \text{id}\}$, and $S = E$.

### 3.2 Exhaustive FIRST Set Computation

**Step 1 — Compute FIRST(F):**
- Production $F \rightarrow (E)$: rule F3 → add `\(` to FIRST(F).
- Production $F \rightarrow \text{id}$: rule F3 → add `id` to FIRST(F).
- No $\epsilon$-production for $F$.

$$
\text{FIRST}(F) = \{\text{\texttt{(}}, \text{\texttt{id}}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute FIRST(T):**
- Production $T \rightarrow T * F$: rule F4 (chained) → add FIRST(T) $\setminus \{\epsilon\}$. Since $\epsilon \notin$ FIRST(T), we simply add FIRST(T) itself, but this is recursive — we defer.
- Production $T \rightarrow F$: rule F4 → add FIRST(F) = $\{\text{\texttt{(}}, \text{\texttt{id}}\}$.

Combining both: $\text{FIRST}(T) = \{\text{\texttt{(}}, \text{\texttt{id}}\}$.

**Step 3 — Compute FIRST(E):**
- Production $E \rightarrow E + T$: deferred (recursive).
- Production $E \rightarrow T$: rule F4 → add FIRST(T) = $\{\text{\texttt{(}}, \text{\texttt{id}}\}$.

$$
\boxed{\text{FIRST}(E) = \text{FIRST}(T) = \text{FIRST}(F) = \{\text{\texttt{(}}, \text{\texttt{id}}\}}
$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive FOLLOW Set Computation

**Step 1 — Initialize (Rule L1):**
$$
\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$\}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Rule L2 to production $E \rightarrow E + T$:**
$B = T$, $\beta = \epsilon$ (nothing after $T$). Apply Rule L3 — add FOLLOW(E) to FOLLOW(T).
$$
\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{\$\} \cup \text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$\}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Rule L2 to production $E \rightarrow T$:**
$B = T$, $\beta = \epsilon$. Rule L3 — add FOLLOW(E) to FOLLOW(T).
$$
\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{\$, +\}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Rule L2 to production $T \rightarrow T * F$:**
$B = F$, $\beta = \epsilon$. Rule L3 — add FOLLOW(T) to FOLLOW(F).
$$
\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{\$, +\}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Rule L2 to production $T \rightarrow F$:**
$B = F$, $\beta = \epsilon$. Rule L3 — add FOLLOW(T) to FOLLOW(F).
$$
\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{\$, +, *\}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Rule L2 to production $F \rightarrow (E)$:**
$B = E$, $\beta = \text{\texttt{)}}$. Rule L2 — add FIRST(`)`) = $\{\text{\texttt{)}}\}$ to FOLLOW(E).
$$
\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$, \text{\texttt{)}}\}
$$

**Step 7 — Re-evaluate E from Step 2 (closed fixed-point):**
$E \rightarrow E + T$ again: nothing new to add.

$$
\boxed{\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$, \text{\texttt{)}}\}, \quad \text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{\$, +\}, \quad \text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{\$, +, *\}}
$$

### 3.4 Final Analytical Matrix (Consolidated Result)

| Non-Terminal | FIRST | FOLLOW |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E$ | $\{\text{\texttt{(}}, \text{\texttt{id}}\}$ | $\{\$, \text{\texttt{)}}\}$ |
| $T$ | $\{\text{\texttt{(}}, \text{\texttt{id}}\}$ | $\{\$, +, *\}$ |
| $F$ | $\{\text{\texttt{(}}, \text{\texttt{id}}\}$ | $\{\$, +, *\}$ |

### 3.5 Reference C-Program Skeleton (KTU Lab Expectation)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <ctype.h>

#define MAX 20

char prod[MAX][MAX];
char ter[MAX], nonter[MAX];
int n;

int isNonTerminal(char c) {
    return (c >= 'A' && c <= 'Z');
}

void findFirst(char c, int visited[]) {
    if (!isNonTerminal(c)) {
        printf("%c ", c);
        return;
    }
    if (visited[c - 'A']) return;
    visited[c - 'A'] = 1;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (prod[i][0] == c) {
            if (prod[i][3] == '#') {           // epsilon production
                printf("# ");
            } else {
                int j = 3;
                while (prod[i][j] != '\0') {
                    int temp[26] = {0};
                    findFirst(prod[i][j], temp);
                    if (strchr(ter, prod[i][j]) == NULL && prod[i][j] != '#')
                        break;
                    j++;
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

void findFollow(char c) {
    if (c == prod[0][0]) printf("$ ");          // start symbol
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        for (int j = 3; prod[i][j] != '\0'; j++) {
            if (prod[i][j] == c) {
                if (prod[i][j+1] != '\0') {
                    if (!isNonTerminal(prod[i][j+1]))
                        printf("%c ", prod[i][j+1]);
                    else {
                        int temp[26] = {0};
                        findFirst(prod[i][j+1], temp);
                    }
                }
                // Rule L3 propagation handled separately in full version
            }
        }
    }
}

int main() {
    printf("Enter number of productions: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);
    printf("Enter productions (e.g., E->E+T, use # for epsilon):\n");
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) scanf("%s", prod[i]);

    printf("\n--- FIRST sets ---\n");
    int visited[26] = {0};
    findFirst('E', visited);

    printf("\n--- FOLLOW sets ---\n");
    findFollow('E');

    return 0;
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is the **reference skeleton**; full lab records must include iterative fixed-point loops (because FIRST/FOLLOW rules reference each other transitively). KTU evaluators check for the `$` marker in FOLLOW(start) and proper handling of `#` (epsilon).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart — FIRST Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: For each non-terminal A] --> B[Mark A as visited]
    B --> C[Scan all productions of A]
    C --> D{Is RHS a terminal?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Add terminal to FIRST A]
    D -- No --> F{Is RHS epsilon?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Add epsilon to FIRST A]
    F -- No --> H[Recurse on first symbol of RHS]
    H --> I{Did recursed symbol produce epsilon?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Add FIRST of next symbol]
    I -- No --> K[Stop for this production]
    E --> L[Next production]
    G --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> C
    C --> M[All productions done?]
    M -- Yes --> N[Fixed point reached: Print FIRST A]
    N --> O[Move to next non-terminal]
```

### 4.2 Algorithm Flowchart — FOLLOW Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: For each non-terminal A] --> B{Is A the start symbol?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Add dollar to FOLLOW A]
    B -- No --> D[Scan all productions]
    C --> D
    D --> E{A appears at position i in RHS?}
    E -- No --> F[Skip this production]
    E -- Yes --> G{Is there a symbol at i plus 1?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Add FIRST of that symbol minus epsilon]
    G -- No --> I[Add FOLLOW of LHS to FOLLOW A]
    H --> J{Is that symbol a non-terminal producing epsilon?}
    J -- Yes --> I
    J -- No --> K[Continue]
    I --> L[Next occurrence of A]
    K --> L
    L --> D
    D --> M[All productions done?]
    M -- No --> D
    M -- Yes --> N[Fixed point reached: Print FOLLOW A]
    N --> O[Move to next non-terminal]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — LL(1) Parser Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer[Input Stage]
        I1[Grammar Productions]
        I2[Start Symbol S]
    end
    subgraph analysisLayer[Set Analysis Stage]
        P1[FIRST Set Builder]
        P2[FOLLOW Set Builder]
        P3[Nullable Set Builder]
    end
    subgraph tableLayer[Table Construction Stage]
        T1[Parsing Table MNT a]
        T2[Conflict Detector]
    end
    subgraph parseLayer[Parsing Stage]
        R1[LL1 Recursive Descent Parser]
        R2[Parse Tree Output]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I1 --> P2
    I2 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    P1 --> T1
    P2 --> T1
    P1 --> P3
    P3 --> P2
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> R1
    T1 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
```

### 4.4 Dependency Graph — How Rules Reference Each Other

```mermaid
graph TD
    rF1[Rule F1: Terminal FIRST] --> finalSet[Final FIRST Set]
    rF2[Rule F2: Epsilon] --> finalSet
    rF3[Rule F3: Terminal RHS] --> finalSet
    rF4[Rule F4: Chained FIRST] --> finalSet
    rL1[Rule L1: Start dollar] --> finalFollow[Final FOLLOW Set]
    rL2[Rule L2: FIRST beta] --> finalFollow
    rL3[Rule L3: Propagate FOLLOW] --> finalFollow
    finalSet --> rL2
    rF4 --> finalSet
    rL3 --> finalFollow
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
> **Q1. Define FIRST and FOLLOW. Why are they needed in compiler design?**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ is the set of terminals that begin strings derivable from $\alpha$. $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ for a non-terminal $A$ is the set of terminals that can immediately follow $A$ in some sentential form, including `$` if $A$ can be the rightmost symbol in a derivation from the start symbol. They are essential for constructing **LL(1) predictive parsing tables**, which enable a parser to make a single-token look-ahead decision without backtracking, thereby improving parser efficiency and ensuring deterministic parsing for suitable grammars. *(3 marks)*

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
> **Q2. State and explain the rule for adding $\epsilon$ to FIRST and FOLLOW sets.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> $\epsilon$ is added to $\text{FIRST}(X)$ whenever $X$ directly produces $\epsilon$ (production $X \rightarrow \epsilon$) or whenever all symbols on the right-hand side of a production $X \rightarrow Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_k$ have $\epsilon$ in their FIRST sets. **However, $\epsilon$ is NEVER added to any FOLLOW set** — FOLLOW contains only terminals and the end-marker `$`. This asymmetry is a frequent KTU evaluation point. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** For the grammar below, compute the FIRST sets of all non-terminals. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Compute the FOLLOW sets of all non-terminals and verify if the grammar is LL(1). *(7 marks)*
>
> Grammar:
> $$S \rightarrow A B \mid b C$$
> $$A \rightarrow a \mid \epsilon$$
> $$B \rightarrow b \mid \epsilon$$
> $$C \rightarrow c \mid \epsilon$$

### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) FIRST Set Computation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — FIRST(A):
- $A \rightarrow a$: add `a`.
- $A \rightarrow \epsilon$: add $\epsilon$.

$$
\boxed{\text{FIRST}(A) = \{\text{\texttt{a}}, \epsilon\}}
$$

Step 2 — FIRST(B):
- $B \rightarrow b$: add `b`.
- $B \rightarrow \epsilon$: add $\epsilon$.

$$
\boxed{\text{FIRST}(B) = \{\text{\texttt{b}}, \epsilon\}}
$$

Step 3 — FIRST(C):
- $C \rightarrow c$: add `c`.
- $C \rightarrow \epsilon$: add $\epsilon$.

$$
\boxed{\text{FIRST}(C) = \{\text{\texttt{c}}, \epsilon\}}
$$

Step 4 — FIRST(S):
- $S \rightarrow A B$: add FIRST(A) $\setminus \{\epsilon\}$ = $\{a\}$. Since $\epsilon \in$ FIRST(A), also add FIRST(B) $\setminus \{\epsilon\}$ = $\{b\}$. Since $\epsilon \in$ FIRST(B), add $\epsilon$.
- $S \rightarrow b C$: add `b` (already present).

$$
\boxed{\text{FIRST}(S) = \{\text{\texttt{a}}, \text{\texttt{b}}, \epsilon\}}
$$

**(b) FOLLOW Set Computation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Initialize: $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$.

Step 2 — $S \rightarrow A B$: $B$ is at end → add FOLLOW(S) to FOLLOW(B). $\text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{\$\}$.

Step 3 — $S \rightarrow A B$: $A$'s follower is $B$, and $\epsilon \in$ FIRST(B), so add FOLLOW(S) to FOLLOW(A). Also add FIRST(B) $\setminus \{\epsilon\} = \{b\}$ to FOLLOW(A). So $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{\$, b\}$.

Step 4 — $S \rightarrow b C$: $C$ is at end → add FOLLOW(S) to FOLLOW(C). $\text{FOLLOW}(C) = \{\$\}$.

Step 5 — $S \rightarrow A B$: $A$'s follower is $B$; $\epsilon \in$ FIRST(B), so add FOLLOW(S) to FOLLOW(A) (already covered). Also, $B$'s follower is end, $\epsilon \in$ FIRST(B) propagated to A, but already considered.

Re-check: Production $S \rightarrow A B$ — A is followed by B, and $\epsilon \in$ FIRST(B) → add FOLLOW(S) to FOLLOW(A). Already done.

$$
\boxed{\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}, \quad \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{\$, \text{\texttt{b}}\}, \quad \text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{\$\}, \quad \text{FOLLOW}(C) = \{\$\}}
$$

**LL(1) Verification:**
- $A$ has two productions: $a$ and $\epsilon$. FIRST($a$) $\cap$ FIRST($\epsilon$) = $\{a\} \cap \{\epsilon\} = \emptyset$. ✓
- $B$: $\{b\} \cap \{\epsilon\} = \emptyset$. ✓
- $C$: $\{c\} \cap \{\epsilon\} = \emptyset$. ✓
- $S$: Productions $AB$ and $bC$. FIRST($AB$) = $\{a, b, \epsilon\}$ and FIRST($bC$) = $\{b\}$. Intersection = $\{b\}$ ≠ ∅. **Grammar is NOT LL(1).** ✗

---

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> **(a)** Explain with an algorithm how the FIRST and FOLLOW sets of a Context-Free Grammar (CFG) are computed. Use the grammar $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$ as illustration. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** For the grammar $S \rightarrow a S b S \mid b S a S \mid \epsilon$, compute the FIRST and FOLLOW sets. Is this grammar LL(1)? Justify. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Algorithm Description (7 marks):**

**FIRST Algorithm:**
1. For each terminal $a$: FIRST($a$) = $\{a\}$.
2. If $X \rightarrow \epsilon$ exists: add $\epsilon$ to FIRST($X$).
3. If $X \rightarrow Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_k$:
   - Add FIRST($Y_1$) $\setminus \{\epsilon\}$ to FIRST($X$).
   - If $\epsilon \in$ FIRST($Y_1$): add FIRST($Y_2$) $\setminus \{\epsilon\}$, and so on.
   - If $\epsilon$ is in FIRST of all $Y_i$: add $\epsilon$ to FIRST($X$).
4. Repeat until no change (fixed point).

**FOLLOW Algorithm:**
1. Place `$` in FOLLOW($S$) (start symbol).
2. For $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$: add FIRST($\beta$) $\setminus \{\epsilon\}$ to FOLLOW($B$).
3. For $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ or $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ with $\epsilon \in$ FIRST($\beta$): add FOLLOW($A$) to FOLLOW($B$).
4. Repeat until no change.

**Illustration with $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$:**
- FIRST($T$) is computed based on additional productions given in the full grammar.
- For the simplified case: FIRST($E$) = FIRST($T$) (from production $E \rightarrow T$).
- FOLLOW($E$) = $\{\$, )\}$ (using Rule L3 from $E \rightarrow E + T$ and $E \rightarrow T$).
- FOLLOW($T$) = FOLLOW($E$) $\cup \{+\} = \{\$, ), +\}$.

**(b) Computation for $S \rightarrow a S b S \mid b S a S \mid \epsilon$ (7 marks):**

FIRST(S):
- From $S \rightarrow \epsilon$: add $\epsilon$.
- From $S \rightarrow a S b S$: add `a`.
- From $S \rightarrow b S a S$: add `b`.

$$
\boxed{\text{FIRST}(S) = \{\text{\texttt{a}}, \text{\texttt{b}}, \epsilon\}}
$$

FOLLOW(S):
- Initialize: FOLLOW($S$) = $\{\$\}$.
- From $S \rightarrow a S b S$: second $S$ is followed by `b`. Add `b` to FOLLOW($S$).
- From $S \rightarrow b S a S$: second $S$ is followed by `a`. Add `a` to FOLLOW($S$).
- From $S \rightarrow a S b S$: first $S$ is followed by `b`; FIRST($bS$) does not contain $\epsilon$, so no propagation.
- From $S \rightarrow b S a S$: first $S$ is followed by `a`; no propagation.

$$
\boxed{\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\text{\texttt{a}}, \text{\texttt{b}}, \$\}}
$$

**LL(1) Verification:**
- For $S$: Productions are $aSbS$, $bSaS$, $\epsilon$.
- FIRST($aSbS$) = $\{a\}$, FIRST($bSaS$) = $\{b\}$, FIRST($\epsilon$) = $\{\epsilon\}$.
- Since $\epsilon \in$ FIRST($S$), we must check FOLLOW($S$) for the $\epsilon$-production conflicts.
- FOLLOW($S$) = $\{a, b, \$\}$.
- FOLLOW($S$) $\cap$ FIRST($aSbS$) = $\{a, b, \$\} \cap \{a\} = \{a\}$ ≠ ∅. **Conflict detected.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Grammar is NOT LL(1).}}
$$

The grammar is the well-known **ambiguous grammar for matched brackets** and is inherently non-LL(1); it requires an LR parser.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — Read Carefully**
> 1. **Forgetting `$` in FOLLOW(start):** The end-marker **must** be added to the FOLLOW set of the start symbol. Examiners specifically test this. Loss: up to **1 mark**.
> 2. **Adding $\epsilon$ to FOLLOW sets:** FOLLOW must NEVER contain $\epsilon$. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Not iterating to fixed point:** FIRST and FOLLOW rules are mutually recursive (FIRST depends on chained non-terminals; FOLLOW depends on FOLLOW of LHS). You must iterate until no new symbol is added. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing FIRST of LHS with FIRST of RHS:** FIRST($X$) is computed from the *right-hand sides* of $X$'s productions, never the left-hand side.
> 5. **In Lab Exam:** When demonstrating output, ensure each non-terminal is printed on a separate line, formatted as `FIRST(E) = { (, id }` exactly — formatting matters in KTU records.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FIRST** is defined for **all grammar symbols** (terminals + non-terminals); **FOLLOW** is defined **only for non-terminals**.
- **$\epsilon$ is a member of FIRST, never of FOLLOW.** (Hard rule.)
- The end-marker **$** is **only** placed in FOLLOW of the **start symbol** initially.
- **Rule F4 (chained):** If RHS is $Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_k$ and $\epsilon$ is in FIRST of each $Y_i$, then $\epsilon \in$ FIRST of LHS.
- **Rule L3 (propagation):** If $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ and $B$ can derive $\epsilon$, then FOLLOW($A$) $\subseteq$ FOLLOW($B$).
- The sets must be computed **iteratively** until a **fixed point** is reached.
- A grammar is **LL(1)** if and only if the parsing table built from FIRST/FOLLOW has **no multiply-defined entries**.
- **In the lab program:** Always:
  1. Hardcode the start symbol.
  2. Use `#` or `e` for $\epsilon$ in input productions.
  3. Print `$` in FOLLOW(start).
  4. Terminate the fixed-point loop only when no set changes in an iteration.
- **Real-world link:** GCC's older recursive-descent parsers, ANTLR-generated parsers, and many IDE syntax highlighters rely on FIRST/FOLLOW-equivalent computations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and implement a recursive descent parser for a given grammar
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL607 - SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and implement a recursive descent parser for a given grammar

# Experiment 2 — Design and Implement a Recursive Descent Parser for a Given Grammar

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Recursive Descent Parser (RDP)** is a top-down, predictive, syntax-analysis technique in which each **non-terminal symbol** of a context-free grammar is realized as a **distinct recursive procedure (function)**. The parser begins with the start symbol of the grammar and attempts to construct a parse tree by recursively invoking the procedure corresponding to the non-terminal that the parser currently expects to recognize. To make deterministic, backtrack-free decisions at every step, the grammar must satisfy the **LL(1)** property — meaning the parser scans the input **Left-to-right**, produces a **Leftmost derivation**, and uses only **1 token of look-ahead** to select the correct production rule.

For a grammar to be parsed by RDP without backtracking, the **FIRST** sets of the right-hand side alternatives of any non-terminal must be **pairwise disjoint**, and the parser must consult a **predictive parsing table** $M[A, a]$ indexed by a non-terminal $A$ and a terminal (look-ahead) $a$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCSL607 — Systems Lab, Module 2):**
> Students are expected to (i) remove **left recursion**, (ii) perform **left factoring** if required, (iii) compute **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets, (iv) construct the **predictive parsing table**, and finally (v) **implement and execute** the recursive descent parser in C for a sample arithmetic-expression grammar and validate it against test strings.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Reading a Recipe with Sub-Procedures

Imagine you are reading a recipe book and the recipe says: *"Make a cake (then make icing (then decorate (then serve))))"*. Instead of trying to understand everything at once, you break it down:

- Procedure **`MakeCake`** asks: "Do I need eggs?" → calls `BeatEggs`
- Procedure **`MakeIcing`** asks: "Do I need sugar?" → calls `MixSugar`
- Procedure **`BeatEggs`** is a sub-routine that returns control to `MakeCake` after finishing

A **recursive descent parser does exactly the same thing** with grammar rules. Each grammar rule (non-terminal) becomes a function. When a function sees its rule, it checks the next word (token) in the input and decides which sub-function to call next. The **"sub-function"** concept matches a **production rule**, and the **"next word"** matches the **look-ahead token**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy summary:**
> Recipe Book ≡ Grammar | Recipe Step ≡ Production Rule | Chef checking the next ingredient ≡ Parser reading next token | Sub-recipe calls ≡ Recursive function calls.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Parameters

The following parameters govern the behaviour of any RDP implementation:

| Parameter | Symbol / Formula | Standard Value / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Look-ahead tokens | $k$ | **$k = 1$** (LL(1) class) |
| Maximum recursion depth | $D$ | Bounded by input length $n$ |
| Time complexity | $T(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for LL(1) grammars |
| Space complexity (call stack) | $S(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ worst case |
| Grammar size | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | $V$ non-terminals, $T$ terminals, $P$ productions, $S$ start symbol |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conditions for Recursive Descent Parsability

A grammar is suitable for RDP **if and only if** it can be transformed into an **LL(1) grammar**. The two mandatory pre-processing steps are:

1. **Elimination of Left Recursion** — both immediate and indirect.
2. **Left Factoring** — to combine productions that share a common prefix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently award **2 marks** just for correctly identifying whether the given grammar is LL(1) and stating the transformations applied. Always show the *transformed* grammar in your answer.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Constructing an RDP

The construction follows a deterministic pipeline:

1. **Input**: A context-free grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$.
2. **Pre-process** the grammar (remove left recursion, left factor).
3. **Compute FIRST sets** for all grammar symbols.
4. **Compute FOLLOW sets** for all non-terminals.
5. **Build the predictive parsing table** $M[A, a]$.
6. **Verify** that $M$ has **no multiple entries** (this confirms LL(1) property).
7. **Translate** each production $A \to \alpha$ into a procedure `A()` in C.
8. **Execute** the parser against test inputs.

### 2.3 FIRST and FOLLOW Set Definitions

$$
\text{FIRST}(\alpha) = \{\, a \in T \cup \{\varepsilon\} \mid \alpha \Rightarrow^{*} a\beta \,\}
$$

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{\, a \in T \cup \{\$\} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} \alpha A a \beta \,\}
$$

where **\$** is the end-of-input marker.

**Rules for computing FIRST:**

- If $X$ is a terminal, then $\text{FIRST}(X) = \{X\}$.
- If $X \to \varepsilon$ is a production, add $\varepsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.
- If $X \to Y_1 Y_2 \ldots Y_k$, add $\text{FIRST}(Y_1) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$; if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(Y_1)$, add $\text{FIRST}(Y_2) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$; and so on.

**Rules for computing FOLLOW:**

- Place **\$** in $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$, where $S$ is the start symbol.
- If $A \to \alpha B \beta$, then every element of $\text{FIRST}(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$ is in $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.
- If $A \to \alpha B$ or $A \to \alpha B \beta$ with $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, then $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

### 2.4 Predictive Parsing Table Construction

For every production $A \to \alpha$ in the grammar:

$$
M[A, a] = A \to \alpha \quad \text{for every } a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)
$$

$$
M[A, b] = A \to \alpha \quad \text{for every } b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A), \text{ if } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> If the same cell $M[A, a]$ receives **more than one production**, the grammar is **NOT LL(1)** and cannot be parsed by a non-backtracking recursive descent parser.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | LL(1) condition | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \cap \text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) = \emptyset$ for all $A \to \alpha_i \mid \alpha_j$ | Ensures unique production choice |
| 2 | FIRST base | $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$ for terminal $a$ | Base case for FIRST |
| 3 | FIRST epsilon | $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(A)$ if $A \to \varepsilon$ | Detects nullable non-terminals |
| 4 | FOLLOW start | $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$ | Marks end-of-input |
| 5 | FOLLOW propagation | $A \to \alpha B \beta \Rightarrow \text{FIRST}(\beta)\setminus\{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$ | Carries terminals to followers |
| 6 | Table fill rule | $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha) \Rightarrow M[A, a] = A \to \alpha$ | Builds parsing table entries |
| 7 | Direct left recursion | $A \to A\alpha \mid \beta$ becomes $A \to \beta A'$ and $A' \to \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$ | Eliminates immediate left recursion |
| 8 | Indirect left recursion | Substitute and apply direct-elimination rule | Eliminates indirect left recursion |
| 9 | Left factoring | $A \to \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2$ becomes $A \to \alpha A'$ and $A' \to \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$ | Removes common prefixes |
| 10 | Complexity | $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n)$, $S(n) = \mathcal{O}(n)$ | Bounds RDP performance |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recursive descent parsers form the **backbone of compilers and interpreters** for most modern programming languages. Real-world deployments include:

- **GCC** uses recursive descent techniques for several language front-ends.
- **Python's `pgen`** parser generator emits recursive descent parsers.
- **JSON / XML / HTML parsers** in browsers and web servers are often hand-written RDPs.
- **Expression evaluators** in spreadsheets, calculators, and database query engines use RDP for arithmetic and Boolean sub-grammars.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Reference Grammar for the Experiment

We adopt the classical **arithmetic expression grammar** (without left recursion), which is the de-facto KTU lab standard for this experiment:

$$
E \to T\,E'
$$

$$
E' \to +\,T\,E' \;\mid\; -\,T\,E' \;\mid\; \varepsilon
$$

$$
T \to F\,T'
$$

$$
T' \to *\,F\,T' \;\mid\; /\,F\,T' \;\mid\; \varepsilon
$$

$$
F \to (E) \;\mid\; \text{id} \;\mid\; \text{num}
$$

**Non-terminals**: $V = \{E, E', T, T', F\}$ | **Terminals**: $T = \{+, -, *, /, (, ), \text{id}, \text{num}\}$ | **Start symbol**: $S = E$.

### 3.2 Derivation of FIRST Sets

Apply the FIRST computation rules iteratively until no change occurs.

**Step 1 — Base assignments from productions**

- $F \to (E)$ gives $\text{FIRST}(F) \supseteq \{(\}$
- $F \to \text{id}$ gives $\text{FIRST}(F) \supseteq \{\text{id}\}$
- $F \to \text{num}$ gives $\text{FIRST}(F) \supseteq \{\text{num}\}$
- $T' \to *F T'$ gives $\text{FIRST}(T') \supseteq \{*\}$
- $T' \to /F T'$ gives $\text{FIRST}(T') \supseteq \{/\}$

**Step 2 — Propagation of epsilon**

- $T' \to \varepsilon$ gives $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T')$.
- $E' \to \varepsilon$ gives $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$.

**Step 3 — Cascading effect on $T$ and $E$**

$$
\text{FIRST}(T) = \text{FIRST}(F) \cup (\text{FIRST}(T') \text{ if } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T'))
$$

Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T')$, we include it as well. So:

$$
\text{FIRST}(T) = \{(\,,\, \text{id}\,,\, \text{num}\,,\, \varepsilon\}
$$

Similarly:

$$
\text{FIRST}(E) = \text{FIRST}(T) \cup (\text{FIRST}(E') \text{ if } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T))
$$

Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T)$ and $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$:

$$
\text{FIRST}(E) = \{+\,,\,-,\,(\,,\, \text{id}\,,\, \text{num}\,,\, \varepsilon\}
$$

**Step 4 — Final compiled FIRST set table**

| Symbol | FIRST set |
| :--- | :--- |
| $E$ | $\{+\,,\,-,\,(\,,\, \text{id}\,,\, \text{num}\,,\, \varepsilon\}$ |
| $E'$ | $\{+\,,\,-,\, \varepsilon\}$ |
| $T$ | $\{(\,,\, \text{id}\,,\, \text{num}\,,\, \varepsilon\}$ |
| $T'$ | $\{*\,,\,/\,,\, \varepsilon\}$ |
| $F$ | $\{(\,,\, \text{id}\,,\, \text{num}\}$ |
| $+$ | $\{+\}$ |
| $-$ | $\{-\}$ |
| $*$ | $\{*\}$ |
| $/$ | $\{/\}$ |
| $($ | $\{(\}$ |
| $)$ | $\{)\}$ |
| $\text{id}$ | $\{\text{id}\}$ |
| $\text{num}$ | $\{\text{num}\}$ |

### 3.3 Derivation of FOLLOW Sets

**Step 1 — Start symbol**

$$
\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(E) \quad \text{(since } E \text{ is the start symbol)}
$$

**Step 2 — Rule $E \to T E'$**

- $E'$ appears at the end of $E$'s right-hand side ⇒ $\text{FOLLOW}(E) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(E')$.
- $T$ is followed by $E'$, so $\text{FIRST}(E') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(T)$.

This gives us $\{+, -\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(T)$.

**Step 3 — Rule $E' \to +T E'$ and $E' \to -T E'$**

- $T$ is followed by $E'$, so $\text{FIRST}(E') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(T)$ — same as above.

**Step 4 — Rule $T \to F T'$**

- $T'$ is at the end of $T$'s right-hand side ⇒ $\text{FOLLOW}(T) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(T')$.

So $\text{FOLLOW}(T') \supseteq \{+, -, \$\}$.

**Step 5 — Rule $T' \to *F T'$ and $T' \to /F T'$**

- $F$ is followed by $T'$, so $\text{FIRST}(T') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(F)$.

This gives $\{*, /\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(F)$.

**Step 6 — Rule $F \to (E)$**

- $E$ is followed by the terminal $)$, so $\{)\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(E)$.

**Step 7 — Propagation chain**

- $\text{FOLLOW}(E) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(E')$: so $\text{FOLLOW}(E') \supseteq \{\$, )\}$.
- $\text{FOLLOW}(T) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(T')$: already covered.
- $E' \to +T E'$ with $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$: $\text{FOLLOW}(E') \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(T)$.
- $T' \to *F T'$ with $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T')$: $\text{FOLLOW}(T') \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(F)$.

So $\text{FOLLOW}(T) \supseteq \{\$, )\}$ and $\text{FOLLOW}(F) \supseteq \{+, -, *, /, \$, )\}$.

**Step 8 — Final compiled FOLLOW set table**

| Non-terminal | FOLLOW set |
| :--- | :--- |
| $E$ | $\{\$\,,\,)\}$ |
| $E'$ | $\{\$\,,\,),\,+,\,-\}$ |
| $T$ | $\{\$\,,\,),\,+,\,-\}$ |
| $T'$ | $\{\$\,,\,),\,+,\,-\}$ |
| $F$ | $\{\$\,,\,),\,+,\,-,\,*,\,/\}$ |

### 3.4 Construction of the Predictive Parsing Table

Using the rule $M[A, a] = A \to \alpha$ for every $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, and $M[A, b] = A \to \alpha$ for every $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$ when $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$:

| Non-terminal | $+$ | $-$ | $*$ | $/$ | $($ | $)$ | $\text{id}$ | $\text{num}$ | $\$$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E$ | $E \to TE'$ | $E \to TE'$ | | | $E \to TE'$ | | $E \to TE'$ | $E \to TE'$ | |
| $E'$ | $E' \to +TE'$ | $E' \to -TE'$ | | | | $E' \to \varepsilon$ | | | $E' \to \varepsilon$ |
| $T$ | | | | | $T \to FT'$ | | $T \to FT'$ | $T \to FT'$ | |
| $T'$ | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to *FT'$ | $T' \to /FT'$ | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ |
| $F$ | | | | | $F \to (E)$ | | $F \to \text{id}$ | $F \to \text{num}$ | |

> [!NOTE]
> Every cell contains **at most one** production ⇒ the grammar is **LL(1)** ⇒ the recursive descent parser is guaranteed to be **deterministic and backtrack-free**.

### 3.5 Translation of Grammar Rules into C Procedures

The predictive table above is converted directly into one C function per non-terminal. Each function:

1. Looks at the current look-ahead token.
2. Selects the appropriate production from the table.
3. For each terminal in the RHS, calls `match(token)`.
4. For each non-terminal in the RHS, calls its corresponding function.

**Skeleton C implementation (representative):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <ctype.h>

char input[100];
int idx = 0;
char lookahead;

void error(const char *msg) {
    printf("Syntax Error: %s at position %d\n", msg, idx);
    exit(1);
}

void match(char t) {
    if (lookahead == t) {
        lookahead = input[++idx];
    } else {
        error("Token mismatch");
    }
}

void E(void);   /* forward declarations */
void Eprime(void);
void T(void);
void Tprime(void);
void F(void);

void E(void) {
    /* M[E, lookahead] -> E -> T E' for ( , id, num, +, - */
    T();
    Eprime();
}

void Eprime(void) {
    if (lookahead == '+') { match('+'); T(); Eprime(); }
    else if (lookahead == '-') { match('-'); T(); Eprime(); }
    /* else epsilon: M[E', )] = eps, M[E', $] = eps */
}

void T(void) {
    F();
    Tprime();
}

void Tprime(void) {
    if (lookahead == '*') { match('*'); F(); Tprime(); }
    else if (lookahead == '/') { match('/'); F(); Tprime(); }
    /* else epsilon */
}

void F(void) {
    if (lookahead == '(') { match('('); E(); match(')'); }
    else if (lookahead == 'i') { /* id token */ match('i'); }
    else if (lookahead == 'n') { /* num token */ match('n'); }
    else error("Expected ( or id or num");
}

int main(void) {
    printf("Enter expression (end with $): ");
    scanf("%s", input);
    lookahead = input[0];
    E();
    if (lookahead == '$') printf("Parsing Successful!\n");
    else error("Extra input after valid expression");
    return 0;
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the lab, students commonly replace the single-character input with a **lexical analyser front-end** that emits tokens like `id` and `num` from real numeric input. KTU expects the lab record to include **at least 5 valid test inputs** and **at least 2 invalid test inputs** with corresponding outputs.

### 3.6 Worked Example — Parsing the String `id + id * id $`

The parser executes as follows (call-stack snapshot):

| Step | Active Call | Look-ahead | Action |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | `E()` | `id` | Calls `T()` |
| 2 | `T()` | `id` | Calls `F()` |
| 3 | `F()` | `id` | Matches `id`, returns to `T()` |
| 4 | `Tprime()` | `+` | `+` not in $\{*, /\}$ → return to `E()` |
| 5 | `Eprime()` | `+` | Matches `+`, calls `T()` |
| 6 | `T()` | `id` | Calls `F()` |
| 7 | `F()` | `id` | Matches `id`, returns |
| 8 | `Tprime()` | `*` | Matches `*`, calls `F()` |
| 9 | `F()` | `id` | Matches `id`, returns |
| 10 | `Tprime()` | `$` | Return |
| 11 | `Eprime()` | `$` | Return |
| 12 | `E()` | `$` | Accept — **Parsing Successful!** |

**Output**: `Parsing Successful!` — confirming precedence ($*$ binds tighter than $+$) is correctly enforced by the grammar.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Recursive Descent Parser Flow

The diagram below illustrates the top-down control flow of the parser, starting from the `main()` driver and propagating through the recursive procedure calls based on the predictive parsing table.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([main: read input, set lookahead]) --> B[Call E start]
    B --> C[Call T]
    C --> D[Call F]
    D --> D1{lookahead in openParen, id, num}
    D1 -- openParen --> D2[match openParen, call E, match closeParen]
    D1 -- id --> D3[match id]
    D1 -- num --> D4[match num]
    D1 -- otherwise --> D5([Syntax Error])
    D2 --> E1[Return to T]
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> F[Call Tprime]
    F --> F1{lookahead in star, slash}
    F1 -- star --> F2[match star, call F, call Tprime]
    F1 -- slash --> F3[match slash, call F, call Tprime]
    F1 -- otherwise --> F4[Return epsilon]
    F2 --> G1[Return to E]
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    G1 --> H[Call Eprime]
    H --> H1{lookahead in plus, minus}
    H1 -- plus --> H2[match plus, call T, call Eprime]
    H1 -- minus --> H3[match minus, call T, call Eprime]
    H1 -- otherwise --> H4[Return epsilon]
    H2 --> I1[Return to main]
    H3 --> I1
    H4 --> I1
    I1 --> I2{lookahead equals dollar}
    I2 -- yes --> I3([Parsing Successful])
    I2 -- no --> I5([Syntax Error extra input])
```

### 4.2 Recursive Call-Stack Architecture

This block diagram captures the **call-stack state** during the parse of `id + id * id $`, showing how each procedure returns control to its caller in strict **last-in-first-out** order.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[main frame: lookahead equals id] --> L1[Call frame E]
    L1 --> L2[Call frame T]
    L2 --> L3[Call frame F matches id returns to T]
    L3 --> L4[Call frame Tprime epsilon returns to T]
    L4 --> L5[Return to E]
    L5 --> L6[Call frame Eprime lookahead plus]
    L6 --> L7[Match plus call T call F match id]
    L7 --> L8[Call frame Tprime match star]
    L8 --> L9[Call frame F match id returns]
    L9 --> L10[Tprime epsilon returns]
    L10 --> L11[Eprime epsilon returns]
    L11 --> L12[Return to main lookahead dollar]
    L12 --> L13([Parsing Successful])
```

### 4.3 Predictive Parsing Table — Visual Block

A block-level view of the predictive parsing table, mapping each non-terminal row to its production rules keyed by look-ahead column.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ROW_E [Row E]
        E1[plus: TEprime]
        E2[minus: TEprime]
        E3[openParen: TEprime]
        E4[id: TEprime]
        E5[num: TEprime]
    end
    subgraph ROW_EPRIME [Row Eprime]
        EP1[plus: plusTEprime]
        EP2[minus: minusTEprime]
        EP3[closeParen: epsilon]
        EP4[dollar: epsilon]
    end
    subgraph ROW_T [Row T]
        T1[openParen: FTprime]
        T2[id: FTprime]
        T3[num: FTprime]
    end
    subgraph ROW_TPRIME [Row Tprime]
        TP1[plus: epsilon]
        TP2[minus: epsilon]
        TP3[star: starFTprime]
        TP4[slash: slashFTprime]
        TP5[closeParen: epsilon]
        TP6[dollar: epsilon]
    end
    subgraph ROW_F [Row F]
        F1[openParen: openParenEcloseParen]
        F2[id: id]
        F3[num: num]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three diagrams together (4.1 = top-down control, 4.2 = stack lifecycle, 4.3 = static parsing table) form the **complete mental model** required by KTU 2024 Scheme lab viva questions on recursive descent parsing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*What is a recursive descent parser? State the conditions under which a grammar can be parsed by recursive descent parsing without backtracking.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **recursive descent parser** is a top-down syntax analyser in which each non-terminal of the grammar is implemented as a separate recursive procedure. The parser begins with the start symbol's procedure and uses the current input token (look-ahead) to decide which production to apply.

A grammar can be parsed by RDP **without backtracking** if and only if it is an **LL(1) grammar**, i.e.:

1. The grammar must be **free of left recursion** (both immediate and indirect).
2. The grammar must be **left-factored** so that no two productions of a non-terminal share a common left prefix that introduces ambiguity in selection.
3. For every non-terminal $A$ with productions $A \to \alpha_1 \mid \alpha_2 \mid \ldots \mid \alpha_n$, the sets $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i)$ must be **pairwise disjoint**, and if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha_i)$, then $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i)$ for that production must be disjoint from $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$'s overlap with other productions.

When these conditions are met, the predictive parsing table $M[A, a]$ has **at most one production** in every cell, and the parser can deterministically select the next production using a single look-ahead token.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between top-down and bottom-up parsers. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Top-Down Parser | Bottom-Up Parser |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Construction direction | Builds parse tree from **root (start symbol) to leaves (terminals)** | Builds parse tree from **leaves to root** |
| Derivation produced | **Leftmost derivation** | **Rightmost derivation in reverse** |
| Typical technique | Recursive Descent, LL(1) Predictive Parsing | Shift-Reduce, LR(0), SLR(1), CLR(1), LALR(1) |
| Grammar class | LL(k) grammars | LR(k) grammars (larger class) |
| Backtracking | Possible in general RDP; eliminated in LL(1) | No backtracking in standard LR parsers |
| Example | Recursive Descent Parser for arithmetic expressions | SLR(1) parser for the same grammar |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests this comparison for 3 marks. Always present it in a table for clarity.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, S7 B.Tech CSE]`

**Q. (a)** Consider the following grammar for arithmetic expressions:

$$
S \to S + S \;\mid\; S * S \;\mid\; (S) \;\mid\; \text{id}
$$

**(i)** Show that the grammar is **ambiguous**. **(2 marks)**
**(ii)** Eliminate **left recursion** from the grammar. **(3 marks)**
**(iii)** Compute **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets for all non-terminals. **(4 marks)**
**(iv)** Construct the **predictive parsing table**. **(3 marks)**
**(v)** Show whether the grammar is **LL(1)**. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete C program to implement a **recursive descent parser** for the transformed grammar. Your program should accept an input expression and report either "Valid Expression" or a "Syntax Error" with the position. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**(i) Ambiguity proof (2 marks):**

The string `id + id * id` has **two distinct parse trees** under the given grammar, demonstrating ambiguity.

- **Parse Tree 1** (left-associative, $+$ applied first): $S \Rightarrow S + S \Rightarrow \text{id} + S \Rightarrow \text{id} + S * S \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$.
- **Parse Tree 2** ($*$ applied first): $S \Rightarrow S * S \Rightarrow S + S * S \Rightarrow \text{id} + S * \text{id} \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$.

Two distinct derivations producing the same string ⇒ **grammar is ambiguous**.

**(ii) Left recursion elimination (3 marks):**

The grammar contains the immediate left recursion $S \to S + S$ and $S \to S * S$. Apply the standard rule:

For $S \to S\alpha_1 \mid S\alpha_2 \mid \beta$ (where $\beta = (S) \mid \text{id}$), replace with:

$$
S \to \beta S'
$$

$$
S' \to \alpha_1 S' \mid \alpha_2 S' \mid \varepsilon
$$

The transformed grammar becomes:

$$
S \to (S)\, S' \;\mid\; \text{id}\, S'
$$

$$
S' \to +S\,S' \;\mid\; *S\,S' \;\mid\; \varepsilon
$$

**(iii) FIRST and FOLLOW computation (4 marks):**

**FIRST sets:**

$$
\text{FIRST}((S)S') = \{(\}
$$

$$
\text{FIRST}(\text{id}\,S') = \{\text{id}\}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{FIRST}(S) = \{(\,,\, \text{id}\}
$$

$$
\text{FIRST}(+S\,S') = \{+\}
$$

$$
\text{FIRST}(*S\,S') = \{*\}
$$

$$
\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{FIRST}(S') = \{+\,,\,*\,,\, \varepsilon\}
$$

**FOLLOW sets:**

- Start symbol $S$: $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$.
- Rule $S \to (S)S'$: terminal `)` follows inner $S$, so $\{)\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S)$.
- Rule $S \to \text{id}\,S'$: $S'$ is at the end, so $\text{FOLLOW}(S) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S')$.
- Rule $S' \to +S\,S'$: inner $S$ is followed by $S'$, so $\text{FIRST}(S') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S)$. This gives $\{+, *\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S)$.
- Rule $S' \to \varepsilon$: nothing added.

Compiling:

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\,,\,),\,+,\,*\,\}
$$

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(S') = \{\$\,,\,),\,+,\,*\,\}
$$

**(iv) Predictive parsing table (3 marks):**

| Non-terminal | $+$ | $*$ | $($ | $)$ | $\text{id}$ | $\$$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $S$ | | | $S \to (S)S'$ | | $S \to \text{id}\,S'$ | |
| $S'$ | $S' \to +S\,S'$ | $S' \to *S\,S'$ | | $S' \to \varepsilon$ | | $S' \to \varepsilon$ |

**(v) LL(1) check (2 marks):**

Every cell in the predictive parsing table contains **at most one production**, and there are **no multiple entries**. Hence the transformed grammar is **LL(1)** and is suitable for recursive descent parsing.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — C Program (7 marks)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <ctype.h>

char input[200];
int pos = 0;
char la;  /* look-ahead */

void error(const char *msg) {
    printf("Syntax Error near position %d: %s\n", pos, msg);
    exit(1);
}

void match(char t) {
    if (la == t) {
        pos++;
        la = input[pos];
    } else {
        char buf[64];
        sprintf(buf, "Expected '%c'", t);
        error(buf);
    }
}

void S(void);   /* forward decls */
void Sprint(void);

void Sprint(void) {
    if (la == '+')      { match('+'); S(); Sprint(); }
    else if (la == '*') { match('*'); S(); Sprint(); }
    /* else epsilon */
}

void S(void) {
    if (la == '(')      { match('('); S(); match(')'); Sprint(); }
    else if (la == 'i') { match('i'); Sprint(); }   /* id token */
    else                error("Expected '(' or 'id'");
}

int main(void) {
    printf("Enter expression (end with $): ");
    scanf("%s", input);
    la = input[0];
    S();
    if (la == '$') printf("Valid Expression\n");
    else           error("Trailing tokens after valid expression");
    return 0;
}
```

**Key points the examiner awards marks for:**

1. One function per non-terminal — `S()` and `Sprime()` — **2 marks**.
2. Correct use of a global `lookahead` variable and `match()` helper — **2 marks**.
3. Proper handling of the **epsilon production** in `Sprime()` via `if-else` cascade — **1 mark**.
4. Error reporting with position information — **1 mark**.
5. Correct `main()` driver that calls the start symbol and checks end-of-input `$` — **1 mark**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, S7 B.Tech CSE, Set A]`

**Q. (a)** For the grammar:

$$
E \to T\,E'
$$

$$
E' \to +\,T\,E' \;\mid\; \varepsilon
$$

$$
T \to F\,T'
$$

$$
T' \to *\,F\,T' \;\mid\; \varepsilon
$$

$$
F \to (E) \;\mid\; \text{id}
$$

**(i)** Compute the **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets of all non-terminals. **(4 marks)**
**(ii)** Construct the **predictive parsing table** and verify the grammar is **LL(1)**. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Implement a recursive descent parser in C for the above grammar. Demonstrate its working on the input strings: **(i)** `id + id * id $`, **(ii)** `( id + id ) * id $`, **(iii)** `id + ( id $` (should fail). Show sample outputs. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**(i) FIRST and FOLLOW sets (4 marks):**

$$
\text{FIRST}(F) = \{(\,,\, \text{id}\}
$$

$$
\text{FIRST}(T') = \{*\,,\, \varepsilon\}
$$

$$
\text{FIRST}(T) = \text{FIRST}(F) \cup \{*\,,\, \varepsilon\} = \{(\,,\, \text{id}\,,\, *\,,\, \varepsilon\}
$$

$$
\text{FIRST}(E') = \{+\,,\, \varepsilon\}
$$

$$
\text{FIRST}(E) = \text{FIRST}(T) \cup \{+\,,\, \varepsilon\} = \{(\,,\, \text{id}\,,\, *\,,\, +\,,\, \varepsilon\}
$$

**FOLLOW sets:**

- $\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$\,,\,)\}$ (start symbol plus from $F \to (E)$).
- $\text{FOLLOW}(E') = \text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$\,,\,)\}$ (since $E' \to +TE'$ and $E \to TE'$).
- $\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{+\,,\, \$\,,\,)\}$ (FIRST of $E'$ minus epsilon, plus FOLLOW of $E$).
- $\text{FOLLOW}(T') = \text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{+\,,\, \$\,,\,)\}$.
- $\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{*\,,\, +\,,\, \$\,,\,)\}$ (FIRST of $T'$ minus epsilon, plus FOLLOW of $T$).

**(ii) Predictive parsing table and LL(1) verification (3 marks):**

| Non-terminal | $+$ | $*$ | $($ | $)$ | $\text{id}$ | $\$$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E$ | | | $E \to TE'$ | | $E \to TE'$ | |
| $E'$ | $E' \to +TE'$ | | | $E' \to \varepsilon$ | | $E' \to \varepsilon$ |
| $T$ | | | $T \to FT'$ | | $T \to FT'$ | |
| $T'$ | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to *FT'$ | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ |
| $F$ | | | $F \to (E)$ | | $F \to \text{id}$ | |

**Verification**: No cell has more than one entry ⇒ **grammar is LL(1)**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — C Program (7 marks)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>

char input[200];
int pos = 0;
char la;

void error(const char *m) {
    printf("Syntax Error at pos %d: %s\n", pos, m);
    exit(1);
}

void match(char t) {
    if (la == t) { pos++; la = input[pos]; }
    else error("token mismatch");
}

void E(void), Ep(void), T(void), Tp(void), F(void);

void E(void)   { T();  Ep(); }
void Ep(void)  { if (la == '+') { match('+'); T(); Ep(); } /* eps */ }
