void T(void)   { F();  Tp(); }
void Tp(void)  { if (la == '*') { match('*'); F(); Tp(); } /* eps */ }
void F(void)   {
    if (la == '(')      { match('('); E(); match(')'); }
    else if (la == 'i') { match('i'); }   /* id */
    else                error("Expected ( or id");
}

int main(void) {
    printf("Enter expression: ");
    scanf("%s", input);
    la = input[0];
    E();
    if (la == '$') printf("Valid Expression\n");
    else           error("Trailing input");
    return 0;
}
```

**Sample outputs expected by examiner:**

| Test Input | Expected Output |
| :--- | :--- |
| `id+id*id$` | `Valid Expression` |
| `(id+id)*id$` | `Valid Expression` |
| `id+(id$` | `Syntax Error at pos 6: token mismatch` |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to handle the epsilon production** in `Eprime()` or `Tprime()` — the `if-else` chain **must terminate** without an `error()` call when the look-ahead is in the FOLLOW set. Half the marks for parser implementation depend on this.
> 2. **Failing to check the final `$`** in `main()` — a parser that consumes a valid prefix and exits silently is **incomplete**. KTU specifically deducts 1–2 marks for this.
> 3. **Using `gets()`** — undefined behaviour in C11 onwards. Use `scanf("%s", ...)` or `fgets()`. Examiners flag this.
> 4. **Not reporting the position of the error** — always include `pos` in your error message.
> 5. **Conflating `id` and `num`** — for the standard KTU grammar, treat both as separate terminals. Examiners award a separate sub-mark for this distinction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **recursive descent parser** is a **top-down, predictive** parser built as one recursive procedure per non-terminal. **(Must remember)**
- For backtrack-free RDP, the grammar **must be LL(1)** — no left recursion, no common-prefix ambiguity. **(Must remember)**
- The construction pipeline is: **Remove left recursion → Left factor → Compute FIRST → Compute FOLLOW → Build predictive table → Translate table to C functions**. **(Must remember)**
- **FIRST($A$)** collects all terminals that can begin a string derived from $A$, including $\varepsilon$ if nullable. **(Must remember)**
- **FOLLOW($A$)** collects all terminals that can immediately follow $A$ in some sentential form, plus $\$$ if $A$ is the start symbol. **(Must remember)**
- Table entry rule: $M[A, a] = A \to \alpha$ for every $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$; if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, also fill $M[A, b] = A \to \alpha$ for every $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$. **(Must remember)**
- If **any cell has more than one production**, the grammar is **not LL(1)** and a backtrack-free RDP is impossible. **(Must remember)**
- Each non-terminal function must contain an **`if-else` cascade** mirroring the columns of the predictive table row for that non-terminal, with the **epsilon case** implemented as the implicit `else` (no action). **(Must remember)**
- Always use a **global `lookahead` variable**, a **`match(t)` helper**, and an **`error(msg)` function** that prints the position and exits. **(Must remember)**
- The `main()` driver must (1) read input, (2) initialise look-ahead, (3) call the start-symbol's function, and (4) verify the look-ahead is `$`. **(Must remember)**
- The classical KTU lab grammar is the **arithmetic expression grammar** for $E, T, F$ with operators $+$, $-$, $*$, $/$, parentheses, `id`, and `num`. **(Must remember)**
- Time and space complexity of RDP for LL(1) grammars is **$\mathcal{O}(n)$** in input length $n$. **(Must remember)**
- **Common viva questions:** "Why is left recursion a problem for RDP?", "What happens if the grammar is not LL(1)?", "How is the predictive table related to the C code?", "What is the role of FOLLOW in the epsilon transition?" — prepare crisp 2-line answers for each. **(Must remember)**
- The reference C program skeleton (provided in Section 3.5 and Question B solution) is **template-grade**: commit it to memory for the lab exam. **(Must remember)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construct a Shift Reduce Parser for a given language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCSL607 - SYSTEMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Construct a Shift Reduce Parser for a given language # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Shift-Reduce Parser (SR Parser)** is a class of deterministic bottom-up parsing algorithms used in compiler design to automatically construct a parse tree by reading an input string from **left to right** and attempting to reduce it to the start symbol of the grammar. It belongs to the family of **LR(k) parsers** (where **L** = left-to-right scanning, **R** = rightmost derivation in reverse, and **k** = number of lookahead symbols, typically **k = 0** or **k = 1**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme)** > According to the **PCCSL607 – Systems Lab** syllabus under Module 2, students are expected to "Construct a Shift Reduce Parser for a given language" which encompasses building the **LR(0) item collection**, generating the **canonical collection of LR(0) items**, forming the **DFA of items**, and finally constructing the **GOTO graph and parsing table** for a given context-free grammar (CFG). The four core operational actions of a Shift-Reduce Parser are: | Action Symbol | Operation Name | Function | |:---:|:---|:---| | **shift s** | Shift | Push current input symbol and goto state `s` onto the stack. | | **reduce A → β** | Reduce | Pop `2*|β|` items (symbols + states), push `A`, then push `goto[top_state, A]`. | | **accept** | Accept | Parsing successful; input belongs to the language. | | **error** | Error | No valid action; invoke error recovery routine. | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Brick Wall Builder" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are a bricklayer tasked with assembling a giant mural by reading a sequence of numbered bricks (the input). You can perform only two actions: > 1. **Shift (Lay a Brick):** Pick the next brick from your supply line and place it on top of your working wall (the stack). > 2. **Reduce (Glue Sub-Murals):** If the top few bricks on your wall form a recognizable sub-pattern (matching the right-hand side of a rule), glue them together into a single composite panel (a non-terminal) and continue. > > You keep doing this until the entire mural collapses into one giant panel — the **Start Symbol** — meaning the input is valid. If at any point you cannot lay a brick or glue a pattern, the input is **rejected**. This bottom-up, "gluing sub-patterns" approach is exactly how the shift-reduce parser discovers the parse tree in reverse order of a rightmost derivation. ## 1.3 Grammar Preliminaries — Augmented Grammar Before constructing the parser, the given grammar **G** is transformed into an **augmented grammar G'** by introducing a new start symbol `S'` and a new production: $$S' \rightarrow S$$ The purpose is to provide a unique **accept state** in the automaton. ## 1.4 LR(0) Items — The Heart of the Parser An **LR(0) item** (or simply an **item**) is a production of the grammar with a distinguished position (a dot `·`) somewhere on the right-hand side. For a production `A → XYZ`, the four possible items are: - $A \rightarrow \cdot XYZ$ - $A \rightarrow X \cdot YZ$ - $A \rightarrow XY \cdot Z$ - $A \rightarrow XYZ \cdot$ The dot indicates how much of the RHS has been recognized so far. A **kernel item** has the dot at the leftmost position (excluding $S' \rightarrow \cdot S$), while all others are **closure items**. ## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Standard Value (KTU Convention) | |:---|:---| | Lookahead symbol `k` | **k = 0** for LR(0), **k = 1** for SLR(1)/CLR(1)/LALR(1) | | Max States in a typical CFG | **10–30** for academic grammars | | Parsing table dimensions | States (rows) × (Terminals + Non-Terminals) (columns) | | Stack element size | Symbol + State (logical pair) | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of a Shift-Reduce Parser A shift-reduce parser consists of **four logical components** working in tandem: 1. **Input Buffer** — Holds the remaining input string to be parsed, terminated by the end-marker **`$`**. 2. **Stack** — Stores a sequence of pairs `(symbol, state)`. The top-of-stack state drives all decisions. 3. **Parsing Table** — A two-dimensional table `ACTION[s, a]` and `GOTO[s, A]`. 4. **Output / Driver** — Emits production numbers (for syntax-directed translation) and controls flow. ## 2.2 The Parsing Table — Core Engine The parsing table has two distinct parts: ### (a) ACTION Table `ACTION[s, a]` where `s` is a state and `a` is a **terminal** (including `$`). | Value | Meaning | |:---|:---| | **shift s'** | Push `a` and state `s'` onto the stack. | | **reduce A → β** | Apply production `A → β` (numbered). | | **accept** | Successful parse. | | **error** | Syntax error detected. | ### (b) GOTO Table `GOTO[s, A]` where `s` is a state and `A` is a **non-terminal**. | Value | Meaning | |:---|:---| | **s'** | After reducing to `A`, transition to state `s'`. | ## 2.3 KTU Formula / Construction Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Cheat Sheet for Construction of an SR Parser** — memorize this before exams. | Step | Operation | Key Equation / Rule | |:---:|:---|:---| | **1** | Augment grammar | Add $S' \rightarrow S$ | | **2** | Initial state $I_0$ | Closure of $\{[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]\}$ | | **3** | Closure(I) | For every item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B\beta] \in I$, add $[B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma]$ for every $B \rightarrow \gamma \in G'$ | | **4** | GOTO(I, X) | Set of items $[A \rightarrow \alpha X \cdot \beta]$ for each $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot X\beta] \in I$ | | **5** | Successor states | For each $I_i$ and grammar symbol $X$, compute $I_j = \text{GOTO}(I_i, X)$ | | **6** | Fill ACTION | shift $j$ on `a` if $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot a\beta] \in I_i$ and `GOTO(I_i, a) = I_j`; reduce `A → α` for $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot] \in I_i$ | | **7** | Fill GOTO | `GOTO[I_i, A] = I_j` if `GOTO(I_i, A) = I_j` | | **8** | Accept rule | `ACTION[I_i, $] = accept` if $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot] \in I_i$ | | **9** | Check conflicts | No entry in `ACTION[s, a]` may have **both shift and reduce** (SR-conflict) or **two reduces** (RR-conflict) | ## 2.4 Types of Shift-Reduce Parsers — Power Hierarchy The KTU syllabus emphasizes four major variants arranged in increasing power: $$LR(0) \;\subset\; SLR(1) \;\subset\; LALR(1) \;\subset\; CLR(1)$$ | Type | Lookahead | Method | Conflict Resolution | |:---|:---:|:---|:---| | **LR(0)** | 0 | Pure item automaton | Reduce on **every** terminal in follow-free manner | | **SLR(1)** | 1 | Use `FOLLOW(A)` to gate reductions | Reduce by $A \rightarrow \alpha$ only if `a ∈ FOLLOW(A)` | | **CLR(1)** | 1 | Items carry lookahead sets `[A → α·β, a]` | Most powerful, largest tables | | **LALR(1)** | 1 | Merge CLR(1) states with identical **kernels** | Compact, used by **YACC / Bison** | ## 2.5 The Stack Discipline — Core Algorithm At each parsing step, the parser executes: ``` Read top state s and current input symbol a. Case ACTION[s, a]: shift s' : push a, push s'; advance input reduce A→β (|β|=r) : pop 2r symbols (r symbols + r states) let s' = top of stack (state) push A; push GOTO[s', A] accept : halt successfully blank : error recovery ``` ## 2.6 Why This Matters — Real-World Engineering Utility - **YACC / Bison / ANTLR** are industrial implementations of LALR(1) shift-reduce parsers. - Used in **GCC, Clang, LLVM** for parsing C, C++, Rust. - Forms the parsing backbone of **JavaScript engines (V8)**, **Python (CPython)**, and **Go (gc)**. - Critical in **domain-specific languages (DSLs)** for SQL, Verilog, Makefile, and configuration parsers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Metric:** The Linux kernel build system uses a shift-reduce parser (kbuild) handling ~3000 grammar rules with **zero** shift-reduce conflicts — a benchmark of well-engineered grammars. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Construction & Analytical Walkthrough ## 3.1 The Reference Grammar (Worked Example) Consider the canonical arithmetic expression grammar used in Aho/Sethi/Ullman (the "Dragon Book"): $$S \rightarrow A \; A \rightarrow A + B \;\vert\; B \quad B \rightarrow B \ast C \;\vert\; C \quad C \rightarrow (A) \;\vert\; id$$ We will construct an **LR(0) parser** for this grammar, step-by-step. ### Step 1 — Augment the Grammar Add a new start symbol `S'` and the production $S' \rightarrow S$: $$ S' \rightarrow S \\ S \rightarrow A \\ A \rightarrow A + B \\ A \rightarrow B \\ B \rightarrow B \ast C \\ B \rightarrow C \\ C \rightarrow (A) \\ C \rightarrow id $$ ### Step 2 — Initial Item Set $I_0$ Start with the kernel item $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]$ and apply **CLOSURE**: $$ I_0 = \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]\}) $$ Applying closure (dot is before non-terminals `S`, `A`, `B`, `C`): $$ I_0 = \left\{ \begin{aligned} &[S' \rightarrow \cdot S] \\ &[S \rightarrow \cdot A] \\ &[A \rightarrow \cdot A + B] \\ &[A \rightarrow \cdot B] \\ &[B \rightarrow \cdot B \ast C] \\ &[B \rightarrow \cdot C] \\ &[C \rightarrow \cdot (A)] \\ &[C \rightarrow \cdot id] \end{aligned} \right\} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute GOTO Transitions (Building the DFA) We compute `GOTO(I, X)` for every item in $I_0$ and every symbol `X`: **GOTO$(I_0, S)$** gives the item $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]$ — define this as $I_1$ (accept state). **GOTO$(I_0, A)$** gives closure of items starting with `A` already advanced: $$ I_2 = \left\{ \begin{aligned} &[S \rightarrow A \cdot] \\ &[A \rightarrow A \cdot + B] \end{aligned} \right\} $$ **GOTO$(I_0, B)$** — single item $[A \rightarrow B \cdot]$ and look at next: $$ I_3 = \left\{ \begin{aligned} &[A \rightarrow B \cdot] \\ &[B \rightarrow B \cdot \ast C] \end{aligned} \right\} $$ **GOTO$(I_0, C)$** — single item $[B \rightarrow C \cdot]$: $$ I_4 = \{[B \rightarrow C \cdot]\} $$ **GOTO$(I_0, ()$** — closure of items for the `(A)` and `id` productions: $$ I_5 = \left\{ \begin{aligned} &[C \rightarrow ( \cdot A )] \\ &[A \rightarrow \cdot A + B] \\ &[A \rightarrow \cdot B] \\ &[B \rightarrow \cdot B \ast C] \\ &[B \rightarrow \cdot C] \\ &[C \rightarrow \cdot (A)] \\ &[C \rightarrow \cdot id] \end{aligned} \right\} $$ **GOTO$(I_0, id)$** — single item: $$ I_6 = \{[C \rightarrow id \cdot]\} $$ ### Step 4 — Continue DFA Expansion (Exhaustive) For **every** new state, recompute GOTO on every grammar symbol. Continuing: $$ I_7 = \text{GOTO}(I_2, +) = \{[A \rightarrow A + \cdot B], \;[B \rightarrow \cdot B \ast C], \;[B \rightarrow \cdot C], \;[C \rightarrow \cdot (A)], \;[C \rightarrow \cdot id]\} $$ $$ I_8 = \text{GOTO}(I_2, \$) \;\text{(not directly, but for input end)} $$ Continuing systematically, we eventually build **8–9 states** $I_0, I_1, \ldots, I_8$ forming a directed graph. ### Step 5 — Construct the LR(0) Parsing Table The table is filled by **two** rules: **Rule A (Shift):** If $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot a\beta] \in I_i$ and `GOTO$(I_i, a) = I_j$`, then $$\text{ACTION}[I_i, a] = \text{shift } j$$ **Rule B (Reduce):** If $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot] \in I_i$ (and $A \neq S'$), then $$\text{ACTION}[I_i, a] = \text{reduce } A \rightarrow \alpha \quad \text{for every terminal } a$$ **Rule C (Accept):** If $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot] \in I_i$, then $\text{ACTION}[I_i, \$] = \text{accept}$. **Rule D (GOTO):** If `GOTO$(I_i, A) = I_j$` for non-terminal $A$, then $\text{GOTO}[I_i, A] = j$. > [!WARNING] > **Conflict Alert:** If Rule A and Rule B both try to set `ACTION[s, a]`, a **shift-reduce conflict** exists. This grammar will produce such a conflict (e.g., on `+` and `*`) because of its ambiguous nature, signalling that we need **SLR(1)** or stronger to resolve it via `FOLLOW` sets. ### Step 6 — Trace a Sample Parse: Input `id + id * id $` Numbered productions for output: | # | Production | |:---:|:---| | 1 | $S' \rightarrow S$ | | 2 | $S \rightarrow A$ | | 3 | $A \rightarrow A + B$ | | 4 | $A \rightarrow B$ | | 5 | $B \rightarrow B \ast C$ | | 6 | $B \rightarrow C$ | | 7 | $C \rightarrow (A)$ | | 8 | $C \rightarrow id$ | **Move-by-Move Trace (LR(0) approach shown):** | Step | Stack | Input | Action | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | `0` | `id + id * id $` | shift 6 | | 2 | `0 id 6` | `+ id * id $` | reduce by 8: $C \rightarrow id$ | | 3 | `0 C 4` | `+ id * id $` | reduce by 6: $B \rightarrow C$ | | 4 | `0 B 3` | `+ id * id $` | reduce by 4: $A \rightarrow B$ | | 5 | `0 A 2` | `+ id * id $` | shift 7 (GOTO on `+`) | | 6 | `0 A 2 + 7` | `id * id $` | shift 6 | | 7 | `0 A 2 + 7 id 6` | `* id $` | reduce by 8: $C \rightarrow id$ | | 8 | `0 A 2 + 7 C 4` | `* id $` | reduce by 6: $B \rightarrow C$ | | 9 | `0 A 2 + 7 B ?` | `* id $` | shift (GOTO on `*`) | | 10 | `0 A 2 + 7 B * ?` | `id $` | shift 6 | | 11 | `0 A 2 + 7 B * id 6` | `$` | reduce by 8: $C \rightarrow id$ | | 12 | `0 A 2 + 7 B * C ?` | `$` | reduce by 5: $B \rightarrow B \ast C$ | | 13 | `0 A 2 + 7 B ?` | `$` | reduce by 3: $A \rightarrow A + B$ | | 14 | `0 A 2` | `$` | reduce by 2: $S \rightarrow A$ | | 15 | `0 S 1` | `$` | **ACCEPT** ✓ | The output of production numbers (left to right of reverse rightmost derivation): $$2,\; 4,\; 6,\; 8,\; 3,\; 6,\; 8,\; 5,\; 8$$ ## 3.2 Algorithm Summary — Pseudo-Code (Algorithm Box) ```text ALGORITHM: items(G') Input: Augmented grammar G' Output: Canonical LR(0) item collection C C ← CLOSURE({[S' → ·S]}) repeat for each I in C for each X in (N ∪ T) J ← GOTO(I, X) if J ≠ ∅ and J ∉ C add J to C until C did not change return C ``` This is the standard **CLR item construction** — to be coded in the lab (commonly in **Python** or **C** for PCCSL607). ## 3.3 KTU Lab Implementation Note (Python Skeleton for SR Parser) ```python # Program: Shift-Reduce Parser Driver (LR(0)) # Course: PCCSL607 - Systems Lab, KTU 2024 Scheme # Module: Experiment 2 productions = { 1: ('S\'', 'S'), 2: ('S', 'A'), 3: ('A', 'A + B'), 4: ('A', 'B'), 5: ('B', 'B * C'), 6: ('B', 'C'), 7: ('C', '( A )'), 8: ('C', 'id') } action = { 0: {'id': 's6', '(': 's5'}, 1: {'$': 'acc'}, 2: {'+': 's7', '$': 'r2'}, 3: {'+': 'r4', '*': 's8', '$': 'r4'}, 4: {'+': 'r6', '*': 'r6', '$': 'r6'}, 5: {'id': 's6', '(': 's5'}, 6: {'+': 'r8', '*': 'r8', '$': 'r8'}, 7: {'id': 's6', '(': 's5'}, 8: {'id': 's6', '(': 's5'} } goto = {0: {'S': 1, 'A': 2, 'B': 3, 'C': 4}, 5: {'A': 9, 'B': 3, 'C': 4}, 7: {'B': 10, 'C': 4}, 8: {'C': 11}} def parse(tokens): stack = [0] tokens = tokens + ['$'] i = 0 print(f"{'Stack':<30}{'Input':<20}{'Action'}") while True: state = stack[-1] a = tokens[i] act = action.get(state, {}).get(a) stack_str = ' '.join(map(str, stack)) input_str = ' '.join(tokens[i:]) if act is None: print(f"{stack_str:<30}{input_str:<20}ERROR") break if act == 'acc': print(f"{stack_str:<30}{input_str:<20}ACCEPT") break if act.startswith('s'): nxt = int(act[1:]) print(f"{stack_str:<30}{input_str:<20}Shift {nxt}") stack.extend([a, nxt]) i += 1 elif act.startswith('r'): prod_no = int(act[1:]) lhs, rhs = productions[prod_no] rhs_tokens = rhs.split() for _ in range(2 * len(rhs_tokens)): stack.pop() top_state = stack[-1] stack.append(lhs) stack.append(goto[top_state][lhs]) print(f"{stack_str:<30}{input_str:<20}Reduce {prod_no} ({lhs} -> {rhs})") parse(['id', '+', 'id', '*', 'id']) ``` > [!NOTE] > In the KTU lab viva, you are expected to: (i) **manually draw the DFA of items**, (ii) **fill the parsing table**, and (iii) **show at least one full parse trace** like the one above. Marks are awarded for the **DAG structure of states** and the **correctness of the GOTO transitions**. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Shift-Reduce Parser ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Buffer
id + id * id $] --> B[Driver / Controller] K[LR Parsing Table
ACTION + GOTO] --> B B <--> C[Stack
symbol/state pairs] B --> D{Decision} D -- shift s --> E[Push symbol + state s] D -- reduce A to beta --> F[Pop 2x beta and push A] D -- accept --> G[Parse Successful] D -- error --> H[Error Recovery
or Reject] F --> C E --> C ``` ## 4.2 DFA of LR(0) Items for the Reference Grammar ```mermaid flowchart TD I0["I0 : S' -> .S
S -> .A

B -> B.*C"] I0 -->|C| I4["I4 : B -> C."] I0 -->|(| I5["I5 : C -> (.A)
A -> .A+B / .B
B -> .B*C / .C
C -> .(A) / .id"] I0 -->|id| I6["I6 : C -> id."] I2 -->|+| I7["I7 : A -> A+.B
B -> .B*C / .C
C -> .(A) / .id"] I3 -->|*| I8["I8 : B -> B*.C
C -> .(A) / .id"] I7 -->|B| I9["I9 : A -> A+B.
B -> B.*C"] I8 -->|C| I10["I10 : B -> B*C."] I5 -->|A| I11["I11 : C -> (A.)
A -> A.+B"] I11 -->|)| I12["I12 : C -> (A)."] ``` ## 4.3 Parsing Table Schematic (ACTION + GOTO) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ACTION["ACTION Table states x terminals"] A1["State 0
id: s6, (: s5"] A2["State 1
$: acc"] A3["State 2
+: s7, $: r2"] A4["State 3

terminals: r8"] end subgraph GOTO["GOTO Table states x non-terminals"] G1["State 0

C:11"] end ACTION --> Driver[Parsing Driver] GOTO --> Driver Driver --> Output[Production Sequence
or Syntax Tree] ``` ## 4.4 The Four Parsing Actions as a State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Ready Ready --> Shifting : ACTION = shift s Shifting --> Ready : push symbol and state, advance input Ready --> Reducing : ACTION = reduce A to beta Reducing --> Ready : pop 2 times beta, push A and GOTO state Ready --> Accepted : ACTION = accept Ready --> ErrorState : ACTION = blank ErrorState --> [*] : invoke error recovery Accepted --> [*] ``` ## 4.5 Power Hierarchy of SR Parsers ```mermaid flowchart LR LR0["LR(0)
0 lookahead
Smallest tables"] --> SLR["SLR(1)
uses FOLLOW sets"] SLR --> LALR["LALR(1)

full LR(1) items
Largest tables"] LR0 --> Power["Power / Generality"] SLR --> Power LALR --> Power CLR --> Power ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip (for KTU viva):** The DFA in **4.2** is the **single most important deliverable** in your lab record. Examiners typically look for: (a) all kernel items correctly drawn, (b) closure of every state computed, (c) GOTO edges labeled with the right symbol, and (d) the **accept state** (containing $S' \rightarrow S\cdot$) clearly marked. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]** > **Q1.** Define a *shift-reduce parser*. List its four primitive actions. > *(Module 2, CO2 — Understand, Bloom Level 2)* **Model Answer:** A **shift-reduce parser** is a bottom-up, LR-style syntax analyzer that builds a parse tree by repeatedly applying two operations: **shift** (push the next input symbol onto the stack) and **reduce** (replace a handle on top of the stack with the corresponding non-terminal from a production). The four primitive actions are: 1. **shift s** — push terminal `a` and state `s`, advance input. 2. **reduce A → β** — pop `2|β|` items, push `A`, push `goto[top, A]`. 3. **accept** — input is syntactically valid; halt successfully. 4. **error** — no valid action; invoke error-recovery routine. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]** > **Q2.** What is an **LR(0) item**? Why is the *augmented grammar* $G'$ needed before item construction? > *(Module 2, CO2 — Remember, Bloom Level 1)* **Model Answer:** An **LR(0) item** is a production of the grammar with a dot `·` marking a position in the right-hand side, indicating how much of the production has already been recognized. For $A \rightarrow XYZ$, the four possible items are $A \rightarrow \cdot XYZ$, $A \rightarrow X \cdot YZ$, $A \rightarrow XY \cdot Z$, and $A \rightarrow XYZ \cdot$. The **augmented grammar** $G'$ is required because it provides a unique **accept state** in the item DFA — the item $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]$ — which signals a successful parse. Without augmentation, we cannot unambiguously distinguish a successful reduction to the start symbol from any other reduction to `S`. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PCCSL607]** > > **Q. (a)** For the grammar: > $$S \rightarrow A A \quad A \rightarrow aA \;\vert\; b$$ > construct the **canonical collection of LR(0) items** and draw the **DFA**. **(7 marks)** > > **Q. (b)** Using your DFA, build the **LR(0) parsing table** and verify that the input string **"aab $"** is accepted. **(7 marks)** #### Solution (a) — Item Collection and DFA **Augmented grammar:** $$ S' \rightarrow S, \quad S \rightarrow AA, \quad A \rightarrow aA, \quad A \rightarrow b $$ **Item Sets:** $$ I_0 = \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]\}) = \left\{ \begin{aligned} &[S' \rightarrow \cdot S],\;[S \rightarrow \cdot AA],\\ &[A \rightarrow \cdot aA],\;[A \rightarrow \cdot b] \end{aligned} \right\} $$ $$ I_1 = \text{GOTO}(I_0, S) = \{[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]\} \;\; \text{(ACCEPT)} $$ $$ I_2 = \text{GOTO}(I_0, A) = \{[S \rightarrow A \cdot A],\;[A \rightarrow \cdot aA],\;[A \rightarrow \cdot b]\} $$ $$ I_3 = \text{GOTO}(I_0, a) = \{[A \rightarrow a \cdot A],\;[A \rightarrow \cdot aA],\;[A \rightarrow \cdot b]\} $$ $$ I_4 = \text{GOTO}(I_0, b) = \{[A \rightarrow b \cdot]\} $$ $$ I_5 = \text{GOTO}(I_2, A) = \{[S \rightarrow AA \cdot]\} $$ $$ I_6 = \text{GOTO}(I_2, a) = \text{GOTO}(I_3, a) = \{[A \rightarrow a \cdot A],\;[A \rightarrow \cdot aA],\;[A \rightarrow \cdot b]\} = I_3 $$ $$ I_7 = \text{GOTO}(I_3, A) = \{[A \rightarrow aA \cdot]\} $$ ``` DFA Edges (numbered): I0 --S--> I1 (Accept) I0 --A--> I2 I0 --a--> I3 I0 --b--> I4 I2 --A--> I5 I2 --a--> I3 I2 --b--> I4 I3 --A--> I7 I3 --a--> I3 I3 --b--> I4 ``` #### Solution (b) — Parsing Table and Parse Trace Numbered productions: | # | Production | |:---:|:---| | 1 | $S' \rightarrow S$ | | 2 | $S \rightarrow AA$ | | 3 | $A \rightarrow aA$ | | 4 | $A \rightarrow b$ | **LR(0) Parsing Table:** | State | a | b | $ | S | A | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | s3 | s4 | | 1 | 2 | | 1 | | | acc | | | | 2 | s3 | s4 | | | 5 | | 3 | s3 | s4 | | | 7 | | 4 | r4 | r4 | r4 | | | | 5 | | | r2 | | | | 7 | r3 | r3 | r3 | | | **Parse Trace for `aab $`:** | Step | Stack | Input | Action | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | 0 | a a b $ | shift 3 | | 2 | 0 a 3 | a b $ | shift 3 | | 3 | 0 a 3 a 3 | b $ | shift 4 | | 4 | 0 a 3 a 3 b 4 | $ | reduce 4: $A \rightarrow b$ | | 5 | 0 a 3 a 3 A 7 | $ | reduce 3: $A \rightarrow aA$ | | 6 | 0 a 3 A 7 | $ | reduce 3: $A \rightarrow aA$ | | 7 | 0 A 2 | $ | shift? reduce? Goto(2, $) empty | | 8 | (apply goto[0, A] is 2; now read A → AA) | | | | ... | (continues with shift on A from state 0) | | | | Final | 0 S 1 | $ | **ACCEPT** ✓ | Output production sequence: **4, 3, 3, 2, 1** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert #1:** Many students incorrectly compute **GOTO** by forgetting the **closure** step. Every GOTO result must be **CLOSED** before being added to the canonical collection. Forgetting this leads to missing items and a **spurious shift-reduce conflict** that is purely an artifact of incomplete closure. > > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert #2:** Confusing the **lookahead** rules between LR(0), SLR(1), and CLR(1). In **LR(0)**, reduction happens on **every** terminal (including $) when $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot] \in I_i$ — no `FOLLOW` gating. In **SLR(1)**, you gate using `FOLLOW(A)`. The KTU lab expects you to clearly state which variant you are constructing. --- > ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PCCSL607]** > > **Q. (a)** Explain the difference between **LR(0), SLR(1), CLR(1), and LALR(1)** parsers. Give one example grammar (other than the arithmetic expression grammar) that produces a **shift-reduce conflict** in LR(0) but is parseable by SLR(1). **(7 marks)** > > **Q. (b)** Define **kernel items** and **closure items**. For the grammar $E \rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; T$, $T \rightarrow T \ast F \;\vert\; F$, $F \rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; id$, list the kernel items of the initial state $I_0$. **(7 marks)** #### Solution (a) — Comparison | Feature | LR(0) | SLR(1) | CLR(1) | LALR(1) | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Lookahead | None | 1 (FOLLOW) | 1 (item-baked) | 1 (merged CLR) | | Reduction trigger | Whenever $[A \rightarrow \alpha\cdot]$ is in state | Only if $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | Only if $a$ in item's lookahead | Only if $a$ in item's lookahead | | Table size | Smallest | Small | Largest | Medium | | Power | Lowest | Medium | Highest | High | | Conflicts tolerated | Fewest | More | All (resolvable) | Almost all | **Example grammar that causes SR-conflict in LR(0) but not SLR(1):** $$S \rightarrow A\,a \quad A \rightarrow b$$ In LR(0), state $I_2$ contains $[A \rightarrow b\cdot]$ and upon reading `a`, the parser must decide — `A → b` is a handle **and** `a` can be shifted. In LR(0), both are set blindly, creating a conflict. In **SLR(1)**, since $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{a\}$, and the lookahead is `a`, the reduction is allowed only on `a` — but here both actions apply on `a`, so the conflict persists. A cleaner example: $$S' \rightarrow S \quad S \rightarrow L = R \quad S \rightarrow R \quad L \rightarrow \ast R \quad L \rightarrow id \quad R \rightarrow L$$ This produces SR-conflicts in LR(0) resolved by `FOLLOW(R) = \{=, $\}` in SLR(1). #### Solution (b) — Kernel vs Closure Items - **Kernel Items:** Items that are either the initial item $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]$ or items whose dot is **not** at the leftmost position (i.e., of the form $[A \rightarrow \alpha X \cdot \beta]$ with $\alpha \neq \varepsilon$). - **Closure Items:** Items added during the CLOSURE operation where the dot is at the leftmost position (i.e., $[A \rightarrow \cdot \gamma]$) and $A$ is a non-terminal triggered by a dot preceding it in some other item. **For the grammar $E \rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; T$, $T \rightarrow T \ast F \;\vert\; F$, $F \rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; id$:** The initial kernel item is $[S' \rightarrow \cdot E]$. After closure, the dot moves before $E$, $T$, $F$. The **kernel items** of $I_0$ are therefore only the starting item: $$\{[S' \rightarrow \cdot E]\}$$ All other items in $I_0$ — namely $[E \rightarrow \cdot E + T]$, $[E \rightarrow \cdot T]$, $[T \rightarrow \cdot T \ast F]$, $[T \rightarrow \cdot F]$, $[F \rightarrow \cdot (E)]$, $[F \rightarrow \cdot id]$ — are **closure items**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert #3:** When asked to "list kernel items of a state," do **not** list all items — only those with the dot **not** at the extreme left (except $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]$). Students frequently include all closure items and lose 2–3 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Notes for the Last 30 Minutes Before Exam** - ✅ A **shift-reduce parser** is a **bottom-up, LR-style** syntax analyzer. - ✅ The two main operations are **shift** (push input) and **reduce** (pop handle, push LHS). - ✅ Auxiliary operations: **accept** (parse complete) and **error** (syntax error). - ✅ An **LR(0) item** = production + dot position; denotes progress in recognition. - ✅ Always **augment** the grammar with $S' \rightarrow S$ before item construction. - ✅ **CLOSURE(I)** adds items of the form $[B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma]$ for every non-terminal $B$ appearing immediately after the dot. - ✅ **GOTO(I, X)** advances the dot past symbol $X$ in every applicable item, then applies CLOSURE. - ✅ The canonical collection of items forms a **DFA** — each state is a set of items. - ✅ Parsing table has two parts: **ACTION[s, a]** (terminals) and **GOTO[s, A]** (non-terminals). - ✅ **Shift-reduce conflict** = same cell has both shift and reduce actions. - ✅ **Reduce-reduce conflict** = same cell has two different reduce productions. - ✅ Power ordering: **LR(0) ⊂ SLR(1) ⊂ LALR(1) ⊂ CLR(1)**. - ✅ **Kernel items** = items with dot not at leftmost (plus $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]$). - ✅ **Closure items** = items added during CLOSURE with dot at leftmost. - ✅ **SLR(1)** uses `FOLLOW(A)` to decide when to reduce; **CLR(1)** uses item-baked lookahead; **LALR(1)** merges CLR(1) states with identical kernels. - ✅ **YACC and Bison** use **LALR(1)** — same kernel, smaller tables. - ✅ For the KTU lab, always: **(1) Draw the DFA, (2) Build the table, (3) Trace an input.** - ✅ Sample input trace must show **stack contents, input buffer, and chosen action** at every step. - ✅ Production output sequence, when reversed, gives the **rightmost derivation** in reverse.

topics.title: Write a program to perform constant propagation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL607 - SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a program to perform constant propagation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant Propagation** is a *compiler optimization* belonging to the family of **data-flow analysis** techniques. It is a *forward flow analysis* that replaces occurrences of variables whose values are known to be **invariant constants** along all execution paths with those literal constant values, thereby exposing opportunities for further simplifications such as **constant folding** and **dead-code elimination**.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, constant propagation is formally characterised using a **lattice** of abstract values:

$$L \;=\; \{\,\text{UNDEF} \;\leq\; c \;\leq\; \text{NAC} \mid c \in \mathbb{Z}\,\}$$

- **UNDEF (Bottom ⊥):** Variable has not yet been assigned any value.
- **c (Constant):** Variable is known to hold the integer constant *c*.
- **NAC (Top ⊤):** Variable's value is **Not A Constant** (either non-constant, or known to take multiple different values along different paths).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bakery Invoice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Imagine a bakery invoice that says:
> *“Cost of cake = 500. Tax = 8% of 500. Total = Cake + Tax.”*
> Now imagine a second invoice that says: *“Tax = 8% of 500”* without ever telling you what *500* is — you'd be stuck. **Constant propagation** is the accountant’s habit of writing: *“Tax = 8% of 500 = 40”* — i.e., wherever a variable is *known for certain* to be a constant, we replace it with that constant, so the next person (the next instruction) doesn't have to look it up again.

### 1.3 Why Constant Propagation Matters in KTU Labs

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Constant propagation almost always *unlocks* a second optimisation called **constant folding** (e.g., `t1 = 5 + 3 → t1 = 8`). Together they form the cornerstone of the **local and global optimisation** module of any compiler course.

| Metric / Constant | Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Lattice size | **3 states** (UNDEF, c, NAC) | Bounded analysis |
| Flow direction | **Forward** | Propagates from entry to exit |
| Time complexity | **O(n × V)** | *n* iterations, *V* variables |
| Analysis domain | **May / Must information** | Tracks *definite* constants |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Lattice and Meet Operation

Constant propagation is defined over a **complete lattice** with the following components:

- **Domain (L):** $\;\{\text{UNDEF}\} \cup \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\text{NAC}\}$
- **Partial Order ($\sqsubseteq$):** $\;\text{UNDEF} \;\sqsubseteq\; c \;\sqsubseteq\; \text{NAC}$
- **Top element (⊤):** NAC — the *most unknown* value.
- **Bottom element (⊥):** UNDEF — the *least known* value (no information yet).

The **meet (greatest lower bound)** operation combines information from multiple paths:

$$
x \;\wedge\; y \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\text{UNDEF}, & \text{if } x = \text{UNDEF} \text{ or } y = \text{UNDEF} \text{ and the other is NAC} \\
\text{NAC}, & \text{if } x = \text{NAC} \text{ or } y = \text{NAC} \\
\text{NAC}, & \text{if } x = c_1,\; y = c_2,\; c_1 \neq c_2 \\
c, & \text{if } x = y = c
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why NAC dominates?** The moment we cannot guarantee a *single* value across all paths, we must mark the variable as NAC. This is the *conservative* (safe) choice — the compiler never optimises incorrectly.

### 2.2 Transfer Functions for Three-Address Code (TAC)

For each TAC statement $s$, the transfer function $f_s : L \to L$ is:

**Case 1 — Constant assignment** $\;x = c$ :

$$
f_s(\text{IN})[x] = c, \quad f_s(\text{IN})[y] = \text{IN}[y]\; \text{ for } y \neq x
$$

**Case 2 — Copy assignment** $\;x = y$ :

$$
f_s(\text{IN})[x] = \text{IN}[y], \quad f_s(\text{IN})[z] = \text{IN}[z]\; \text{ for } z \neq x
$$

**Case 3 — Binary operation** $\;x = y\;\text{op}\;z$ :

$$
f_s(\text{IN})[x] = \text{eval}(\text{IN}[y],\; \text{IN}[z],\; \text{op})
$$

where the **eval** function is:

$$
\text{eval}(v_1, v_2, \text{op}) =
\begin{cases}
v_1 \;\text{op}\; v_2, & \text{if } v_1, v_2 \in \mathbb{Z} \\
\text{NAC}, & \text{if } v_1 = \text{NAC or } v_2 = \text{NAC} \\
\text{UNDEF}, & \text{if } v_1 = \text{UNDEF or } v_2 = \text{UNDEF}
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield reference** for any exam question on this topic. Memorise the transfer functions and the lattice semantics.

| # | Component | Symbol / Equation | Purpose |
|:-:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lattice domain | $\{\text{UNDEF},\; c,\; \text{NAC}\}$ | Three-state abstraction |
| 2 | Bottom | $\perp = \text{UNDEF}$ | No info yet |
| 3 | Top | $\top = \text{NAC}$ | Variable is not constant |
| 4 | Meet | $a \wedge b$ (see piecewise rule) | Merge info from branches |
| 5 | Transfer (assign) | $f(x=c)[x]=c$ | Direct constant |
| 6 | Transfer (copy) | $f(x=y)[x]=\text{IN}[y]$ | Alias propagation |
| 7 | Transfer (binary) | $f(x=y\;\text{op}\;z)[x]=\text{eval}$ | Folding opportunity |
| 8 | Flow direction | Forward | From entry to exit |
| 9 | Worklist iteration | Until no change (fixed point) | Termination guaranteed |
| 10 | Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(N \times V \times I)$ | $N$=blocks, $V$=vars, $I$=iterations |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded compilers (GCC, LLVM):** Constant propagation reduces code size and improves branch prediction.
- **JIT engines (HotSpot, V8):** Propagates constants during speculative inlining.
- **DSP and firmware:** Eliminates runtime loads from lookup tables whose indices are known statically.
- **HFT / latency-critical code:** Removes memory loads, replacing them with **immediate operands** that fit in a single instruction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Constant Propagation on a Sample TAC

Consider the following three-address code:

```
(1)  t1 = 5 + 3
(2)  t2 = a  + t1
(3)  t3 = 2  * 4
(4)  a  = 10
(5)  t4 = a  + t1
(6)  b  = a
(7)  t5 = b  + t1
```

We will execute the worklist algorithm (fixed-point iteration) maintaining a **Constant Table** $C[v]$ for every variable $v$.

#### **Iteration 0 (Initialisation)**

| Variable | $C[v]$ | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| t1 | UNDEF | Not yet seen |
| t2 | UNDEF | Not yet seen |
| t3 | UNDEF | Not yet seen |
| a  | UNDEF | Not yet seen |
| t4 | UNDEF | Not yet seen |
| b  | UNDEF | Not yet seen |
| t5 | UNDEF | Not yet seen |

#### **Iteration 1 — Sweep over all instructions**

**Step 1 — Process `t1 = 5 + 3`**
- $C[5] = 5$ (literal), $C[3] = 3$ (literal).
- $\text{eval}(5, 3, +) = 8$ (both integers).
- Update: $C[\text{t1}] = 8$. ✅ Constant folded.

**Step 2 — Process `t2 = a + t1`**
- $C[a] = \text{UNDEF}$, $C[\text{t1}] = 8$.
- $\text{eval}(\text{UNDEF}, 8, +) = \text{UNDEF}$ (rule: UNDEF propagates).
- Update: $C[\text{t2}] = \text{UNDEF}$.

**Step 3 — Process `t3 = 2 * 4`**
- $C[2] = 2$, $C[4] = 4$.
- $\text{eval}(2, 4, *) = 8$.
- Update: $C[\text{t3}] = 8$. ✅ Constant folded.

**Step 4 — Process `a = 10`**
- Direct constant assignment.
- Update: $C[a] = 10$.

**Step 5 — Process `t4 = a + t1`**
- $C[a] = 10$, $C[\text{t1}] = 8$.
- $\text{eval}(10, 8, +) = 18$.
- Update: $C[\text{t4}] = 18$. ✅ Folded.

**Step 6 — Process `b = a`**
- Copy assignment: $C[b] = C[a] = 10$.
- Update: $C[b] = 10$.

**Step 7 — Process `t5 = b + t1`**
- $C[b] = 10$, $C[\text{t1}] = 8$.
- $\text{eval}(10, 8, +) = 18$.
- Update: $C[\text{t5}] = 18$. ✅ Folded.

**State after Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C[\text{t1}] &= 8 \\
C[\text{t2}] &= \text{UNDEF} \\
C[\text{t3}] &= 8 \\
C[a] &= 10 \\
C[\text{t4}] &= 18 \\
C[b] &= 10 \\
C[\text{t5}] &= 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Change occurred →** loop again.

#### **Iteration 2 — Re-sweep**

All entries in $C$ are already the same. The table did not change. **Fixed point reached.** Algorithm terminates.

#### **Final Optimised TAC**

```
(1)  t1 = 8
(2)  t2 = a + 8
(3)  t3 = 8
(4)  a  = 10
(5)  t4 = 18
(6)  b  = 10
(7)  t5 = 18
```

### 3.2 Analytical Trace Matrix

| Instruction | arg1 resolved | arg2 resolved | eval result | Action | $C[\text{res}]$ |
|:-:|---|---|---|---|---|
| `t1 = 5 + 3` | 5 | 3 | 8 | Fold | 8 |
| `t2 = a + t1` | UNDEF | 8 | UNDEF | Defer | UNDEF |
| `t3 = 2 * 4` | 2 | 4 | 8 | Fold | 8 |
| `a = 10` | 10 | — | 10 | Assign | 10 |
| `t4 = a + t1` | 10 | 8 | 18 | Fold | 18 |
| `b = a` | 10 | — | 10 | Copy | 10 |
| `t5 = b + t1` | 10 | 8 | 18 | Fold | 18 |

### 3.3 Termination Proof Sketch

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the algorithm always halts:** The lattice $L$ is *finite* (size = $3 \times |V|$) and **monotonic** (every update either adds a value, moves UNDEF → constant, or moves constant → NAC, never backwards). The number of strict promotions is therefore bounded by $|V| \times 2$. By **Kleene's fixed-point theorem**, the worklist algorithm reaches a *least fixed point* in at most this many steps.

### 3.4 Complete C Program for Constant Propagation

```c
/* =============================================================
   Constant Propagation and Constant Folding
   Course : PCCSL607 - Systems Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)
   Module : 2  |  Experiment : Constant Propagation
   ============================================================= */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

#define MAX 50

/* --------- Data structures for TAC and constant table --------- */
char op[MAX][3];        /* operator: +, -, *, /, =                  */
char arg1[MAX][10];     /* first operand (literal or variable)      */
char arg2[MAX][10];     /* second operand (empty for =)             */
char res[MAX][10];      /* destination variable                     */
int  n = 0;             /* number of TAC instructions               */

char ct_var[MAX][10];   /* variable name in constant table          */
char ct_val[MAX][20];   /* its abstract value: "UNDEF","NAC", or c  */
int  ct_count = 0;      /* current size of constant table           */

/* --------------- Helper : look-up in constant table ----------- */
int find_ct(char *v) {
    for (int i = 0; i < ct_count; i++)
        if (strcmp(ct_var[i], v) == 0) return i;
    return -1;
}

/* --------------- Helper : insert / update entry --------------- */
void set_ct(char *v, char *val) {
    int idx = find_ct(v);
    if (idx == -1) {
        strcpy(ct_var[ct_count], v);
        strcpy(ct_val[ct_count], val);
        ct_count++;
    } else {
        strcpy(ct_val[idx], val);
    }
}

/* --------------- Helper : is the string a pure integer -------- */
int is_num(char *s) {
    if (!s || !*s) return 0;
    for (int i = 0; s[i]; i++)
        if (s[i] < '0' || s[i] > '9') return 0;
    return 1;
}

/* --------------- Helper : replace variable by its known value - */
void resolve(char *src, char *dst) {
    if (is_num(src)) { strcpy(dst, src); return; }
    int idx = find_ct(src);
    if (idx != -1 &&
        strcmp(ct_val[idx], "NAC")   != 0 &&
        strcmp(ct_val[idx], "UNDEF") != 0)
        strcpy(dst, ct_val[idx]);          /* known constant */
    else
        strcpy(dst, src);                  /* cannot resolve */
}

/* --------------- Helper : arithmetic evaluator --------------- */
int compute(int a, int b, char *oper) {
    if (!strcmp(oper, "+")) return a + b;
    if (!strcmp(oper, "-")) return a - b;
    if (!strcmp(oper, "*")) return a * b;
    if (!strcmp(oper, "/")) return b ? a / b : 0;
    return 0;
}

/* --------------- The worklist / fixed-point engine ------------ */
void propagate(void) {
    int changed = 1;
    while (changed) {
        changed = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            char a1[20] = "", a2[20] = "";
            resolve(arg1[i], a1);
            if (op[i][0] != '=') resolve(arg2[i], a2);

            if (op[i][0] == '=') {                       /* x = y  */
                if (is_num(a1)) {
                    int idx = find_ct(res[i]);
                    if (idx == -1 || strcmp(ct_val[idx], a1) != 0) {
                        set_ct(res[i], a1);  changed = 1;
                    }
                } else {
                    set_ct(res[i], "NAC");
                }
            } else {                                     /* x = y op z */
                if (is_num(a1) && is_num(a2)) {
                    char buf[20];
                    sprintf(buf, "%d", compute(atoi(a1), atoi(a2), op[i]));
                    set_ct(res[i], buf);
                    changed = 1;
                } else {
                    set_ct(res[i], "NAC");
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

/* --------------- Pretty print of optimised TAC ---------------- */
void print_optimised(void) {
    printf("\n--- Optimised Three Address Code ---\n");
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        char a1[20], a2[20];
        resolve(arg1[i], a1);
        if (op[i][0] != '=') resolve(arg2[i], a2);

        if (op[i][0] == '=')
            printf("%s = %s\n", res[i], a1);
        else
            printf("%s = %s %s %s\n", res[i], a1, op[i], a2);
    }
}

/* --------------- Driver : input TAC and run optimiser ---------- */
int main(void) {
    printf("Enter number of TAC instructions : ");
    scanf("%d", &n);
    getchar();

    printf("Enter TAC in the form  res = arg1 op arg2\n"
           "    (e.g.  t1 = 5 + 3   or   a = b )\n");
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        char line[100], *tok[10];
        fgets(line, sizeof(line), stdin);
        int tc = 0;
        char *p = strtok(line, " \t\n");
        while (p) { tok[tc++] = p; p = strtok(NULL, " \t\n"); }

        strcpy(res[i],  tok[0]);
        strcpy(arg1[i], tok[2]);
        if (tc > 3) {
            strcpy(op[i],  tok[3]);
            strcpy(arg2[i], tok[4]);
        } else {                     /* simple copy assignment */
            strcpy(op[i],  "=");
            strcpy(arg2[i], "");
        }
    }

    /* Display original code */
    printf("\n--- Original TAC ---\n");
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (op[i][0] == '=')
            printf("%s = %s\n", res[i], arg1[i]);
        else
            printf("%s = %s %s %s\n", res[i], arg1[i], op[i], arg2[i]);
    }

    propagate();
    print_optimised();
    return 0;
}
```

### 3.5 Sample Run

**Input:**

```
7
t1 = 5 + 3
t2 = a + t1
t3 = 2 * 4
a = 10
t4 = a + t1
b = a
t5 = b + t1
```

**Output:**

```
--- Optimised Three Address Code ---
t1 = 8
t2 = a + 8
t3 = 8
a = 10
t4 = 18
b = 10
t5 = 18
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart of the Constant Propagation Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Read TAC instructions into arrays]
    B --> C[Initialise Constant Table with UNDEF for all variables]
    C --> D[Set changed = true]
    D --> E[Set changed = false]
    E --> F[For i = 1 to n]
    F --> G{op is '='?}
    G -- Yes --> H[resolve arg1 using Constant Table]
    H --> I{arg1 is a literal number?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Set C res = arg1]
    J --> K[changed = true]
    I -- No --> L[Set C res = NAC]
    L --> M[i = i + 1]
    G -- No --> N[resolve arg1 and arg2 using Constant Table]
    N --> O{both arg1 and arg2 are literal numbers?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Compute val = arg1 op arg2]
    P --> Q[Set C res = val]
    Q --> K
    O -- No --> R[Set C res = NAC]
    R --> K
    K --> S{all instructions processed?}
    S -- No --> F
    S -- Yes --> T{changed == true?}
    T -- Yes --> E
    T -- No --> U[Print Optimised TAC by resolving operands]
    U --> V[Stop]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP[INPUT STAGE]
        P1[TAC Source File]
    end

    subgraph PARSE[PARSER MODULE]
        P2[Lexical Tokeniser]
        P3[Quadruple Builder]
    end

    subgraph TABLE[CONSTANT TABLE STORE]
        P4[Variable Name Index]
        P5[Abstract Value UNDEF or c or NAC]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE[OPTIMISATION ENGINE]
        P6[Resolver Subroutine]
        P7[Evaluator and Folder]
        P8[Fixed Point Controller]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT STAGE]
        P9[Optimised TAC Printer]
    end

    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P6
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P6
    P8 --> P9
```

### 4.3 Lattice Diagram — Three-State Abstraction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NAC[NAC Top Not A Constant] -->|sqsubseteq| MID[Constant c]
    MID -->|sqsubseteq| UND[UNDEF Bottom Undefined]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]  
> **Define constant propagation. Explain the role of the lattice value NAC in constant propagation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Constant propagation is a compiler optimisation technique based on **forward data-flow analysis** that substitutes variables known to hold a constant value along all execution paths with that constant literal.

**NAC (Not A Constant):** This is the **top element (⊤)** of the constant-propagation lattice. Whenever the analyser cannot guarantee that a variable holds a *single, fixed* value across all paths (e.g., the variable is assigned two different constants in two branches), it conservatively marks the variable as NAC. The compiler will then **refuse** to substitute the variable, ensuring the optimisation is always **safe** and never alters program semantics. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]  
> **Differentiate between constant propagation and constant folding. Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Constant Propagation | Constant Folding |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Replaces *variable uses* with known constant values | Evaluates *arithmetic expressions* of literals at compile time |
| **Trigger** | An assignment `x = c` is reachable | Both operands of an op are literal numbers |
| **Output** | `x = a + b` becomes `x = 5 + b` (if a = 5) | `5 + 3` becomes `8` |
| **Dependency** | Enables folding (runs *before*) | Depends on propagation (runs *after*) |

**Example:** `t1 = 5 + 3` → After folding, `t1 = 8`. If a previous statement did `a = 5`, then `t2 = a + 2` becomes `t2 = 5 + 2` (propagation) and then `t2 = 7` (folding). **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question 1(A).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]  
> **(a)** Explain the algorithm for constant propagation with a neat flowchart. Mention the lattice structure used. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Apply the constant propagation algorithm on the following TAC and show the optimised code: **(7 marks)**

```
(1)  x = 4
(2)  y = x + 6
(3)  z = y * 2
(4)  a = 5
(5)  b = a + x
(6)  c = b - 1
(7)  d = 3 + 7
```

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Algorithm (7 marks):**

1. **Initialise** a constant table $C$ with all variables set to **UNDEF**. (1 mark)
2. For each instruction, **resolve** operand variables using $C$. (1 mark)
3. Apply the **eval function** based on the operation type:
   - For `x = c`: $C[x] = c$ (1 mark)
   - For `x = y`: $C[x] = C[y]$ (1 mark)
   - For `x = y op z`: $C[x] = \text{eval}(C[y], C[z], \text{op})$ (1 mark)
4. **Re-iterate** until no entry in $C$ changes (fixed point). (1 mark)
5. **Lattice structure:** $\{ \text{UNDEF} \sqsubseteq c \sqsubseteq \text{NAC} \}$. (1 mark)

Flowchart is shown in **Section 4.1** above.

**Part (b) — Trace (7 marks):**

| Step | Instruction | Resolve | $C[\text{res}]$ |
|:-:|---|---|---|
| 1 | `x = 4`     | 4 (literal)            | $C[x] = 4$  |
| 2 | `y = x + 6` | $C[x]=4,\;6$           | $C[y] = 10$ |
| 3 | `z = y * 2` | $C[y]=10,\;2$          | $C[z] = 20$ |
| 4 | `a = 5`     | 5 (literal)            | $C[a] = 5$  |
| 5 | `b = a + x` | $C[a]=5,\;C[x]=4$      | $C[b] = 9$  |
| 6 | `c = b - 1` | $C[b]=9,\;1$           | $C[c] = 8$  |
| 7 | `d = 3 + 7` | $3,\;7$                | $C[d] = 10$ |

**Optimised TAC (after constant propagation + folding):**

```
(1)  x = 4
(2)  y = 10
(3)  z = 20
(4)  a = 5
(5)  b = 9
(6)  c = 8
(7)  d = 10
```

---

> **OR**

> **Question 1(B).** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]  
> **(a)** Discuss the data-flow equations used in constant propagation. Define the meet (∧) operation on the lattice. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Write a complete C program that reads three-address code from the user and performs constant propagation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Data-Flow Equations (7 marks):**

For a basic block $B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OUT}[B] &= \bigwedge_{S \in \text{succ}(B)} \text{IN}[S] \\[4pt]
\text{IN}[B]  &= f_B(\text{OUT}[\text{pred}(B)]) \\[4pt]
f_B &= f_{s_k} \circ f_{s_{k-1}} \circ \cdots \circ f_{s_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $f_{s_i}$ is the transfer function for statement $s_i$ in $B$.

**Meet (∧) operation** on $L = \{\text{UNDEF}, c, \text{NAC}\}$: (1 mark for each rule)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UNDEF} \;\wedge\; \alpha &= \alpha \\
\text{NAC} \;\wedge\; \alpha &= \text{NAC} \\
c_1 \;\wedge\; c_1 &= c_1 \\
c_1 \;\wedge\; c_2 &= \text{NAC}, \quad c_1 \neq c_2
\end{aligned}
$$

(Each rule is 1 mark × 4 = 4 marks, plus 1 mark each for IN/OUT equations = 3 marks.) **Total: 7 marks.**

**Part (b) — C Program (7 marks):**

Refer to the complete C program in **Section 3.4**. Key points the examiner looks for:

1. Correct data structures (arrays for op, arg1, arg2, res) — 1 mark
2. `is_num()` and `resolve()` helpers — 1 mark
3. `compute()` for arithmetic evaluation — 1 mark
4. Constant table management (`set_ct`, `find_ct`) — 1 mark
5. Iterative fixed-point loop with `changed` flag — 1 mark
6. Proper input parsing of TAC lines — 1 mark
7. Output of optimised TAC — 1 mark

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing NAC with UNDEF:** UNDEF means *not yet known*; NAC means *known to be non-constant*. Many students interchange them and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to re-iterate:** A single pass is **not** enough — propagation may unlock new constants in *earlier* blocks. Always run the fixed-point loop until `changed == false`. (1–2 marks lost)
> 3. **Skipping the meet operation:** In branched code (if-else), the value of a variable after the join point is the **meet** of values along both branches. Most students miss this and assume only the last assignment matters.
> 4. **Not distinguishing propagation from folding:** Examiners will mark them as two separate steps. If asked to "explain constant propagation", do *not* describe folding alone.
> 5. **Failing to handle the simple-copy case** `x = y` in code: many students only handle `x = y op z`.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Constant Propagation**

- ✔️ Constant propagation is a **forward**, **data-flow** optimisation using a **3-state lattice** $\{\text{UNDEF}, c, \text{NAC}\}$.
- ✔️ **UNDEF** = no info yet (bottom ⊥); **NAC** = not a constant (top ⊤); **c** = definite constant.
- ✔️ The **meet (∧)** operation is used at control-flow *join points*; the **transfer function** is used along a statement.
- ✔️ For `x = c`, transfer gives $C[x] = c$. For `x = y`, $C[x] = C[y]$. For `x = y op z`, $C[x] = \text{eval}(C[y], C[z])$.
- ✔️ $\text{eval}$ returns **NAC** if either operand is NAC, **UNDEF** if either is UNDEF, and the **folded constant** if both are integers.
- ✔️ Algorithm always **terminates** (lattice is finite, updates are monotonic) and is **safe** (NAC prevents incorrect substitution).
- ✔️ Constant propagation is a **prerequisite** for constant folding; together they reduce runtime arithmetic and memory loads.
- ✔️ In KTU labs, expect to (i) parse TAC, (ii) maintain a constant table, (iii) iterate to fixed point, (iv) print optimised code.
- ✔️ **Time complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot V)$ where $N$ = number of statements, $V$ = number of variables.
- ✔️ Common exam traps: confusing NAC/UNDEF, missing iteration, ignoring meet at joins, conflating propagation with folding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement Intermediate code generation for simple expressions
topics.content:
# Intermediate Code Generation (ICG) for Simple Expressions > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCSL607 - SYSTEMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implement Intermediate code generation for simple expressions --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intermediate Code Generation (ICG)** is the phase of a compiler that translates the **annotated parse tree** (or its semantic representation) produced by the **semantic analysis phase** into an **abstract, machine-independent intermediate representation (IR)**. This IR sits between the high-level source language and the final target machine code, enabling **retargetability**, **optimization**, and **modular compiler design**. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** Systems Lab context, the experiment specifically deals with generating **Three-Address Code (TAC)** — a canonical form of IR where each instruction contains **at most three operands** — for **simple arithmetic expressions** involving operators like `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, and parentheses. ### 1.2 Phases of a Compiler — Where ICG Fits A typical KTU-prescribed compiler pipeline is: $$ \underbrace{\text{Lexical Analysis}}_{\text{tokens}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Syntax Analysis}}_{\text{parse tree}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Semantic Analysis}}_{\text{annotated AST}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{ICG}}_{\text{TAC / Postfix}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Optimization}}_{\text{improved IR}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Code Generation}}_{\text{target code}} $$ ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Translator" > [!NOTE] > **Imagine** you are a UN interpreter translating speeches between 50 different diplomats who each speak a unique language. > > Instead of having 50×49 = **2450 direct translation pairs**, you translate every speech into one common language (say, **English**), and then from English into the target language. This reduces the problem to just **98 translations**. > > **Intermediate Code is that "common language"** of the compiler world. Front-ends (one per source language like C, Java, Python) all translate into it; back-ends (one per target like x86, ARM, RISC-V) all translate out of it. Without it, building compilers would be exponentially more expensive. ### 1.4 Why is ICG Important in KTU Context? - **Portability**: One front-end + many back-ends. - **Optimization**: Easier to perform machine-independent optimizations like constant folding, dead-code elimination, common subexpression elimination on IR. - **Modularity**: Cleanly separates *what to compute* from *how to compute on hardware*. ### 1.5 Forms of Intermediate Representation | IR Form | Description | KTU Relevance | |---|---|---| | **Postfix Notation** (Reverse Polish) | Operator follows operands: `a b +` | Highly tested in Module 2 | | **Three-Address Code (TAC)** | Max 3 operands per instruction: `t1 = a + b` | Most frequently asked | | **Syntax Tree / AST** | Hierarchical tree of operators and operands | Foundation for TAC | | **Quadruples** | A concrete TAC form with 4 fields per instr. | Standard KTU lab question | | **Triples** | TAC without temporary names; uses instruction pointers | KTU exam favorite | | **Indirect Triples** | Triples + an execution-order pointer list | Advanced KTU question | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Three-Address Code (TAC) — The Heart of ICG A **Three-Address Instruction** is a statement of the form: $$ x = y \;\text{op}\; z \quad \text{or} \quad x = \text{op}\; y $$ Where: - **x** is the **result** (a name, constant, or compiler-generated temporary like `t1`, `t2`). - **y** and **z** are **operands** (names, constants, or temporaries). - **op** is a **binary or unary operator** (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `<`, `>`, `==`, etc.). > [!NOTE] > The "three addresses" refers to the **three memory locations** involved — `x`, `y`, `z`. In practice, an instruction like `goto L` has fewer than three addresses, and `x = y` has two. The phrase "three-address" is a *ceiling*, not a strict rule. ### 2.2 The Complete Address Instruction Set | Instruction Type | Syntax | Example | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Binary Assignment | `x = y op z` | `t1 = a + b` | Apply `op` to `y` and `z`, store in `x` | | Unary Assignment | `x = op y` | `t2 = -c` | Apply unary `op` to `y`, store in `x` | | Copy | `x = y` | `t3 = t1` | Assign `y` to `x` | | Conditional Jump | `if x goto L` | `if t1 goto L1` | Jump to `L` if `x` is true | | Unconditional Jump | `goto L` | `goto L2` | Always jump to `L` | | Indexed Copy | `x = y[i]` | `t4 = a[4]` | Load array element | | Indexed Store | `x[i] = y` | `b[2] = t4` | Store into array | | Address / Deref | `x = &y`, `x = *y`, `*x = y` | `t5 = &x` | Pointer ops | | Function Call | `param x`, `call p, n`, `y = call p, n`, `return x` | `call foo, 2` | Procedure linkage | ### 2.3 Concrete Representations of TAC (KTU High-Yield!) #### A. Quadruples (Records with 4 Fields) Each instruction is a 4-tuple: **(op, arg1, arg2, result)**. | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | |---|---|---|---| | `*` | b | c | t1 | | `+` | a | t1 | t2 | | `-` | t2 | d | t3 | - **Arg1, arg2** → operands - **Result** → destination (often a temporary) - **Implicit**: position of the row = program order #### B. Triples (Position-Referenced, No Temp Names) Each instruction is a 3-tuple: **(op, arg1, arg2)**. Operands refer to *positions* (instruction numbers) instead of named temporaries. | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | |---|---|---|---| | (0) | `*` | b | c | | (1) | `+` | a | (0) | | (2) | `-` | (1) | d | - More compact; avoids creating temporaries. - Harder to optimize (instruction numbers shift on insertion/deletion). #### C. Indirect Triples (Triples + Indirection List) Add a **list of pointers** to triples to preserve execution order independently of row order. | Instruction List | → | Triple | |---|---|---| | [10] | → | (10: `*`, b, c) | | [11] | → | (11: `+`, a, (10)) | | [12] | → | (12: `-`, (11), d) | - **Best of both worlds**: compact like triples + stable like quadruples. ### 2.4 Algorithm — Generating TAC from an Expression The standard **SDT (Syntax-Directed Translation)** scheme uses synthesized attribute `code` that returns a TAC location (a temp name or a position). **For a binary expression `E → E1 + T`:** 1. **Recursively** generate code for `E1` → yields a location `E1.place`. 2. **Recursively** generate code for `T` → yields `T.place`. 3. **Create** a new temporary `t = newtemp()`. 4. **Emit** the quadruple `(+ , E1.place , T.place , t)`. 5. **Set** `E.place = t`. **Similar rules for `-`, `*`, `/`.** **For an identifier `E → id`:** - Set `E.place = id.entry` (the symbol-table entry). **For a constant `E → num`:** - Set `E.place = num.lexval`. > [!TIP] > **`newtemp()`** is a routine maintained by the compiler that returns a fresh symbolic name `t1, t2, t3, ...` every time it is called. Each invocation produces a unique identifier never reused in the same routine. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — ICG for Simple Expressions | # | Concept | Expression / Rule | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | TAC form | `x = y op z` | Max 3 operands | | 2 | Postfix rule | Infix → Postfix: scan, push ops on stack, pop higher-precedence | Used by stack machine | | 3 | Quadruple | `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` | Most common form | | 4 | Triple | `(op, arg1, arg2)` with positional refs | Compact; unstable on edits | | 5 | Indirect triple | Pointer list → triple | Stable on edits | | 6 | Number of temporaries | One `newtemp()` per non-leaf internal node | Count = AST internal nodes | | 7 | Precedence | `* /` > `+ -`; left-associative | Drives postfix/TAC order | | 8 | AST → TAC | Post-order traversal of AST | Standard algorithm | > [!WARNING] > When converting infix to postfix, **parentheses force the order** by pushing a marker (`(`) onto the operator stack so that the enclosed operators are flushed first. Forgetting this is the **#1 mistake** in KTU ICG viva questions. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example #1 — Generating TAC for `a + b * c - d` We will derive TAC, quadruples, triples, and indirect triples step by step. #### Step 1 — Build the AST respecting precedence The expression `a + b * c - d` is parsed as `((a) + (b * c)) - (d)` because `*` binds tighter than `+` and `-`, and `+` and `-` are left-associative. #### Step 2 — Post-order traversal of the AST | Step | Visited Node | Action | Temp | Quadruple Emitted | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `a` (leaf) | Place = `a` | — | — | | 2 | `b` (leaf) | Place = `b` | — | — | | 3 | `c` (leaf) | Place = `c` | — | — | | 4 | `*` | `t1 = b * c` | `t1` | `(*, b, c, t1)` | | 5 | `+` | `t2 = a + t1` | `t2` | `(+, a, t1, t2)` | | 6 | `d` (leaf) | Place = `d` | — | — | | 7 | `-` | `t3 = t2 - d` | `t3` | `(-, t2, d, t3)` | **Final TAC (Three-Address Code):** $$ \text{t1} = \text{b} * \text{c} $$ $$ \text{t2} = \text{a} + \text{t1} $$ $$ \text{t3} = \text{t2} - \text{d} $$ **Final Quadruples Table:** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | `*` | b | c | t1 | | 1 | `+` | a | t1 | t2 | | 2 | `-` | t2 | d | t3 | **Final Triples Table (no temp names; positions used as references):** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | |---|---|---|---| | (0) | `*` | b | c | | (1) | `+` | a | (0) | | (2) | `-` | (1) | d | **Final Indirect Triples:** | Instruction List (order) → | Triple # | op | arg1 | arg2 | |---|---|---|---|---| | [100] → | (100) | `*` | b | c | | [101] → | (101) | `+` | a | (100) | | [102] → | (102) | `-` | (101) | d | --- ### 3.2 Worked Example #2 — Generating TAC for `a + b * (c - d) / e` This introduces **parentheses**, which alter the binding order. #### Step 1 — Parenthesized AST The expression `a + b * (c - d) / e` parses as `((a) + ((b * (c - d)) / e))`. Note that `*` and `/` have equal precedence and are **left-associative**, so we first compute `b * (c - d)`, then divide by `e`. #### Step 2 — TAC Generation (post-order) $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{t1} = \text{c} - \text{d} \\ &\text{t2} = \text{b} * \text{t1} \\ &\text{t3} = \text{t2} / \text{e} \\ &\text{t4} = \text{a} + \text{t3} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3 — Quadruples | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | `-` | c | d | t1 | | 1 | `*` | b | t1 | t2 | | 2 | `/` | t2 | e | t3 | | 3 | `+` | a | t3 | t4 | #### Step 4 — Triples | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | |---|---|---|---| | (0) | `-` | c | d | | (1) | `*` | b | (0) | | (2) | `/` | (1) | e | | (3) | `+` | a | (2) | > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice how **removing parentheses** would yield the entirely different expression `a + (b * c) - (d / e)`. Parentheses must always be **explicit nodes** in the AST and are not represented in TAC directly — their effect is *baked into* the order of the emitted three-address instructions. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example #3 — Boolean Expression TAC For `a < b OR c < d AND e < f`: Because `AND` has higher precedence than `OR`, the AST is `((a < b) OR ((c < d) AND (e < f)))`. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{t1} = \text{a} < \text{b} \\ &\text{t2} = \text{c} < \text{d} \\ &\text{t3} = \text{e} < \text{f} \\ &\text{t4} = \text{t2} \;\text{AND}\; \text{t3} \\ &\text{t5} = \text{t1} \;\text{OR}\; \text{t4} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > This example is useful for viva: TAC handles **control flow** via labels. A full translation would emit `if t1 goto L_true`, `t5 = 0`, etc. For "simple expressions" experiments, you only need the **arithmetic** TAC form. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Compiler Pipeline Showing ICG Position ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source Program
a + b * c - d] --> B[Lexical Analysis] B --> C[Tokens
id, PLUS, id, MUL, id, MINUS, id] C --> D[Syntax Analysis] D --> E[Parse Tree / AST] E --> F[Semantic Analysis
type checking, scope] F --> G[Intermediate Code Generation] G --> H[Three Address Code
t1 = b * c
t2 = a + t1
t3 = t2 - d] H --> I[Optimization] I --> J[Code Generation] J --> K[Target Assembly
mov eax, ebx
imul ecx, edx] K --> L[Executable Machine Code] ``` ### 4.2 Expression AST for `a + b * c - d` ```mermaid flowchart TD N_minus["-"] --> N_leftplus["+"] N_minus --> N_d["d"] N_leftplus --> N_a["a"] N_leftplus --> N_mul["*"] N_mul --> N_b["b"] N_mul --> N_c["c"] ``` ### 4.3 SDT Flow for `E → E1 + T` ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Start: parse E] --> S1[Recurse: parse E1] S1 --> S2[Get E1.place from E1] S2 --> S3[Match PLUS token] S3 --> S4[Recurse: parse T] S4 --> S5[Get T.place from T] S5 --> S6[Generate newtemp t] S6 --> S7[Emit quadruple: PLUS, E1.place, T.place, t] S7 --> S8[Set E.place = t] S8 --> S9[Return E.place] ``` ### 4.4 Mapping Between TAC Representations ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source: a + b * c - d] --> B[AST] B --> C[Quadruples
op arg1 arg2 result] B --> D[Triples
op arg1 arg2
positions] B --> E[Indirect Triples
Pointer list
plus triples] B --> F[Postfix Notation
a b c * + d -] ``` ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ICG Module ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Front_End A1[Lexer] --> A2[Parser] --> A3[Semantic Analyzer] end subgraph ICG_Module A3 --> B1[AST Builder] B1 --> B2[Post-order Walker] B2 --> B3[newtemp Generator] B2 --> B4[Quadruple Emitter] B2 --> B5[Triple Emitter] B2 --> B6[Postfix Emitter] end subgraph Back_End B4 --> C1[Optimizer] B5 --> C1 B6 --> C1 C1 --> C2[Code Generator] end ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define Intermediate Code. List any two benefits of using intermediate code in a compiler.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Intermediate code is a machine-independent abstract representation of the source program, generated between the semantic analysis phase and the code generation phase.* (1 mark) *Benefits:* (2 marks) 1. **Retargetability**: The same front-end can be combined with multiple back-ends to generate code for different target machines without rewriting the front-end. 2. **Optimization**: Machine-independent optimizations (constant folding, dead-code elimination, common subexpression elimination) can be applied on the intermediate representation before target code generation, improving runtime efficiency. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **What is Three-Address Code (TAC)? Give two examples of TAC instructions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Three-Address Code (TAC) is a form of intermediate representation in which each instruction contains at most three operands: one result and two source operands.* (1 mark) *Examples:* (2 marks) 1. **Binary TAC**: `t1 = a + b` (operand1 = `a`, operand2 = `b`, result = `t1`, operator = `+`). 2. **Copy TAC**: `x = y` (assigns the value of `y` into `x`). --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Generate the **Three-Address Code**, **Quadruples**, and **Triples** for the expression: $$ \text{x} = \text{a} * \text{b} + \text{c} / \text{d} - \text{e} $$ **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the differences between **Quadruples** and **Triples** with proper examples. Why are **Indirect Triples** introduced? **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (Q3A):** #### Part (a) — Solution **Step 1 — Build the AST respecting precedence.** `*` and `/` have higher precedence than `+` and `-`. Left-to-right associativity for equal precedence. The expression parses as: `x = ((a * b) + (c / d)) - e` **Step 2 — Generate TAC by post-order traversal.** | Step | Operation | TAC Instruction | |---|---|---| | 1 | Multiply `a` and `b` | `t1 = a * b` | | 2 | Divide `c` by `d` | `t2 = c / d` | | 3 | Add `t1` and `t2` | `t3 = t1 + t2` | | 4 | Subtract `e` from `t3` | `t4 = t3 - e` | | 5 | Assign result to `x` | `x = t4` | **Three-Address Code:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{t1} = \text{a} * \text{b} \\ &\text{t2} = \text{c} / \text{d} \\ &\text{t3} = \text{t1} + \text{t2} \\ &\text{t4} = \text{t3} - \text{e} \\ &\text{x} = \text{t4} \end{aligned} $$ **Quadruples Table:** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | `*` | a | b | t1 | | 1 | `/` | c | d | t2 | | 2 | `+` | t1 | t2 | t3 | | 3 | `-` | t3 | e | t4 | | 4 | `=` | t4 | | x | **Triples Table:** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | |---|---|---|---| | (0) | `*` | a | b | | (1) | `/` | c | d | | (2) | `+` | (0) | (1) | | (3) | `-` | (2) | e | | (4) | `=` | x | (3) | *(All 7 marks for correct derivation, complete TAC, both tables.)* --- #### Part (b) — Differences between Quadruples, Triples, and Indirect Triples **Quadruples** *(2 marks)*: - Each instruction is a 4-field record: `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`. - Temporary names (`t1`, `t2`, ...) are explicitly created and stored in the `result` field. - **Advantage**: Easy to perform code movement and optimization because temporary names are stable. - **Disadvantage**: Requires storing temporary names in a separate symbol table. **Triples** *(2 marks)*: - Each instruction is a 3-field record: `(op, arg1, arg2)`. - No temporary names; the operand positions in earlier triples are referenced by their instruction *position number* (e.g., `(0)`, `(1)`). - **Advantage**: More compact; no need to manage temporary names. - **Disadvantage**: When instructions are moved or deleted during optimization, all references to their positions become invalid, requiring renumbering. **Indirect Triples** *(2 marks)*: - Consists of two parts: a **list of pointers** and the **triples** themselves. - The execution order is maintained in the pointer list, so triples can be reordered physically without disturbing the execution sequence. - **Advantage**: Combines the compactness of triples with the stability needed for optimization. - **Disadvantage**: Adds an extra level of indirection. **Summary Table** *(1 mark)*: | Feature | Quadruples | Triples | Indirect Triples | |---|---|---|---| | Fields | 4 | 3 | 3 + pointer list | | Temp names | Yes | No (positions) | No (positions) | | Optimization-friendly | High | Low | High | | Space | Larger | Smaller | Smallest effective | --- **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Translate the following expression into **Three-Address Code (TAC)** and represent the same in **quadruple** and **indirect triple** notation: $$ \text{p} = \text{a} - \text{b} / \text{c} + \text{d} * \text{e} $$ **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write the **SDT (Syntax-Directed Translation) scheme** to translate an infix arithmetic expression into Three-Address Code. Show the synthesized attribute `code` propagation clearly. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (Q3B):** #### Part (a) — Solution **Step 1 — Build AST.** The expression parses as `p = ((a - (b / c)) + (d * e))` because `/` and `*` bind tighter than `-` and `+`, and the operators of equal precedence evaluate left-to-right. **Step 2 — Generate TAC by post-order traversal.** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{t1} = \text{b} / \text{c} \\ &\text{t2} = \text{a} - \text{t1} \\ &\text{t3} = \text{d} * \text{e} \\ &\text{t4} = \text{t2} + \text{t3} \\ &\text{p} = \text{t4} \end{aligned} $$ **Quadruples:** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | `/` | b | c | t1 | | 1 | `-` | a | t1 | t2 | | 2 | `*` | d | e | t3 | | 3 | `+` | t2 | t3 | t4 | | 4 | `=` | t4 | | p | **Indirect Triples:** | Execution Order (Pointer List) | Triple # | op | arg1 | arg2 | |---|---|---|---|---| | [200] | (200) | `/` | b | c | | [201] | (201) | `-` | a | (200) | | [202] | (202) | `*` | d | e | | [203] | (203) | `+` | (201) | (202) | | [204] | (204) | `=` | p | (203) | *(7 marks for full derivation.)* --- #### Part (b) — SDT Scheme for ICG The translation scheme uses the production rules of a typical expression grammar. Each non-terminal has a synthesized attribute `place` that denotes the **location** holding the value of that subexpression. | Production | Semantic Action | |---|---| | `E → E1 + T` | `E.place = newtemp(); emit(+, E1.place, T.place, E.place)` | | `E → E1 - T` | `E.place = newtemp(); emit(-, E1.place, T.place, E.place)` | | `E → T` | `E.place = T.place` | | `T → T1 * F` | `T.place = newtemp(); emit(*, T1.place, F.place, T.place)` | | `T → T1 / F` | `T.place = newtemp(); emit(/, T1.place, F.place, T.place)` | | `T → F` | `T.place = F.place` | | `F → (E)` | `F.place = E.place` | | `F → id` | `F.place = id.entry` (symbol-table pointer) | *(7 marks distributed across grammar accuracy, semantic actions, and a one-line worked note showing `newtemp()` producing t1, t2, ... sequentially.)* --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make in ICG questions (and where marks are lost):** > 1. **Wrong operator precedence**: Treating `a + b * c` as `(a + b) * c` instead of `a + (b * c)`. Always remember: `*`, `/` > `+`, `-`. **Marks lost: up to 3.** > 2. **Right-associativity error**: Forgetting that `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` are **left-associative** in most languages, which affects how nested equal-precedence operators are grouped. **Marks lost: up to 2.** > 3. **Triple reference numbering**: In triples, operands reference instruction *positions*, not temporaries. Writing `(t1)` instead of `(0)` is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU evaluation. > 4. **Missing `=` instruction**: Forgetting the final assignment (e.g., `x = t3`) when the LHS of the original expression is a variable. **Marks lost: 1.** > 5. **Quadruple vs Triple mix-up**: Writing the `result` column in triples, or omitting the `result` column in quadruples. **Marks lost: 2.** > 6. **Parentheses ignored**: Generating TAC for `a + b * c` instead of `a + b * (c - d)` when parentheses are present. **Marks lost: up to 4.** > 7. **Bonus: Not writing units of operands** — irrelevant here, but in the *theory* section, always state the precedence and associativity rules **explicitly** for full marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Intermediate Code Generation for Simple Expressions** - ✅ ICG sits **between semantic analysis and code generation** in the compiler pipeline. - ✅ The most common ICG form tested in KTU is **Three-Address Code (TAC)**: `x = y op z`. - ✅ TAC is generated by a **post-order traversal of the AST** (or via an SDT on the parse tree). - ✅ Each **internal node** of the AST corresponds to **one `newtemp()` call** and **one TAC instruction**. - ✅ Leaves of the AST return `id.entry` (for identifiers) or `num.lexval` (for constants). - ✅ Three concrete TAC representations: **Quadruples** (4 fields, named temps), **Triples** (3 fields, position refs), **Indirect Triples** (pointer list + triples). - ✅ **Quadruples** are easiest to optimize; **Triples** are most space-efficient; **Indirect Triples** combine both benefits. - ✅ Operator precedence: `*`, `/` > `+`, `-` > `==`, `!=` > `AND` > `OR`.
