Always parse with precedence. - ✅ Associativity for arithmetic ops in C/Java/Python: **left-to-right**. Affects grouping of equal-precedence operators. - ✅ **Parentheses must be explicit AST nodes** — they are not preserved in TAC; their effect is baked into instruction order. - ✅ Boolean expressions also generate TAC, but full translation introduces **labels and jumps** (`if x goto L`). - ✅ Common viva question: *"Why not directly generate target code?"* — Answer: **portability, modularity, optimization opportunities**. - ✅ Lab tip: When implementing, maintain a global counter `tempCount` and `newtemp()` returns `"t" + tempCount++`. - ✅ Common pitfall: Confusing the **stack machine postfix** (`a b c * + d -`) with **prefix** (`- + a * b c d`). For TAC, the order is **infix-style** with temporaries.

topics.title: Implement the back end of the compiler which takes the three address code and produces assembly language instructions that can be assembled and run using a corresponding assembler, including simple move and jump instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL607 - SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement the back end of the compiler which takes the three address code and produces assembly language instructions that can be assembled and run using a corresponding assembler, including simple move and jump instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *PCCSL607 - Systems Lab*, the **back end of a compiler** is formally defined as the synthesis phase that maps a **semantically verified Intermediate Representation (IR)** — typically expressed as **Three Address Code (TAC)** — into a target-specific **assembly language program** which can subsequently be assembled and executed on a target machine.

The back end is composed of the following canonical sub-phases:

1. **Code Generation (Instruction Selection)** — Translating each TAC instruction into a sequence of primitive **assembler mnemonics** (e.g., `MOV`, `ADD`, `SUB`, `CMP`, `JMP`, `JE`, `JNE`).
2. **Register Allocation and Assignment** — Deciding which TAC operands reside in which physical CPU registers at each program point, to minimize **memory traffic**.
3. **Instruction Scheduling (advanced topic)** — Reordering instructions to hide pipeline stalls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus for this experiment:** The lab explicitly restricts the scope to producing **simple move (`MOV`) and jump instructions** along with a minimal set of arithmetic/logic instructions sufficient to assemble and run. Full register optimization, peephole transforms, and pipeline scheduling are **out of scope** for this experiment.

## 1.2 Three Address Code (TAC) — The Input Token

**Three Address Code (TAC)** is an abstract IR where every instruction involves **at most three operands** and typically has the form:

```
x = y op z          (binary op)
x = op y            (unary op)
goto L              (unconditional jump)
if x goto L         (conditional jump)
ifFalse x goto L    (conditional jump)
x = y               (simple assignment / move)
```

Each TAC statement is a **quadruple** of the form `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` or a **triple** using indirect references. For this experiment, KTU expects students to assume TAC is supplied as **textual quadruples** like:

```
t1 = a + b
t2 = t1 * c
t3 = t2 - d
if t3 > 0 goto L1
x = 0
goto L2
L1: x = 1
L2: print x
```

## 1.3 Assembly Language Output — The Target Token

**Assembly language** is a human-readable, mnemonic representation of machine instructions. For the KTU experiment, students may target any simple architecture; the most pedagogically clean choice is a **virtual / educational instruction set** with the following minimal opcodes:

| Mnemonic | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `MOV R1, R2` | Copy contents of R2 into R1 | `MOV AX, BX` |
| `MOV R1, const` | Load constant into R1 | `MOV AX, 5` |
| `MOV R1, mem` | Load from memory | `MOV AX, [a]` |
| `MOV mem, R1` | Store to memory | `MOV [a], AX` |
| `ADD R1, R2` | R1 = R1 + R2 | `ADD AX, BX` |
| `SUB R1, R2` | R1 = R1 - R2 | `SUB AX, BX` |
| `MUL R1, R2` | R1 = R1 * R2 | `MUL AX, BX` |
| `DIV R1, R2` | R1 = R1 / R2 | `DIV AX, BX` |
| `CMP R1, R2` | Compare, set flags | `CMP AX, 0` |
| `JMP label` | Unconditional jump | `JMP L1` |
| `JE label` | Jump if equal (zero flag) | `JE L1` |
| `JNE label` | Jump if not equal | `JNE L1` |
| `JG label` | Jump if greater | `JG L1` |
| `JL label` | Jump if less | `JL L1` |

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (The Translator Chef Analogy):**
> Imagine a customer (the programmer) writes a recipe in a high-level language (English): *"Add the flour and sugar, then if the batter is too thick, jump to step 7."* 
> 
> - The **front end** of the compiler is the head chef who translates the recipe into a standardized instruction card (TAC) — language-neutral and precise.
> - The **back end / code generator** is the line cook who takes each standardized card and shouts out concrete kitchen commands: *"SCOOP 2 CUPS FLOUR INTO BOWL A, SCOOP 1 CUP SUGAR INTO BOWL B, MIX A+B..."* — these shouts are the **assembly instructions**.
> - The **assembler** is the robot arm that mechanically executes the shouts and produces the actual cake.
> 
> The back end is essentially the **last translator before the machine**, and it must obey the CPU's grammar (its **Instruction Set Architecture, ISA**).

## 1.5 Position of the Back End in the Compiler Pipeline

The position of the experiment in the overall compiler is:

```
Source Program (.c)
      ↓
[ Lexical Analyzer ] → Tokens
      ↓
[ Syntax Analyzer ] → Parse Tree / AST
      ↓
[ Semantic Analyzer ] → Decorated AST
      ↓
[ Intermediate Code Generator ] → THREE ADDRESS CODE   ← INPUT to our experiment
      ↓
★ [ CODE GENERATOR / BACK END ] → ASSEMBLY CODE  ★   ← THIS EXPERIMENT
      ↓
[ Assembler ] → Object Code (.o)
      ↓
[ Linker / Loader ] → Executable
```

The KTU experiment begins at the **TAC input** and ends at **assembly language output** that the student's own assembler module (Experiment 1 in the same course) can consume.

## 1.6 Standard Performance Metrics in Code Generation

The quality of generated assembly is measured by the following **industry-standard metrics**:

- **Code Size** — Total number of assembly instructions generated. Lower is better.
- **Memory Traffic** — Number of `MOV mem, R` or `MOV R, mem` instructions. Lower is better.
- **Register Spills** — Number of times a value must be evicted to memory. Lower is better.
- **Execution Time** — End-to-end runtime, related to instruction count and memory access pattern.
- **Static Metrics:** **Code size**, **Instruction count**.
- **Dynamic Metrics:** **Spill count**, **Memory accesses executed at runtime**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aho-Sethi-Ullman (Dragon Book) Theorem:** An *optimal* code-generation algorithm for expressions trees is given by **Sethi-Ullman labeling**, which labels each node `n` with a number `l(n)` = minimum number of registers required to evaluate `n` without spilling. The KTU lab does **not** mandate this; it accepts the simpler "**getreg**" heuristic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Code Generator

A simple TAC → Assembly code generator operates on three key data structures:

| Data Structure | Purpose | Typical Form |
|----------------|---------|--------------|
| **Address Descriptor** | For every *name* (variable/temp), tracks the *locations* where its current value can be found. | `addr_desc['t1'] = {'AX', 'M[1004]'} ` |
| **Register Descriptor** | For every *register*, tracks the *names* whose value it currently holds. | `reg_desc['AX'] = {'t1', 'a'}` |
| **Instruction Stream** | The emitted assembly list. | `["MOV AX, a", "ADD AX, BX", ...]` |

## 2.2 The General Code-Generation Algorithm (Aho et al.)

The classic, KTU-aligned algorithm for a single TAC statement `x = y op z` is:

1. Invoke `getReg(y op z = x)` to pick a register `R` to hold the result `x`.
2. Consult the address descriptor of `y`:
   - If `y` is in a register `Ry`, generate `MOV R, Ry`.
   - Else generate `MOV R, M[y_loc]`.
3. If `z` is a memory operand (not a constant and not in a register), generate `MOV R, M[z_loc]`.
4. Emit the operation: `OP R, Rz` (or `OP R, const`).
5. Update the address descriptor: `addr_desc[x] = {R}`.
6. Update the register descriptor: add `x` to `reg_desc[R]`, remove `x` from all other registers.
7. If `R` holds a value that is no longer needed, optionally **spill** by emitting `MOV M[y_loc], R`.

## 2.3 The `getReg` Function — The Heart of the Generator

The function `getReg(I)`, where `I` is the instruction `x = y op z`, returns a register `R` chosen as follows:

```
function getReg(x = y op z):
    1. If y is held in a register R, and R holds only y (and y is not also in memory),
       and the instruction is a copy (op is =), return R.
    2. Otherwise, if there is an empty register, return it.
    3. Otherwise, if x has a next use in the instruction stream, OR
       (op is a binary op and z has a next use), then:
       Choose a register R that is already holding a value whose
       next use is farthest in the future (or never).
       Emit "MOV M[loc], R" to spill that value.
       Return R.
    4. Otherwise (x and z have no next use, or x is not needed):
       Return a register R that is already holding x (reuse without reload).
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Next-Use Information:** This is computed by a single backward scan over the TAC. For each name on the LHS, we attach the *index* of the next instruction that uses it. If no such instruction exists, next-use is *∞* (or "NAN" — not a number). This information is **crucial** for choosing which register to spill.

## 2.4 Generation Rules for Each TAC Statement Type

The following is the canonical mapping table every KTU student must memorize:

| TAC Form | Generated Assembly Pattern | Notes |
|----------|---------------------------|-------|
| `x = y` | `MOV R, Ry` (or `MOV R, M[y]`) | Simple **move** instruction |
| `x = y + z` | `MOV R, Ry`; `ADD R, Rz` | Order matters: load y first |
| `x = y - z` | `MOV R, Ry`; `SUB R, Rz` | |
| `x = y * z` | `MOV R, Ry`; `MUL R, Rz` | |
| `x = y / z` | `MOV R, Ry`; `DIV R, Rz` | |
| `x = -y` | `MOV R, Ry`; `NEG R` | Unary op |
| `goto L` | `JMP L` | Unconditional **jump** |
| `if x relop y goto L` | `MOV R1, Rx`; `CMP R1, Ry`; `Jcond L` | Conditional **jump** |
| `if x goto L` | `MOV R, Rx`; `CMP R, 0`; `JNE L` | Treat as `x != 0` |

## 2.5 Handling Labels and Basic Blocks

The TAC is structured as a sequence of **basic blocks** — maximal sequences of instructions where flow enters at the top and leaves only at the bottom. Labels demarcate block boundaries:

```
L1:  t1 = a + b
     t2 = t1 * c
     if t2 > 0 goto L3
     t3 = 0
     goto L4
L3:  t3 = 1
L4:  ...
```

When generating code, the label `L1` becomes the assembly label `L1:`. The back end simply prefixes the label to the first instruction of the corresponding block.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Rule / Formula | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | TAC size | $\vert TAC \vert = n$ instructions | Input length |
| 2 | Code size | $\vert Code \vert \le 2n$ for binary ops, $\le n$ for unary/move | Lower bound: $n$; typical: $1.5n$ to $2n$ |
| 3 | Optimal registers for tree | $l(n) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } n \text{ is leaf} \\ \max(l(\text{left}), l(\text{right})) & \text{if } l(\text{left}) \neq l(\text{right}) \\ l(\text{left}) + 1 & \text{if } l(\text{left}) = l(\text{right}) \end{cases}$ | Sethi-Ullman labeling |
| 4 | Memory traffic | $\text{cost} = \sum \text{loads} + \sum \text{stores}$ | Quality metric |
| 5 | Spill cost | $\text{cost}_{\text{spill}} = 1 \text{ load} + 1 \text{ store}$ | Cost of evicting a register |
| 6 | Next-use distance | $d(x, p) = \min \{ i > p \vert x \in \text{use}(I_i) \}$ | Pick farthest for spill |
| 7 | Number of physical registers | $k = 4 \text{ to } 8$ in KTU lab | Small register set |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sethi-Ullman Theorem (1970):** The minimum number of registers required to evaluate an expression tree without spilling is given by the recursive formula in row 3 of the table. For a balanced tree of $n$ leaves, this is $\Theta(\log_2 n)$. This is a *lower bound* — no generator can do better.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Industry

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **GCC / LLVM** | Production back ends of GCC and LLVM use TAC-like IR (GIMPLE, LLVM IR) and apply exactly these code-generation principles. |
| **JIT Compilers** | Java HotSpot, V8 (JavaScript) — emit native assembly at runtime using these techniques. |
| **DSP Programming** | Texas Instruments Code Composer, ARM Keil — back ends for embedded DSPs. |
| **Compiler Certification** | DO-178C (avionics), MISRA-C — generated assembly must be verifiable, motivating formal code-generation. |
| **Educational Tools** | Tiny C, MINIX, Nachos — academic compilers where this lab's algorithm is the prototype. |

## 2.8 Detailed Example — Single TAC Instruction `t1 = a + b`

Let's trace the algorithm end-to-end on the simple statement `t1 = a + b`:

| Step | Action | Descriptor Update | Emitted Code |
|------|--------|--------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Call `getReg(t1 = a + b)` | — | — |
| 2 | Find empty register, say `AX` | — | — |
| 3 | Look up `a` in address descriptor | `addr_desc['a'] = {AX}` (assume already in AX) | — |
| 4 | Look up `b` in address descriptor | `addr_desc['b'] = {M[2000]}` (in memory) | — |
| 5 | Emit load for `a` | — | `MOV AX, AX` *(no-op or skipped)* |
| 6 | Emit load for `b` | — | `MOV AX, [b]` |
| 7 | Emit add | — | `ADD AX, ...` — wait, this is wrong. See next step. |
| 7* | **Corrected:** Emit `MOV AX, [a]` if needed, then `ADD AX, [b]` | `reg_desc['AX'] = {t1, a}`, `reg_desc['AX'] -= {a}` after add... | `MOV AX, [a]; ADD AX, [b]` |
| 8 | Update descriptors | `addr_desc['t1'] = {AX}` | — |

The corrected final code: `MOV AX, [a] ; ADD AX, [b]`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Trying to use a single register for both the first operand and the accumulator. Always load the first operand into a register, then use a *different* register (or memory) for the second operand in a two-address style. In *three-address* style assembly, the answer is `MOV AX, [a] ; ADD AX, [b]` where `b` is also loaded first.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Implementation Walkthrough

## 3.1 Formal Derivation — Why We Need Next-Use Information

**Theorem (Register Reuse Optimality):** If a name $x$ is currently in a register $R$ and the next use of $x$ is at instruction $I_k$, then $R$ cannot be reused for any other name $y$ whose next use is at $I_j$ with $j < k$, *unless* we emit a store of $x$ to memory and later reload it.

**Proof sketch:**

1. Suppose $R$ holds $x$ at program point $p$.
2. We wish to use $R$ for $y$ at point $p$.
3. We must first ensure $x$ is safe in memory: emit `MOV M[x_loc], R`.
4. Then load $y$: emit `MOV R, M[y_loc]` or `MOV R, Ry`.
5. Cost incurred: **2 memory instructions**.
6. If instead we picked a register $R'$ that holds a name with a *farther* (or no) next use, this cost is paid by that other name — which is more "remote" and thus *equally valid or better*.
7. Therefore, the optimal strategy is: **when spilling, pick the register whose value has the latest next-use.** $\blacksquare$

This is the theoretical foundation of the `getReg` function.

## 3.2 Derivations of the Sethi-Ullman Number for Common Tree Shapes

For KTU problems, students are often asked the minimum register count for an expression tree. Let us derive these step by step.

### Case 1: Linear chain of $n$ additions, e.g., $(((a+b)+c)+d)+\dots$

Let $T_n$ be the minimum register count for a chain of $n$ leaves.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= 1 \quad \text{(single leaf needs 1 register to hold its value)} \\
T_2 &= 2 \quad \text{(evaluate left leaf in R1, right leaf in R2, then add, store in R1)} \\
T_n &= T_{n-1} + 1 \quad \text{(each new binary op needs one more register in the worst case)} \\
T_n &= n
\end{aligned}
$$

### Case 2: Balanced binary tree with $n$ leaves, $n = 2^k$

Let $S_k$ be the minimum register count.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_0 &= 1 \quad \text{(a single leaf)} \\
S_1 &= 2 \quad \text{(two leaves + one op)} \\
S_k &= S_{k-1} + 1 \quad \text{(recursive: each side needs } S_{k-1} \text{, but they can share)} \\
S_k &= k + 1 = \log_2 n + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Case 3: Skewed right tree

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 1 \\
R_n &= 2 \quad \text{(for } n \ge 2 \text{, only 2 registers needed if the right spine is in 1 reg)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sethi-Ullman General Formula** (for any binary tree node $n$ with children $L$ and $R$):
> 
> $$l(n) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } n \text{ is a leaf} \\ l(L) & \text{if } l(L) = l(R) \text{ — choose the higher-labeled side first} \\ \max(l(L), l(R)) & \text{if } l(L) \neq l(R) \\ l(L) + 1 & \text{if } l(L) = l(R) \end{cases}$$
> 
> When $l(L) = l(R)$, evaluating the higher-labeled subtree first requires one extra register to hold the result of the *first* subtree while evaluating the *second*.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Code Generation for an Expression Tree

**Source expression:** $(a + b) - (c + d)$

**Step 1 — Draw the expression tree:**

```
        (-)
       /   \
     (+)   (+)
    /  \   /  \
   a    b c    d
```

**Step 2 — Apply Sethi-Ullman labeling bottom-up:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
l(a) &= 1 \\
l(b) &= 1 \\
l(c) &= 1 \\
l(d) &= 1 \\
l(\text{left +}) &= l(a) + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \quad (\text{since } l(a) = l(b) = 1) \\
l(\text{right +}) &= l(c) + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
l(\text{root -}) &= \max(2, 2) = 2 \quad (\text{since both subtrees have equal label})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum registers needed: 2** (labeled R1, R2).

**Step 3 — Generate code using the algorithm:**

Since both sides need 2 registers, we must pick a side to evaluate first. By convention, evaluate the **left** (or higher-labeled in tie) first.

```
MOV R1, a       ; R1 = a
ADD R1, b       ; R1 = a + b
MOV R2, c       ; R2 = c
ADD R2, d       ; R2 = c + d
SUB R1, R2      ; R1 = (a+b) - (c+d)
```

**Code size: 5 instructions. Memory traffic: 4 loads + 0 stores = 4.** Perfect — no spills.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Generating a Conditional Jump

**Source TAC:**
```
if x > 0 goto L1
t1 = 0
goto L2
L1: t1 = 1
L2: print t1
```

**Step-by-step translation:**

1. The TAC `if x > 0 goto L1` is a conditional jump. Apply the pattern:
   - `MOV R1, x` — load $x$
   - `CMP R1, 0` — set flags
   - `JG L1` — jump if greater (signed)
2. The TAC `t1 = 0` generates `MOV R, 0`.
3. The TAC `goto L2` generates `JMP L2`.
4. The TAC `t1 = 1` generates `MOV R, 1`.
5. The label `L2:` and `print t1` are emitted as-is or via a `PRINT` pseudo-op.

**Generated assembly:**

```asm
        MOV  AX, [x]     ; load x
        CMP  AX, 0       ; compare with 0
        JG   L1          ; jump if x > 0
        MOV  BX, 0       ; t1 = 0
        JMP  L2          ; goto L2
L1:     MOV  BX, 1       ; t1 = 1
L2:     ; print t1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The `if x > 0 goto L1` is decomposed into **3 instructions** (load, compare, jump) because the source-level relational operator `>` must be implemented via the CPU's flag-based comparison mechanism.

## 3.5 Full Algorithm — Code Generation Pseudocode (KTU Reference)

The following is the **canonical, examinable pseudocode** for a simple TAC → Assembly generator. KTU students are expected to know this *cold*.

```text
// Input:  TAC instructions in a list T[1..n]
// Output: Assembly instructions emitted to output

Initialize reg_desc[1..k] = empty for all k registers
Initialize addr_desc[name] = ∅ for all names
Compute next-use for all names by backward scan

for i = 1 to n:
    read T[i] = (op, arg1, arg2, result)
    
    switch (op):
        case "=":        // x = y
            R = getReg(T[i])
            emit("MOV", R, lookup(arg1))
            addr_desc[result] = {R}
            reg_desc[R] = {result}
            break
            
        case "+", "-", "*", "/":  // x = y op z
            R = getReg(T[i])
            Ry = lookup(arg1)        // where y lives
            if Ry is a register R2:
                emit("MOV", R, R2)
            else:
                emit("MOV", R, M[arg1_loc])
            
            if z is a constant:
                emit(op, R, constant)
            elif z is in register Rz:
                emit(op, R, Rz)
            else:
                emit("MOV", tmp, M[z_loc])
                emit(op, R, tmp)
            
            addr_desc[result] = {R}
            reg_desc[R] = {result}
            break
            
        case "goto":
            emit("JMP", arg1)   // arg1 is the label
            break
            
        case "if", "ifFalse":   // if x relop y goto L
            R = lookup(arg1)
            emit("MOV", R, M[arg1_loc])   // load x
            emit("CMP", R, M[arg2_loc])   // compare with y
            emit(jumpMnemonic[relop], arg1) // JE, JNE, JG, JL, JGE, JLE
            break
            
    end switch
end for
```

## 3.6 Worked Example — Full Mini-Program End-to-End

**Input TAC (post-semantic-analysis):**

```
t1 = a + b
t2 = t1 * c
t3 = t2 - d
if t3 > 0 goto L1
x = 0
goto L2
L1: x = 1
L2: halt
```

**Output Assembly (using registers R1, R2, R3):**

```asm
        MOV   R1, a       ; t1 = a + b
        ADD   R1, b
        MOV   R2, R1      ; t2 = t1 * c
        MUL   R2, c
        MOV   R3, R2      ; t3 = t2 - d
        SUB   R3, d
        MOV   R1, R3      ; load t3 for compare
        CMP   R1, 0
        JG    L1
        MOV   x, 0        ; x = 0
        JMP   L2
L1:     MOV   x, 1        ; x = 1
L2:     HALT
```

**Code size = 12 instructions. Memory traffic = 8 (loads) + 0 (stores, because everything stays in regs) = 8.** This is **near-optimal** for the given register set of 3.

## 3.7 Mathematical Bound — Lower Bound on Code Size

**Theorem:** For an expression with $n$ binary operators and $k$ available registers, the minimum code size is:

$$
\vert Code \vert_{\min} = n + \text{(number of distinct leaves)} + \text{spill\_cost}
$$

where $\text{spill\_cost}$ = number of times we must write a register to memory because no free register exists.

**Derivation for a balanced tree with $n$ binary ops and $k$ registers:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ops} &= n \\
\text{leaves} &= n + 1 \\
\text{loads} &= n + 1 \quad \text{(each leaf loaded once)} \\
\text{arithmetic emits} &= n \\
\text{total} &= 2n + 1 + \text{spill\_cost}
\end{aligned}
$$

For our example $(a+b)-(c+d)$, $n = 3$ (three `+`/`-` ops), leaves = 4.

$$
\vert Code \vert_{\min} = 2(3) + 1 + 0 = 7
$$

Our generator emitted 5 instructions for the expression part (excluding label and halt), which is **better** because we reused registers efficiently.

> [!WARNING]
> If a student emits 8+ instructions for this case, it indicates a bug — likely loading both operands into the *same* register without preserving the first.
