# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors. Give two examples for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — key points only):**

| Parameter | Type-1 (Bare-Metal) | Type-2 (Hosted) |
|---|---|---|
| Installation | Directly on hardware | As an application on host OS |
| Performance | Near-native (95–98%) | Reduced (80–90%) |
| Examples | KVM, Xen, ESXi, Hyper-V, Proxmox | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels |
| Use case | Production datacenters | Desktop development, labs |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Type-1 hypervisors are preferred for production VM clusters because they eliminate the host-OS overhead, providing better performance, stability, and direct hardware access.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Supplementary]**
**List any three open-source hypervisors suitable for setting up a VM cluster in a B.Tech lab. State one key feature of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Proxmox VE** — Integrated web-based cluster management with built-in Corosync/Pacemaker HA, Ceph storage, and live migration. *(1 mark)*
2. **KVM (Kernel-based Virtual Machine)** — Native Linux kernel module, leverages hardware virtualization extensions (VT-x/AMD-V), near-native performance. *(1 mark)*
3. **XCP-ng** — Community-driven fork of Citrix XenServer; supports Xen hypervisor with strong enterprise features (pooling, HA, distributed virtual switching). *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In Part B cluster questions, students often *describe* steps but fail to include the **actual commands** or **configuration snippets**. Always pair each procedural step with a code block or command. Also, students frequently skip the **"verification"** step (e.g., `pvecm status` output) — this is where 2–3 marks are routinely lost.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester Evaluation]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat architectural diagram, explain the **three-tier architecture of a virtual machine cluster** and the role of each tier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design and document the **step-by-step procedure to set up a 3-node Proxmox VE cluster** using shared NFS storage. Include all necessary commands and verification steps. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

The three-tier architecture of a VM cluster consists of:

**Tier 1 — Compute Tier (Hypervisor + VMs):** The bottom-most layer comprises the physical hosts running Type-1 hypervisors (e.g., Proxmox VE). Each host runs multiple guest VMs. The hypervisor abstracts CPU, RAM, and I/O devices, presenting virtual resources to each guest. *(2 marks)*

**Tier 2 — Coordination & Storage Tier (Cluster Middleware + Shared Storage):** The middle layer provides the "glue" that binds independent hosts into a single cluster. It consists of:
- **Cluster membership service** (e.g., Corosync) — maintains a consistent view of all nodes and their liveness via heartbeats. *(1 mark)*
- **HA policy engine** (e.g., Pacemaker) — decides which node should host which VM and orchestrates failover. *(1 mark)*
- **Shared storage** (e.g., NFS, Ceph, iSCSI) — provides a unified, location-transparent storage pool that all nodes can read/write. *(1 mark)*

**Tier 3 — Management & Application Tier (GUI + Workloads):** The top layer provides:
- A **centralized web-based GUI** (Proxmox GUI on port 8006) for administrators to monitor and control the cluster. *(1 mark)*
- The **guest application workloads** (Hadoop, Kubernetes, web servers, databases) that benefit from the cluster's HA and scalability. *(1 mark)*

*(Diagram drawn from Section 4.2 of these notes — refer to it for full credit.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Install Proxmox VE on 3 Physical Hosts** *(1 mark)*
Download the ISO from `proxmox.com`. Boot each host, install with hostname `pve1.lab.local`, `pve2.lab.local`, `pve3.lab.local`. Configure static IPs on the management network (e.g., `10.0.0.11/24`, `10.0.0.12/24`, `10.0.0.13/24`).

**Step 2 — Configure Network Bridges** *(1 mark)*
On each node, edit `/etc/network/interfaces` to ensure `vmbr0` is the management bridge and create `vmbr1` for VM guest traffic. Restart networking with `systemctl restart networking`.

**Step 3 — Set Up NFS Shared Storage** *(1 mark)*
On a separate storage VM (or one of the PVE nodes), install NFS:
```
apt update && apt install nfs-kernel-server -y
mkdir -p /srv/nfs
echo "/srv/nfs 10.0.0.0/24(rw,sync,no_subtree_check,no_root_squash)" >> /etc/exports
exportfs -a
systemctl enable --now nfs-server
```

**Step 4 — Initialize the Cluster on Node 1** *(1 mark)*
On `pve1`, run:
```
pvecm create mycluster
pvecm status
```
Expected output: 1 node, expected votes = 1, quorum = 1.

**Step 5 — Join Nodes 2 and 3 to the Cluster** *(1 mark)*
On `pve2`:
```
pvecm add pve1.lab.local -link0 10.10.10.12
```
On `pve3`:
```
pvecm add pve1.lab.local -link0 10.10.10.13
```

**Step 6 — Add NFS Storage to the Datacenter** *(1 mark)*
In Proxmox GUI: Datacenter → Storage → Add → NFS. Fill in:
- ID: `nfs-cluster`
- Server: `10.0.0.50`
- Export: `/srv/nfs`
- Content: `Disk image, ISO image, Snippets`

**Step 7 — Deploy VMs and Enable HA** *(1 mark)*
Create 3 VMs (one per node) using an Ubuntu 22.04 ISO. Distribute VMs across the 3 nodes. Right-click each VM → HA → Add to HA. Verify with:
```
ha-manager status
```
Expected: All 3 VMs listed as "started" and "monitored".

**Verification Snapshot (must include in lab record):**
```
$ pvecm status
Cluster information
Name:             mycluster
Config Version:   3
Transport:        knet
Secure auth:      on

Quorum information
Date:             Mon Jan 15 10:00:00 2024
Quorum provider:  corosync_votequorum
Nodes:            3
Expected votes:   3
Total votes:      3
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, End Semester Evaluation]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **deployment of a VM cluster using open-source hypervisors (on-premises)** vs. **public cloud platforms (IaaS)**. Cover at least 4 parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete **procedure to set up a 3-node Kubernetes (K3s) cluster on AWS free-tier EC2 instances**, including VPC configuration, security group rules, and K3s installation commands. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

| Parameter | On-Prem Open-Source | Public Cloud IaaS |
|---|---|---|
| **Capital Expenditure (CapEx)** | High — servers, networking gear, UPS, cooling | Zero — pay-as-you-go |
| **Operational Expenditure (OpEx)** | Power, cooling, admin staff | Pay per vCPU-hour, GB-hour, GB-month |
| **Time to Deploy** | Days to weeks (procurement, racking) | Minutes (API call) |
| **Scalability** | Limited by physical hardware; weeks to add nodes | Elastic — add/remove VMs in seconds via API |
| **High Availability** | Manual setup of redundant power, network, storage | Multi-AZ deployments built-in |
| **Control & Customization** | Full control over hypervisor, kernel, hardware | Limited — "noisy neighbor", restricted hypervisor access |
| **Free Tier Suitability (KTU)** | Ideal — zero cost for B.Tech labs | Limited — 1 vCPU/1 GB tier only |

*(Fill 7 rows × 1 mark = 7 marks.)*

**Conclusion (must include):** For a B.Tech lab environment, **open-source on-premises** is preferred due to zero cost, full control, and the ability to demonstrate cluster internals (Corosync, Pacemaker, Ceph). For production enterprise workloads, **public cloud IaaS** offers unmatched scalability and resilience.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Create a VPC and Subnet** *(1 mark)*
In AWS Console: VPC → Create VPC → Name `k3s-vpc`, CIDR `10.0.0.0/16`. Create a public subnet `10.0.1.0/24` in availability zone `ap-south-1a`. Attach an Internet Gateway and configure the route table to allow `0.0.0.0/0` outbound.

**Step 2 — Launch 3 EC2 t2.micro Instances** *(1 mark)*
Use Ubuntu 22.04 LTS AMI. Configure:
- Subnet: `k3s-subnet`
- Auto-assign Public IP: Enabled
- Key pair: `k3s-key.pem`

Tag instances as `k3s-server`, `k3s-worker1`, `k3s-worker2`.

**Step 3 — Configure Security Group** *(1 mark)*
Create SG `k3s-sg` with inbound rules:

| Type | Port | Source | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| SSH | 22 | My IP | Admin access |
| Custom TCP | 6443 | 10.0.0.0/16 | K3s API server |
| Custom TCP | 10250 | 10.0.0.0/16 | Kubelet metrics |
| Custom UDP | 8472 | 10.0.0.0/16 | Flannel VXLAN |
| All Traffic | All | Self | Inter-node communication |

**Step 4 — Install K3s Server** *(1 mark)*
On `k3s-server` (as `ubuntu` user):
```
curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | sh -s - server --node-external-ip <SERVER_PUBLIC_IP>
sudo cat /var/lib/rancher/k3s/server/node-token  # Save this token
```

**Step 5 — Join Worker Nodes** *(1 mark)*
On `k3s-worker1` and `k3s-worker2`:
```
curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | sh -s - agent \
  --server https://<SERVER_PRIVATE_IP>:6443 \
  --token <NODE_TOKEN>
```

**Step 6 — Verify Cluster** *(1 mark)*
On `k3s-server`:
```
sudo kubectl get nodes
```
Expected output:
```
NAME           STATUS   ROLES                  AGE   VERSION
k3s-server     Ready    control-plane,master   2m    v1.28.x
k3s-worker1    Ready    <none>                 1m    v1.28.x
k3s-worker2    Ready    <none>                 1m    v1.28.x
```

**Step 7 — Deploy a Sample Workload** *(1 mark)*
```
sudo kubectl create deployment nginx --image=nginx
sudo kubectl expose deployment nginx --port=80 --type=LoadBalancer
sudo kubectl get svc nginx  # Note the EXTERNAL-IP
```
Test the cluster by accessing the EXTERNAL-IP from a browser. Successful response confirms the cluster is fully operational.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Exam Day**

### **Core Concepts (Memorize These)**

- ✅ **Virtualization** = abstraction of physical resources via a hypervisor
- ✅ **Type-1 (bare-metal)** = directly on hardware → KVM, Xen, ESXi, Proxmox, Hyper-V
- ✅ **Type-2 (hosted)** = on host OS → VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels
- ✅ **VM cluster** = 2+ VMs coordinated by middleware for HA, LB, and scalability
- ✅ **High Availability (HA)** = automatic failover on node failure
- ✅ **Live migration** = moving a running VM between hosts with zero downtime
- ✅ **STONITH** = "Shoot The Other Node In The Head" — fencing mechanism to prevent split-brain
- ✅ **Quorum** = majority vote (N/2 + 1) required for cluster decisions
- ✅ **Odd number of nodes** = always use 3, 5, 7 to break ties
- ✅ **Shared storage** = NFS (lab), iSCSI (prod-block), Ceph (distributed), GlusterFS (replicated)

### **Critical Formulas (Memorize All 8 from Section 2.4)**

- ✅ VM density by RAM: $N_{vm} = \lfloor (RAM_{host} - RAM_{hv}) / (RAM_{vm} + RAM_{oh}) \rfloor$
- ✅ VM density by CPU: $N_{vm} = \lfloor (vCPU \times T) / (vCPU_{vm} \times O) \rfloor$
- ✅ Storage provisioning: $S_{phys} = S_{logic} \times (1 + R_{prov})$
- ✅ HA failover time: $T_{fail} = T_{detect} + T_{fence} + T_{boot}$
- ✅ Cluster availability: $U_{cl} = 1 - \prod (1 - U_i)$
- ✅ Live migration time: $T_{mig} = M_{vm} / B_{net} + T_{sync}$

### **Commands You Must Remember**

- ✅ `pvecm create <name>` — initialize cluster
- ✅ `pvecm addnode <peer>` — join node to cluster
- ✅ `pvecm status` — check cluster health
- ✅ `ha-manager status` — check HA resource state
- ✅ `qm create <vmid>` — create a VM (Proxmox)
- ✅ `virsh list --all` — list VMs (KVM/libvirt)
- ✅ `kubectl get nodes` — verify K3s/K8s cluster

### **Common Mistakes to Avoid (Exam Day)**

- ❌ Reporting only RAM-based VM count — always check **CPU bottleneck**
- ❌ Forgetting to include **verification commands** in procedure answers
- ❌ Confusing **Type-1** with **Type-2** examples
- ❌ Forgetting that **NFS requires** `no_root_squash` for Proxmox to write disk images
- ❌ Designing a **2-node cluster** without a tie-breaker (quorum disk)
- ❌ Not mentioning **STONITH/fencing** in HA answers

### **Key Numbers to Remember**

- 📌 Proxmox web GUI: **port 8006**
- 📌 K3s API server: **port 6443**
- 📌 NFS: **port 2049** (TCP)
- 📌 iSCSI: **port 3260**
- 📌 Corosync heartbeat: **UDP 5405**
- 📌 Default STONITH timeout: **30 seconds**
- 📌 Recommended oversubscription: **4:1 vCPU ratio** (general workloads)

### **One-Line Definitions for Last-Minute Revision**

- 📝 **Hypervisor:** Software that creates and manages virtual machines.
- 📝 **VM Cluster:** A group of VMs acting as one logical compute unit.
- 📝 **Quorum:** Minimum number of nodes required for cluster operation.
- 📝 **Live Migration:** Moving a running VM between physical hosts without downtime.
- 📝 **Fencing (STONITH):** Isolating a failed node to prevent data corruption.
- 📝 **Shared Storage:** Storage accessible by all cluster nodes simultaneously.
- 📝 **Oversubscription:** Allocating more virtual resources than physically available.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** If asked to "set up a VM cluster", always present your answer in the **3-phase structure**: (1) Infrastructure preparation (hypervisor install, network), (2) Cluster formation (middleware, quorum), (3) Workload deployment + verification. This structure matches KTU's marking scheme and guarantees full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Setting host name for virtual machine nodes in cluster and ssh set up for remote login
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCSL607 - SYSTEMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Setting host name for virtual machine nodes in cluster and ssh set up for remote login ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In a distributed computing cluster, a **Virtual Machine (VM) node** is a guest operating system instance running on a hypervisor (e.g., Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, KVM) that participates as a worker or master unit in a High-Performance Computing (HPC) or cloud-style cluster. The **hostname** is the unique human-readable alphanumeric label assigned to a node so that other nodes, network services, and the DNS resolver can identify and route requests to it. **SSH (Secure Shell)** is a cryptographic network protocol (defined in RFC 4251–4254) that operates on **TCP port 22** and provides authenticated, encrypted, bidirectional communication between two networked hosts. The SSH setup for a cluster mandates: 1. A unique, persistent **Fully Qualified Domain Name (FQDN)** on each node. 2. Resolution of these hostnames through the local `/etc/hosts` file (since the cluster is isolated, no external DNS is used). 3. Installation and enablement of the `openssh-server` daemon on every node. 4. Generation of an asymmetric key pair and propagation of the **public key** to remote nodes to enable **passwordless, key-based authentication**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight (CO Mapping):** This experiment maps to **CO2 – *Configure networked services for distributed/clustered environments*** under the Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels: **Apply (Level 3)** and **Analyse (Level 4)**. Students are expected to *execute* the configuration AND *interpret* the network behaviour (e.g., why a hostname fails to resolve, why SSH asks for a password). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine a new office building with **four floors**. Before the receptionist can forward your phone call to a colleague on the 4th floor, two things must happen: 1. Every floor must be **labelled** — "Floor 1 – Accounts", "Floor 2 – HR", "Floor 3 – R&D", "Floor 4 – Servers". This label is the **hostname**. 2. The receptionist needs a **private directory** that maps every label to a direct phone number. This is the **`/etc/hosts`** file. 3. The 4th-floor engineer wants a **pre-approved security badge** so he can walk into the server room without signing a register every single time. This badge is the **SSH key pair** — the public key is the badge scanner's permission list, the private key is the engineer's actual badge. So, in our cluster: - **Hostname** = the "name tag" worn by each VM node. - **`/etc/hosts`** = the in-house phone directory shared by all nodes. - **SSH key pair** = the cryptographic badge that lets nodes trust each other without passwords. > [!NOTE] > **Physical constants / standard values to remember:** > - SSH listens on **TCP port 22** (default; configurable in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`). > - Default key algorithm: **RSA 2048-bit** (or **ED25519 256-bit** for stronger security). > - Default hostname limit: **64 characters**, must match RFC 1123 (alphanumeric + hyphens, no underscores at the start). --- ### 1.3 Why This Step Is Foundational In any cluster (Hadoop, Kubernetes, Slurm, MPI, Proxmox, Eucalyptus, OpenStack), the master node orchestrates worker nodes *by name*. If two nodes share a hostname, the master cannot distinguish them. If a node's hostname does not resolve from a peer, the SSH connection will hang and eventually fail with *"Could not resolve hostname"*. Hence, this experiment is the **Gatekeeper Experiment** of the entire lab — no Hadoop, no Kube, no MPI will work without it being done correctly. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown #### Phase A — Hostname Configuration (3 Sub-Steps) 1. **Inspect Current Hostname** The kernel maintains a transient hostname in memory. Reading it confirms the present state and validates that the change is needed. Command family: `hostname`, `hostnamectl status`, `cat /proc/sys/kernel/hostname`. 2. **Set the Static Hostname** The static hostname is written by `systemd-hostnamed` to `/etc/hostname` and persists across reboots. `hostnamectl set-hostname ` is the modern, atomic, distribution-agnostic way to do it. On legacy SysV-init systems, one would manually echo into `/etc/hostname`. 3. **Update the Local Name Resolution File** `/etc/hosts` is consulted *before* DNS for every name lookup. For a private cluster, every node's hostname–IP mapping must be added to **every** node's `/etc/hosts` so that cross-node name resolution is immediate and offline. Format: ` `. #### Phase B — SSH Server Installation (2 Sub-Steps) 1. **Package Installation** `openssh-server` is the daemon; `openssh-client` is the user tool. On Debian/Ubuntu (most common in KTU labs): `sudo apt install openssh-server`. On RHEL/CentOS: `sudo yum install openssh-server`. The daemon is `sshd`. 2. **Daemon Enablement & Startup** `sudo systemctl enable ssh` creates a symlink so sshd auto-starts on every boot. `sudo systemctl start ssh` activates it immediately. `sudo systemctl status ssh` confirms the state. #### Phase C — SSH Key-Based Authentication (4 Sub-Steps) 1. **Generate the Key Pair** `ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 2048` produces a private key (`~/.ssh/id_rsa`) and a public key (`~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub`). The private key **must never leave the local node**. Default permissions: private key `600`, directory `700`. 2. **Propagate the Public Key** `ssh-copy-id user@nodeN` appends the public key into the remote node's `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` file. The `-i` flag explicitly specifies the key file. 3. **Restrict Permissions on the Remote Side** The remote `~/.ssh` directory must be `700`, and `authorized_keys` must be `600`. If permissions are too lax, the SSH daemon *refuses* the key (this is by design — it prevents key theft from world-readable directories). 4. **Test the Connection** `ssh user@nodeN "hostname"` should log in non-interactively and print the remote hostname without prompting for a password. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" behind Key-Based Auth:** Passwords are vulnerable to brute-force, shoulder-surfing, and keylogging. A 2048-bit RSA key has ~$2^{2048}$ possible values — brute force is computationally infeasible. The protocol uses a **challenge-response** handshake where the client proves it holds the private key *without ever transmitting it* on the wire. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Table Because this experiment is procedural (not analytical), the "formula" equivalent is a **Command & Configuration Reference Table**. This is the high-yield surface KTU examiners test. | # | Operation | Command / Path | Effect / Output | |---|-----------|----------------|-----------------| | 1 | View current hostname | `hostname` or `hostnamectl` | Prints transient hostname | | 2 | Set persistent hostname | `sudo hostnamectl set-hostname nodeN` | Writes to `/etc/hostname` | | 3 | Local name resolution | `sudo nano /etc/hosts` | Add ` ` | | 4 | Install SSH server | `sudo apt install openssh-server` | Installs `sshd` | | 5 | Enable SSH at boot | `sudo systemctl enable ssh` | Creates systemd symlink | | 6 | Start SSH now | `sudo systemctl start ssh` | Launches daemon on port 22 | | 7 | Verify SSH listening | `sudo ss -tlnp \vert grep :22` | Confirms port 22 is open | | 8 | Generate RSA key pair | `ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 2048` | Creates `id_rsa` + `id_rsa.pub` | | 9 | Push public key to peer | `ssh-copy-id user@nodeN` | Adds key to `authorized_keys` | | 10 | Manual key copy (alt) | `cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub \vert ssh user@nodeN "mkdir -p ~/.ssh && chmod 700 ~/.ssh && cat >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys && chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys"` | When `ssh-copy-id` unavailable | | 11 | Test login | `ssh user@nodeN` | Should not ask password | | 12 | Test non-interactive | `ssh user@nodeN hostname` | Returns remote hostname | | 13 | Cluster-wide /etc/hosts | Edit on **every** node | Cluster-wide name resolution | | 14 | SSH config shortcut | `nano ~/.ssh/config` | Define `Host` aliases | | 15 | Allow firewall traffic | `sudo ufw allow 22/tcp` or `sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=ssh` | Permit SSH through firewall | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall #1:** Students often edit `/etc/hosts` on only **one** node. The `/etc/hosts` file is **per-node local** — there is no automatic sync. You must edit it on **every node** in the cluster. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **DevOps / SRE:** Every Ansible playbook, every Kubernetes kubelet registration, every Hadoop `slaves`/`workers` file relies on hostname resolution. - **Cloud Computing (AWS, Azure, GCP):** EC2 instances are launched with user-data scripts that perform *exactly* this experiment at boot — `hostnamectl set-hostname` + writing peers to `/etc/hosts` + pushing SSH keys via cloud-init. - **Edge & IoT:** Lightweight K3s clusters on Raspberry Pi nodes perform the same `hostnamectl` + `/etc/hosts` step. - **High-Performance Computing (HPC):** MPI jobs (OpenMPI, MPICH) use `mpirun -hosts node1,node2,node3 ./a.out` — the host list is resolved via `/etc/hosts`. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Configuration Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Assumption Set (Lab Environment) For KTU lab uniformity, the following configuration is assumed. Your examiner may vary the subnet, but the *method* is identical. | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Hypervisor | Oracle VirtualBox 7.x (or VMware) | | Network Mode | Host-Only Adapter (or Internal Network) | | Subnet | 192.168.56.0/24 | | Master node | `node1` (192.168.56.11) | | Worker 1 | `node2` (192.168.56.12) | | Worker 2 | `node3` (192.168.56.13) | | OS | Ubuntu 22.04 LTS Server (recommended for KTU labs) | | User account | `keralastudent` (non-root, with `sudo`) | | SSH version | OpenSSH 8.9+ | --- ### 3.2 Phase A — Hostname Configuration (Detailed Walkthrough) #### Step A.1 — Verify Pre-Change State ```bash hostname hostnamectl status ``` You will see the auto-generated `ubuntu` or `localhost` name. This is the kernel transient value. > [!NOTE] > **Why verify first?** Demonstrates a methodical engineering approach. KTU examiners reward "Observe → Hypothesize → Modify → Verify" workflow. #### Step A.2 — Set the Static Hostname On **node1**, run: ```bash sudo hostnamectl set-hostname node1 ``` On **node2**, run: ```bash sudo hostnamectl set-hostname node2 ``` On **node3**, run: ```bash sudo hostnamectl set-hostname node3 ``` The change is **immediate** in the current shell after the next prompt refresh, and **persistent** because `systemd-hostnamed` rewrites `/etc/hostname`. No reboot required. Verify: ```bash hostnamectl status | grep -i "static hostname" ``` Expected output on node1: ``` Static hostname: node1 ``` #### Step A.3 — Edit `/etc/hosts` on **Every Node** Open the file on **each** node: ```bash sudo nano /etc/hosts ``` Append the following block at the bottom of the file on **every** node (substitute your actual usernames and IPs): ``` 192.168.56.11 node1.cluster.lab node1 192.168.56.12 node2.cluster.lab node2 192.168.56.13 node3.cluster.lab node3 ``` Save and exit (`Ctrl+O`, `Enter`, `Ctrl+X`). Test resolution from each node: ```bash ping -c 2 node1 ping -c 2 node2 ping -c 2 node3 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analytical Insight (Bloom's Level 4 – Analyse):** The order in `/etc/hosts` does not matter, but the entries must be **mutually consistent across all nodes**. If node1 says "192.168.56.12 = node2" but node3 says "192.168.56.12 = node4", the cluster has a *split-brain naming* condition. This is a classic KTU exam question. --- ### 3.3 Phase B — SSH Server Installation & Enablement #### Step B.1 — Install on **Every Node** ```bash sudo apt update sudo apt install -y openssh-server openssh-client ``` The `-y` flag auto-confirms the APT prompt (use with care in production, but ideal for lab scripts). #### Step B.2 — Enable and Start ```bash sudo systemctl enable ssh sudo systemctl start ssh sudo systemctl status ssh ``` The `status` command should show `active (running)` in green. #### Step B.3 — Verify Port 22 is Listening ```bash sudo ss -tlnp | grep :22 ``` Expected output (truncated): ``` LISTEN 0 128 0.0.0.0:22 0.0.0.0:* users:(("sshd",pid=812,fd=3)) ``` If you see `127.0.0.1:22` instead of `0.0.0.0:22`, sshd is bound to loopback only. Edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`, set `ListenAddress 0.0.0.0`, then `sudo systemctl restart ssh`. --- ### 3.4 Phase C — Key-Based Authentication Setup #### Step C.1 — Generate Keys on **Every Node** (Required for Bidirectional Trust) > [!NOTE] > **Why on every node?** A full-mesh key trust lets *any* node SSH to *any* other node. This is required for Hadoop, MPI, and Kube cluster members to communicate bidirectionally without password prompts. ```bash ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 2048 ``` When prompted: - `Enter file in which to save the key (/home/keralastudent/.ssh/id_rsa):` → press Enter (accept default). - `Enter passphrase (empty for no passphrase):` → press Enter twice (lab convention; production should use a passphrase + ssh-agent). This creates: - `~/.ssh/id_rsa` (private, mode 600) - `~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub` (public, mode 644) #### Step C.2 — Push the Public Key from Each Node to All Other Nodes From **node1**, run: ```bash ssh-copy-id keralastudent@node2 ssh-copy-id keralastudent@node3 ``` Accept the host-key fingerprint on first contact (`yes`). Enter the *target* node's password once per target. The public key is appended to `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` on the target. Repeat from **node2** → node1, node3, and from **node3** → node1, node2. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Alternative Manual Method (no `ssh-copy-id`):** This is exam-favourite because it tests the student's understanding of the *underlying file plumbing*: ```bash cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub | ssh keralastudent@node2 \ "mkdir -p ~/.ssh && \ chmod 700 ~/.ssh && \ cat >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys && \ chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys" ``` This single pipeline performs four operations atomically: 1. Creates `~/.ssh` if absent. 2. Sets `700` permission. 3. Appends the public key. 4. Sets `600` permission on the key file. The double-quoted block executes *on the remote node* via SSH; the `cat` on the left side is executed *locally* and piped over the wire. #### Step C.3 — Test the Connections From node1: ```bash ssh keralastudent@node2 "hostname && date" ``` Expected: prints `node2` and the current date, **with no password prompt**. Repeat for all pairs to verify a full mesh. --- ### 3.5 Optional Enhancement — `~/.ssh/config` Shortcut On **every node**, create `~/.ssh/config`: ```bash nano ~/.ssh/config ``` ``` Host node1 HostName 192.168.56.11 User keralastudent IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_rsa Host node2 HostName 192.168.56.12 User keralastudent IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_rsa Host node3 HostName 192.168.56.13 User keralastudent IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_rsa ``` Set strict permissions: ```bash chmod 600 ~/.ssh/config ``` Now you can simply type `ssh node2` instead of `ssh keralastudent@192.168.56.12`. --- ### 3.6 Verification Matrix (Analytical Summary) | Test | Command | Expected Result | Failure Diagnosis | |------|---------|-----------------|-------------------| | Hostname is persistent | `hostnamectl` | `Static hostname: node1` | Run `hostnamectl set-hostname node1` again | | Peer resolves by name | `ping -c1 node2` | 0% packet loss, reply from 192.168.56.12 | Re-check `/etc/hosts` on **both** ends | | SSH daemon active | `systemctl is-active ssh` | `active` | `sudo systemctl start ssh` | | Port 22 listening on 0.0.0.0 | `ss -tlnp \| grep :22` | `0.0.0.0:22` | Edit `sshd_config`, restart | | Passwordless SSH | `ssh node2 hostname` | Prints `node2` | Check `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` perms (must be 600) and directory perms (must be 700) | | Bidirectional trust | SSH from every node to every other | No password prompt | `ssh-copy-id` may have only been run one way | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall #2:** If a student sets up keys only from `node1 → node2` but not `node2 → node1`, jobs orchestrated from node2 back to node1 will *still ask for a password*. KTU examiners frequently test this with a question like: *"You can SSH from A to B, but not from B to A. Diagnose."* Answer: keys were pushed in one direction only. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Cluster Architecture Overview ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Hypervisor["ORACLE VIRTUALBOX HOST MACHINE"] subgraph ClusterNet["HOST-ONLY NETWORK 192.168.56.0/24"] nodeA["NODE1 MASTER
192.168.56.11
Hostname: node1
Role: NameNode / Master"] nodeB["NODE2 WORKER
192.168.56.12
Hostname: node2
Role: DataNode / Worker"] nodeC["NODE3 WORKER
192.168.56.13
Hostname: node3
Role: DataNode / Worker"] end ethA["eth0: 192.168.56.11"] ethB["eth0: 192.168.56.12"] ethC["eth0: 192.168.56.13"] end user["ADMIN WORKSTATION
SSH Client Terminal"] nodeA --- ethA nodeB --- ethB nodeC --- ethC user -.SSH.-> nodeA nodeA -.SSH KEY.-> nodeB nodeA -.SSH KEY.-> nodeC nodeB -.SSH KEY.-> nodeA nodeB -.SSH KEY.-> nodeC nodeC -.SSH KEY.-> nodeA nodeC -.SSH KEY.-> nodeB ``` **Reading the diagram:** Solid lines are physical/virtual NIC connections. Dashed lines are SSH logical channels. Every node has a full mesh of SSH keys — this is *bidirectional trust*. --- ### 4.2 SSH Public-Key Authentication Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant Client as SSH Client (Node1) participant Network as TCP/IP Network participant Server as SSHD Daemon (Node2) Client->>Server: TCP SYN on port 22 Server->>Client: TCP SYN-ACK Client->>Server: TCP ACK (connection established) Server->>Client: SSH Version String + Host Key (RSA/ED25519) Client->>Client: Verify Host Key against known_hosts Client->>Server: Key Exchange Init (KEXINIT) Server->>Client: KEXINIT Note over Client,Server: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange
Session key derived (symmetric AES-256) Client->>Server: Authentication Request: publickey (sends key fingerprint) Server->>Client: Authentication Challenge (random nonce) Note over Client: Sign nonce with PRIVATE key
(never sent over wire) Client->>Server: Send signed nonce Server->>Server: Verify signature using PUBLIC key from authorized_keys Server->>Client: Authentication Accepted Note over Client,Server: Encrypted session established
User shell granted Client->>Server: Execute command e.g. hostname Server->>Client: Return output ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Takeaway (Bloom's Level 4 – Analyse):** Notice that the **private key NEVER leaves the client machine**. The server only ever sees the *signature* of a challenge — the private key is mathematically bound to the public key, but extracting one from the other is computationally infeasible (RSA factoring problem). This is the cryptographic heart of "passwordless" login. --- ### 4.3 File-Level Configuration Map ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Node1["NODE1 FILESYSTEM"] h1["/etc/hostname
Contains: node1"] e1["/etc/hosts
Contains: 192.168.56.11 node1 etc"] k1["~/.ssh/id_rsa
PRIVATE KEY mode 600"] p1["~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub
PUBLIC KEY"] a1["~/.ssh/authorized_keys
Contains public keys of node2 and node3"] c1["~/.ssh/config
Host aliases for cluster"] end subgraph Node2["NODE2 FILESYSTEM"] h2["/etc/hostname
Contains: node2"] e2["/etc/hosts
Contains all 3 cluster IPs"] k2["~/.ssh/id_rsa
PRIVATE KEY"] a2["~/.ssh/authorized_keys
Contains public keys of node1 and node3"] end subgraph Node3["NODE3 FILESYSTEM"] h3["/etc/hostname
Contains: node3"] e3["/etc/hosts
Contains all 3 cluster IPs"] k3["~/.ssh/id_rsa
PRIVATE KEY"] a3["~/.ssh/authorized_keys
Contains public keys of node1 and node2"] end p1 -.COPY.-> a2 p1 -.COPY.-> a3 p2["./id_rsa.pub on node2"] -.COPY.-> a1 p2 -.COPY.-> a3 p3["./id_rsa.pub on node3"] -.COPY.-> a1 p3 -.COPY.-> a2 ``` This file-level map answers the KTU exam favourite: *"List all the files modified in the cluster SSH setup experiment and state the purpose of each."* ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** **Q1.** Differentiate between transient hostname and static hostname in a Linux system. Which command sets the static hostname persistently? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Transient hostname:** A kernel-level, in-memory hostname set by the OS at boot; lost on reboot unless mirrored to static (1 mark). - **Static hostname:** The persistent hostname stored in `/etc/hostname`, managed by `systemd-hostnamed`. Survives reboots (1 mark). - **Command:** `sudo hostnamectl set-hostname ` sets the static hostname atomically and persists it to `/etc/hostname` (1 mark). --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Q2.** Why is `/etc/hosts` edited on **every** node in a private cluster, even though the same content is repeated? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The `/etc/hosts` file is a **local, per-node** name resolution table. There is no automatic synchronization mechanism between nodes (1 mark). - If node1's `/etc/hosts` contains node2's IP but node3's `/etc/hosts` does not, then node3 **cannot resolve** node2's hostname, and any SSH or service from node3 to node2 will fail (1 mark). - Hence, manual editing on every node is mandatory to ensure *uniform, offline, DNS-independent* name resolution across the cluster (1 mark). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Module Internal Choice Pattern]** --- #### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Adapted]** **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the architecture of a 3-node private cluster. List the steps to set unique hostnames for each VM and configure the `/etc/hosts` file for inter-node resolution. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** 1. **Cluster Architecture Description (2 marks):** A 3-node cluster consists of: - 1 master node (`node1`, 192.168.56.11) — orchestrates jobs. - 2 worker nodes (`node2` at 192.168.56.12 and `node3` at 192.168.56.13) — execute tasks. All three VMs are connected via a VirtualBox **Host-Only Adapter** so they can communicate with each other and the host, but are isolated from external networks. 2. **Hostname Configuration Steps (2 marks):** On each node, execute: ```bash sudo hostnamectl set-hostname node1 # on node1 sudo hostnamectl set-hostname node2 # on node2 sudo hostnamectl set-hostname node3 # on node3 ``` Verify with `hostnamectl status`. 3. **/etc/hosts Configuration Steps (2 marks):** On **each** of the three nodes, open `/etc/hosts`: ```bash sudo nano /etc/hosts ``` Append: ``` 192.168.56.11 node1.cluster.lab node1 192.168.56.12 node2.cluster.lab node2 192.168.56.13 node3.cluster.lab node3 ``` Test with `ping -c 2 node1`, `ping -c 2 node2`, `ping -c 2 node3` from every node. 4. **Diagram (1 mark):** Include a cluster block diagram showing the three VMs, their IPs, and the Host-Only network (refer to Section 4.1). --- **(b)** Explain the procedure to install and configure SSH for passwordless remote login between all three nodes. List the files involved and their required permissions. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** 1. **Install SSH Server on All Nodes (1 mark):** ```bash sudo apt update sudo apt install -y openssh-server openssh-client ``` 2. **Enable and Start Daemon (1 mark):** ```bash sudo systemctl enable ssh sudo systemctl start ssh ``` 3. **Generate Key Pair on Every Node (1 mark):** ```bash ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 2048 ``` Creates `~/.ssh/id_rsa` (private) and `~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub` (public). 4. **Distribute Public Keys to All Peers (2 marks):** From each node, run `ssh-copy-id` to all other nodes. Example from node1: ```bash ssh-copy-id keralastudent@node2 ssh-copy-id keralastudent@node3 ``` Repeat from node2 and node3 to establish a **full mesh**. 5. **File Permission Table (2 marks):** | File / Directory | Required Permission | Reason | |------------------|---------------------|--------| | `~/.ssh/` | `700` | Directory must be inaccessible to other users | | `~/.ssh/id_rsa` (private) | `600` | Owner read/write only; sshd refuses if broader | | `~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub` (public) | `644` | World-readable is safe (it is public) | | `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` | `600` | sshd refuses if group/other writable | | `~/.ssh/config` | `600` | Prevents other users from reading host aliases | --- #### ❖ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted]** **(a)** Describe the SSH public-key authentication handshake in detail. Why is this method more secure than password-based authentication? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** 1. **Handshake Steps (4 marks):** - **Step 1 – TCP Connection:** Client opens a TCP connection to port 22 on the server. - **Step 2 – Protocol Negotiation:** Both sides exchange SSH version strings and supported algorithms (KEXINIT). A common key-exchange method (e.g., `curve25519-sha256`) is chosen. - **Step 3 – Key Exchange:** Diffie-Hellman key exchange produces a *shared session key* known to both ends but never transmitted. All subsequent traffic is encrypted with AES-256 (or ChaCha20). - **Step 4 – Server Authentication:** Server sends its *host key* (a long-term public key). Client verifies it against `~/.ssh/known_hosts` to detect man-in-the-middle attacks. - **Step 5 – User Authentication (Public Key):** - Client sends `ssh-rsa` + key fingerprint as the proposed auth method. - Server checks if this fingerprint matches any line in `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`. - If yes, server generates a random 256-bit nonce and sends it to the client. - Client **signs the nonce with its private key** (the private key never leaves the client). - Server **verifies the signature using the stored public key**. - If valid → user is authenticated. 2. **Security Advantages over Passwords (3 marks):** - **No secret transmission:** The private key never leaves the client machine; the password would otherwise be sent (hashed, but still vulnerable to offline brute force). - **Computational infeasibility of forgery:** A 2048-bit RSA key has 2^2048 entropy; brute force requires ~300 trillion years on current hardware. - **Resistance to phishing and keylogging:** Even if the user types the wrong "password," there is no password to steal. - **Granular revocation:** Compromised keys can be removed from `authorized_keys` instantly; password changes must propagate through every system. --- **(b)** A junior administrator sets up SSH keys from `master` to `worker1` successfully, but reports that SSH from `worker1` back to `master` still prompts for a password. Diagnose the issue, list the possible root causes, and provide a step-by-step remedy. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** 1. **Diagnosis (1 mark):** The SSH trust is **unidirectional** — keys were pushed from master to worker1, but the reverse direction (worker1 → master) was never configured. 2. **Root Cause Enumeration (3 marks):** - `ssh-copy-id keralastudent@worker1` was run from master, but the equivalent command was *not* run from worker1 to master. - The `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` file on master does not contain worker1's public key. - Possibly, the directory permissions on worker's `~/.ssh` are too lax (`chmod 755` instead of `chmod 700`), causing sshd to silently ignore the key. 3. **Remedy Steps (3 marks):** - On worker1, run: ```bash ssh-copy-id keralastudent@master ``` - Verify on master: ```bash cat ~/.ssh/authorized_keys ``` The output should contain worker1's public key. - On worker1, fix permissions if needed: ```bash chmod 700 ~/.ssh chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys ``` - Test: ```bash ssh keralastudent@master "hostname" ``` Should return `master` with no password prompt. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Examiner Insights):** > 1. **Editing `/etc/hosts` on only one node.** A surprising 30% of students do this. KTU examiners deduct 2 marks outright. *Always mention "on every node" explicitly in your answer.* > 2. **Confusing `ssh` (client) with `sshd` (server).** The package is `openssh-server`; the daemon is `sshd`. Writing "install ssh" gets 0.5 marks instead of 1. > 3. **Not mentioning permission requirements.** Stating "create authorized_keys" without specifying `chmod 600` loses 1 mark in Part B(b) of Question A. > 4. **Forgetting the firewall rule.** If `ufw` is enabled, SSH on port 22 must be explicitly allowed. Many real-world and exam scenarios test this. > 5. **Bidirectional vs unidirectional trust.** Examiners love asking "your cluster has N nodes. How many `ssh-copy-id` commands must you run for full mesh?" Answer: **N × (N−1)** — e.g., for 3 nodes, **6** commands. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Hostname command:** `sudo hostnamectl set-hostname ` — persistent; sets the static hostname in `/etc/hostname`. - **Local name resolution:** `/etc/hosts` is consulted *before* DNS. **Edit on every node.** - **SSH server package:** `openssh-server`. **Daemon:** `sshd`. **Port:** TCP **22**. - **Enable and start:** `sudo systemctl enable ssh && sudo systemctl start ssh`. - **Key generation:** `ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 2048` (or `-t ed25519` for stronger / shorter keys). - **Key distribution:** `ssh-copy-id user@peer` — appends public key to peer's `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`. - **Full-mesh key count for N nodes:** `N × (N−1)` ssh-copy-id invocations. - **Critical file permissions:** `~/.ssh` = 700, `id_rsa` = 600, `authorized_keys` = 600. Anything more permissive → sshd **refuses** the key. - **Test commands:** `ping -c 2 `, `ssh user@ hostname`, `sudo ss -tlnp | grep :22`. - **SSH config shortcut:** `~/.ssh/config` lets you write `ssh node2` instead of `ssh keralastudent@192.168.56.12`. - **Firewall:** `sudo ufw allow 22/tcp` (Ubuntu) or `sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=ssh` (RHEL). - **Bidirectional trust is mandatory** for any cluster framework (Hadoop, Kube, Slurm, MPI). - **Lab deliverable checklist (for KTU record):** 1. Screenshot of `hostnamectl` output on each node. 2. Screenshot of `/etc/hosts` on at least one node. 3. Screenshot of `systemctl status ssh` showing `active (running)`. 4. Screenshot of `ssh nodeX "hostname"` returning the peer's name without password prompt. 5. A short note (3–4 lines) explaining the role of `/etc/hosts` and SSH keys. > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Line Mantra for the Exam:** > *"Set the name, tell everyone the name, install the doorbell, give every door a key, then ring the bell from every door to every other door."*

topics.title: Copy a file from one virtual machine to another virtual machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL607 - SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Copy a file from one virtual machine to another virtual machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Virtual Machine (VM) to Virtual Machine File Copying** is the process of transferring a digital file from the local file system of one guest operating system running inside a hypervisor to the local file system of another guest operating system (on the same or a different host machine) over a logical or physical network link, using a secure file-transfer protocol such as **SSH (Secure Shell)**, **SCP (Secure Copy Protocol)**, **SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol)**, or a shared file-system service such as **NFS (Network File System)** or **SMB (Server Message Block)**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 *Systems Lab* syllabus, the experiment typically requires the student to:

1. Bring up two virtual machines (often Linux) inside a Type-2 hypervisor such as **Oracle VirtualBox** or **VMware Workstation**.
2. Configure their virtual network interfaces so that they are mutually reachable (i.e., they share an IP subnet).
3. Install and start the **OpenSSH server (`openssh-server`)** on at least one VM (the *destination*).
4. Authenticate from the *source* VM using a username/password or an **SSH key pair**.
5. Execute a file-copy command (commonly `scp`, `rsync`, or `sftp`) and verify the file on the destination VM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** The experiment is officially classified under the *Networking & System Administration* cluster of PCCSL607. The expected learning outcome aligns with **CO2**: *"Configure network services and perform file/process management across multiple systems."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Houses" Model

Imagine two independent houses (House-A and House-B) standing in the same gated colony. Each house has its own postal address, its own lock, and its own mailbox.

- **The hypervisor (VirtualBox/VMware)** is the *gated colony* — it owns the compound walls and assigns house numbers.
- **Each VM** is a *house* with its own operating system, users, and files.
- **The virtual NIC (Network Interface Card)** assigned to each VM is the *front door* of that house.
- **A network cable (virtual cable) inside the hypervisor** is the *intercom line* that connects the two front doors.
- **SSH** is the *trusted courier* who knows the lock combination of House-B; you hand him the sealed envelope (file) and he delivers it safely and verifies the recipient.
- **A password or SSH key** is the *lock combination* that authenticates the courier.
- **SCP / SFTP / rsync** are the *delivery methods* — bike, car, or van — chosen based on the size and urgency of the parcel.

So, "copying a file from one VM to another" is, in plain English: **placing a sealed envelope into a trusted courier's hands, giving him the address and the lock code of the second house, and asking him to slip it through the second mailbox — all from the comfort of the first house.**

### 1.3 Core Standards & Metrics Highlighted

| Item | Standard / Default | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **SSH default port** | **TCP 22** | Defined by IANA |
| **SCP** | Runs over **SSH protocol (RFC 4251-4254)** | Legacy, simple one-shot copy |
| **SFTP** | Subsystem of **SSH (not FTPS)** | Interactive, resume-capable |
| **NFS** | Uses **TCP/UDP 2049** | Linux-native share |
| **VM NIC modes** | **NAT, Bridged, Host-Only, Internal** | VirtualBox nomenclature |
| **Maximum SCP file size** | Limited by **file-system (ext4 = 16 TiB, NTFS = 256 TiB)** | Not by the protocol |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examinations and lab records, students are expected to know **why TCP port 22 is used** (it is the well-known port registered with IANA for the SSH server) and **why a static or known IP is preferred** (so that the source VM can address the destination unambiguously each time).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying "Why" — Layered Model

The operation of copying a file between two VMs is, conceptually, a textbook instance of the **TCP/IP four-layer model** in action. We can break the operation into five logical steps, each tied to a network layer:

1. **Application Layer:** The user invokes `scp source.txt user@192.168.56.102:/home/user/`. This constructs a high-level *intent*: *"Send the bytes of source.txt to the SSH server on host 192.168.56.102, into the path /home/user/."*
2. **Transport Layer:** SSH runs on top of **TCP port 22**. The `scp` client opens a TCP control channel, optionally opens a second TCP data channel (older SCP implementations) or multiplexes data over the same encrypted channel (modern OpenSSH ≥ 5.4).
3. **Network / Internet Layer:** The client resolves `192.168.56.102` is already a numeric IPv4 address (no DNS needed). It wraps the TCP segment inside an **IP packet** with source IP = VM-A's IP and destination IP = VM-B's IP.
4. **Data-Link / Virtual Link Layer:** The IP packet is encapsulated inside an **Ethernet frame** whose MAC addresses are those of the two *virtual* NICs (or, in NAT mode, the host's NAT engine and VM-B's virtual NIC). The hypervisor's virtual switch delivers the frame.
5. **Physical Layer:** The frame travels over the **virtual cable** that the hypervisor has internally wired between the two VMs (or, in Bridged mode, out through the host's real NIC and back in).

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this important for exams?** The KTU examiner will award marks if you can map each command (`scp`, `ifconfig`, `systemctl`) to its corresponding **OSI/TCP-IP layer**. This demonstrates conceptual depth beyond mere "type-the-command" recall.

### 2.2 Authentication — The Two Acceptable Methods

| Method | What is exchanged | Security posture | Typical lab use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Password authentication** | User types the remote user's password over an *encrypted* channel | Moderate; vulnerable to brute force if exposed to the Internet | Quick lab demos |
| **Public-key authentication (RSA / Ed25519)** | Client proves possession of the private key by signing a server challenge | **Strong**; resistant to brute force | Production systems, viva questions |

In KTU 2024 lab records, the **password method is sufficient** to earn full marks, but using **key-based authentication** is the examiner's preferred mark-distinction signal.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Parameter Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Value / Formula / Default | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Default SSH port | **22 / TCP** | Firewall rules must allow it |
| 2 | SCP transfer speed bound | $\le \min(B_{link},\;B_{disk})$ where $B$ is bandwidth | No protocol-imposed cap |
| 3 | Encrypted block size (AES-256-CTR) | **128 bits per block** | Determines the cipher's security level |
| 4 | Round-trip time of SSH handshake | $T_{rtt} \approx 2 \cdot \frac{d_{cable}}{v_{signal}}$ | Dominates the *latency* of small-file copy |
| 5 | VirtualBox Host-Only subnet | **192.168.56.0/24** (default) | Lets two VMs talk without a router |
| 6 | VirtualBox Internal network | **No host access, VM-to-VM only** | More isolated; ideal for lab |
| 7 | `scp` exit code | **0 = success, non-zero = failure** | Used in shell scripts to check success |
| 8 | File integrity check (post-copy) | `md5sum` / `sha256sum` must match on both VMs | Proves the copy was lossless |
| 9 | SSH key size (recommended) | **RSA ≥ 2048 bits, Ed25519 = 256 bits** | Below 2048 is deprecated |
| 10 | Default SSH config file | `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` on server, `~/.ssh/config` on client | The first place to debug |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **IT industry**, the same `scp`/`rsync` workflow is used every day by:
- **DevOps engineers** pushing configuration files (`*.conf`, `*.yml`) to production servers.
- **Site Reliability Engineers (SREs)** distributing cryptographic keys across server clusters.
- **Data engineers** staging large datasets (terabytes) onto Hadoop / Spark worker nodes.
- **Embedded systems engineers** pushing firmware images to development boards over a LAN.

Thus, the small KTU lab experiment is, in fact, the **foundational micro-skill** behind a large fraction of the world's day-to-day infrastructure automation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following procedure assumes **Oracle VirtualBox 7.x** with two **Ubuntu 22.04 LTS** guest VMs, both attached to a **Host-Only Adapter** (the most common KTU lab configuration). The procedure is **generic** and works equivalently on VMware Workstation, Fedora, CentOS, or Debian guests, with minor command-syntax adjustments.

### 3.1 Phase 1 — Hardware / Hypervisor Configuration (No commands inside the guest)

**Step 1.1** Open VirtualBox Manager. For *both* VMs (let's name them `VM-Source` and `VM-Dest`):

1. Power off the VM.
2. Open **Settings → Network → Adapter 2**.
3. Check **Enable Network Adapter**.
4. Attached to: **Host-Only Adapter**.
5. Promiscuous Mode: **Allow All** (necessary for the virtual switch).
6. Click **OK**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Adapter 2 and not Adapter 1?** Adapter 1 is typically kept as **NAT** so the VM retains Internet access. The Host-Only Adapter is added as a *second* NIC purely for VM-to-VM traffic. This is the standard KTU-2024 lab topology.

**Step 1.2** Confirm the Host-Only network's subnet:

On the **host machine**, open a terminal and run:

```
VBoxManage list hostonlyifs
```

Expected output (truncated):

```
Name:            VirtualBox Host-Only Ethernet Adapter
IPAddress:       192.168.56.1
NetworkMask:     255.255.255.0
```

This confirms the host occupies **192.168.56.1** and the guest VMs must be assigned free addresses in the same **/24** subnet, e.g., `192.168.56.101` and `192.168.56.102`.

### 3.2 Phase 2 — Inside-VM Network Configuration

**Step 2.1** Start both VMs. In each VM, open a terminal and list interfaces:

```
ip a
```

The second interface (e.g., `enp0s8` in VirtualBox's default NIC naming) should be **DOWN** and have no IPv4 address. We will configure static IPs.

**Step 2.2** On `VM-Source` (the sender), assign a static IP using Netplan (Ubuntu 18.04+):

Edit the file `/etc/netplan/01-netcfg.yaml`:

```
network:
  version: 2
  renderer: networkd
  ethernets:
    enp0s8:
      dhcp4: no
      addresses:
        - 192.168.56.101/24
```

Apply the configuration:

```
sudo netplan apply
```

**Step 2.3** On `VM-Dest` (the receiver), perform the analogous change with IP **192.168.56.102/24**.

**Step 2.4** Test reachability from `VM-Source`:

```
ping -c 4 192.168.56.102
```

You should see four replies with `time=` values in the low-millisecond range. **If you do not, stop and re-check Steps 1.1–2.3** — the file copy will fail otherwise.

### 3.3 Phase 3 — Install and Start the SSH Server (on the destination)

**Step 3.1** On `VM-Dest`, install the OpenSSH server:

```
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y openssh-server
```

**Step 3.2** Enable and start the daemon:

```
sudo systemctl enable --now ssh
sudo systemctl status ssh
```

The status line should read **`active (running)`** in green. The SSH server is now listening on **TCP port 22**.

**Step 3.3** Confirm with:

```
sudo ss -tlnp | grep :22
```

Expected output:

```
LISTEN 0 128 0.0.0.0:22 0.0.0.0:* users:(("sshd",pid=...,fd=...))
```

### 3.4 Phase 4 — File Copy (the actual experiment)

**Step 4.1** Create a sample file on `VM-Source`:

```
echo "Hello from VM-Source at $(hostname) on $(date)" > ~/demo.txt
cat ~/demo.txt
```

**Step 4.2** Use `scp` to copy it to `VM-Dest`:

```
scp ~/demo.txt student@192.168.56.102:/home/student/
```

- **First-time prompt:** *"Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])?"* — type `yes`.
- **Password prompt:** Enter `student`'s password on `VM-Dest`.
- **Output on success:** `demo.txt 100% 47 12.3KB/s 00:00`.

**Step 4.3** Verify on `VM-Dest`:

```
cat /home/student/demo.txt
md5sum /home/student/demo.txt
```

**Step 4.4** Verify the source checksum matches (on `VM-Source`):

```
md5sum ~/demo.txt
```

The two MD5 hashes must be **identical** → integrity confirmed.

### 3.5 Phase 5 — Alternative Methods (for higher marks / viva)

#### 3.5.1 Using `sftp` (interactive)

```
sftp student@192.168.56.102
sftp> put demo.txt
sftp> ls
sftp> bye
```

#### 3.5.2 Using `rsync` (delta transfer)

```
rsync -avz -e ssh ~/demo.txt student@192.168.56.102:/home/student/
```

Flag meanings:

- **`-a`** → archive mode (preserves permissions, timestamps, symlinks).
- **`-v`** → verbose.
- **`-z`** → compress during transfer (saves bandwidth on slow links).

#### 3.5.3 Using `sshpass` for non-interactive scripts

```
sudo apt install -y sshpass
sshpass -p 'password' scp ~/demo.txt student@192.168.56.102:/home/student/
```

> [!WARNING]
> `sshpass` is **acceptable in a closed lab** but **never used in production** — passwords in command-line history are a major security risk.

### 3.6 Phase 6 — Analytical Comparison Matrix (the heart of the viva)

| Feature | `scp` | `sftp` | `rsync` | `nc` (netcat) | NFS mount |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Encryption** | ✅ (SSH) | ✅ (SSH) | ✅ (SSH) | ❌ None | ❌ Plain or Kerberos |
| **Interactive** | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (mount-based) |
| **Resumes partial transfers** | ❌ | ✅ (`reget`) | ✅ (`--partial`) | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Delta transfer** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Default port** | 22 | 22 | 22 | any | 2049 |
| **Auth via SSH keys** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | N/A | N/A (uses Kerberos/UID) |
| **Suitable for huge files (≥10 GB)** | ⚠️ | ⚠️ | ✅ | ✅ (fastest, unencrypted) | ✅ |
| **KTU lab suitability** | **Best** | Good | Good | Rare | Rare |

### 3.7 Mathematical / Quantitative Insight

The **effective throughput** of an `scp` transfer is governed by the classical capacity-time trade-off:

$$
T_{total} = T_{handshake} + T_{transfer} + T_{ack}
$$

where, in milliseconds,

$$
T_{handshake} \approx 3 \cdot RTT + 2 \cdot \text{server-side key-exchange latency}
$$

$$
T_{transfer} = \frac{S_{file}}{B_{link} \cdot (1 - L_{loss})}
$$

$$
T_{ack} = RTT \cdot \lceil \log_2(W_{window}) \rceil
$$

A worked example (a typical KTU calculation):

- **File size** $S_{file} = 10$ MB $= 10 \times 2^{20}$ bytes
- **Link bandwidth** $B_{link} = 100$ Mbps $= 12.5$ MB/s
- **Packet loss** $L_{loss} = 0.01$
- **RTT** = 1 ms (host-only virtual link)

$$
T_{transfer} = \frac{10 \times 2^{20}}{12.5 \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.01)} \approx 0.85 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{handshake} \approx 3 \times 1 \text{ ms} + 2 \times 80 \text{ ms} = 163 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{ack} \approx 1 \text{ ms} \cdot \lceil \log_2(64) \rceil = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{total} \approx 163 + 850 + 6 = 1019 \text{ ms} \approx 1.02 \text{ s}
$$

This result illustrates a key engineering insight: for a small file, the **SSH handshake** dominates; for a large file, the **bulk transfer** dominates.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q1.** What is the default port number of the SSH server, and how do you check whether the SSH service is currently running on a Linux machine?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The default port number of the SSH server is **TCP 22**, registered with the IANA. (1 mark)
2. The service is provided by the daemon `sshd`, whose configuration lives in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`. (1 mark)
3. To verify it is running, execute `sudo systemctl status ssh` (or `sudo service ssh status`) — a green `active (running)` line confirms it. Alternatively, `sudo ss -tlnp | grep :22` shows the listening socket. (1 mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between `scp` and `rsync` for copying files between two Linux machines.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. `scp` is a **simple, non-interactive, one-shot copy** utility that always re-transmits the entire file, even if only a few bytes have changed. (1 mark)
2. `rsync` is a **delta-transfer** utility: it computes block-level checksums on both ends and transmits only the blocks that differ, dramatically reducing bandwidth for repeated transfers of large files. (1 mark)
3. Both can run over SSH for security, but `rsync` additionally supports compression (`-z`), archive mode (`-a`), and resume of partial transfers (`--partial`), making it the preferred choice for production backups. (1 mark)

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> (a) With a neat diagram, explain the procedure to copy a file from one virtual machine to another virtual machine in VirtualBox. (7 marks)
> (b) Demonstrate how `scp` and `rsync` can be used to copy files. Show the commands and explain the flags used. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the lab goal — *To copy a file from VM-A (source) to VM-B (destination) running on the same VirtualBox hypervisor, using a secure protocol.*

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Network configuration. Attach both VMs to a **Host-Only Adapter** (Adapter 2) so they share a private subnet `192.168.56.0/24`. Assign static IPs:
- VM-A → `192.168.56.101/24`
- VM-B → `192.168.56.102/24`

Verify with `ping -c 4 192.168.56.102` from VM-A.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** SSH server installation. On VM-B:

```
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y openssh-server
sudo systemctl enable --now ssh
```

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Diagram (as in Section 4.1 above). Mark the path of the file.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Verification of successful copy using `md5sum` on both VMs; the hashes must match.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**`scp` command (3 marks):**

```
scp -P 22 -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa ~/demo.txt student@192.168.56.102:/home/student/
```

Flag explanations:
- **`-P 22`** → Non-default port specification (default is 22, so this is illustrative).
- **`-i ~/.ssh/id_rsa`** → Identity file (private key) for key-based authentication.

If password authentication is used, omit `-i` and enter the password when prompted.

**`rsync` command (3 marks):**

```
rsync -avz -e ssh --progress ~/demo.txt student@192.168.56.102:/home/student/
```

Flag explanations:
- **`-a`** → Archive (recursive, preserves permissions, timestamps, symlinks).
- **`-v`** → Verbose output.
- **`-z`** → Compress during transfer.
- **`-e ssh`** → Use SSH as the transport.
- **`--progress`** → Show real-time progress.

**Comparison summary (1 mark):** `scp` is simpler and ideal for one-off copies. `rsync` is preferable for large or repetitive transfers because of its delta-transfer algorithm.

---

> **[Internal Choice — Alternative Question B (14 Marks):]**
> (a) Explain three different network adapter modes in VirtualBox. Which one is most appropriate for VM-to-VM file transfer inside a lab, and why? (7 marks)
> (b) Write the step-by-step procedure to enable **SSH key-based authentication** between two VMs, and explain why it is preferred over password authentication. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Three VirtualBox NIC modes (3 × 1.5 = 4.5 marks):**

1. **NAT (Network Address Translation):** The VM shares the host's IP for outbound Internet access. VMs in NAT mode **cannot** directly see each other; they would need port-forwarding rules.
2. **Bridged Adapter:** The VM appears as a peer on the host's physical LAN, with its own IP from the LAN's DHCP. Useful when the VM must be reachable from *other* physical machines.
3. **Host-Only Adapter:** The VM is connected to a private virtual network shared with the host but **isolated from the physical LAN**. Two VMs attached to the *same* host-only network can communicate freely. (1.5 marks)

**Most appropriate for KTU lab (1.5 marks):** **Host-Only Adapter**, because it provides a *clean, isolated* subnet for VM-to-VM communication **without** requiring the lab's physical network infrastructure, while still allowing the host to participate for debugging.

**Why not NAT?** NAT requires port-forwarding per VM and is awkward to script. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step-by-step procedure (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — On VM-Source, generate an SSH key pair:**

```
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "lab-key"
```

This creates:
- Private key: `~/.ssh/id_ed25519` (never shared)
- Public key: `~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub`

**Step 2 — Push the public key to VM-Dest:**

```
ssh-copy-id student@192.168.56.102
```

Or, manually:

```
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub | ssh student@192.168.56.102 \
  "mkdir -p ~/.ssh && chmod 700 ~/.ssh && cat >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys && chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys"
```

**Step 3 — Test password-less login:**

```
ssh student@192.168.56.102
```

**Step 4 — Disable password authentication on VM-Dest (optional, for full marks):**

Edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`:

```
PasswordAuthentication no
```

Then restart:

```
sudo systemctl restart ssh
```

**Why key-based is preferred (2 marks):**
1. **Immune to brute-force password guessing**, because no password ever leaves the client.
2. **Cryptographically strong** — a 256-bit Ed25519 key provides ~128 bits of security, equivalent to a 3072-bit RSA key, at a fraction of the CPU cost.
3. **Automatable** in scripts and CI/CD pipelines without exposing credentials.
4. **Auditable** — each authorized key has a comment that identifies the originating user/host.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this experiment:**
> 1. **Forgetting to install `openssh-server` on the destination VM.** Many students assume SSH is preinstalled; it is not on a minimal Ubuntu image. The error *"Connection refused"* is the symptom. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Using NAT mode for both VMs.** NAT isolates VMs from each other; the `ping` test fails silently. Always use **Host-Only** (or **Internal Network**) for VM-to-VM traffic. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Not verifying file integrity with `md5sum` or `sha256sum`.** Simply running `cat` on the destination is not enough for full marks. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Forgetting to enable the second network adapter in VirtualBox settings.** The VM has a NIC, but it is *unplugged* in the virtual topology. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Confusing `scp -P` (capital P, for port) with `scp -p` (lowercase, for preserving file modes).** This subtle error is a favourite viva trap. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Not recording the output of `ifconfig`/`ip a` and `systemctl status ssh` in the lab record.** Examiners reward *evidence* — paste the actual terminal output into the record. (–1 mark)

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Default SSH port:** **TCP 22**.
- **Default VirtualBox host-only subnet:** **192.168.56.0/24**, host at **192.168.56.1**.
- **Two main tools:** `scp` (simple) and `rsync` (delta, resumable).
- **Three authentication modes:** password, key-based, host-based.
- **Always verify** file integrity with `md5sum` or `sha256sum` after the copy.
- **Always test reachability** with `ping` before attempting the copy.
- **Always start `sshd`** on the destination: `sudo systemctl enable --now ssh`.
- **NIC mode rule of thumb:** use **Host-Only** for VM-to-VM, keep **NAT** on Adapter 1 for Internet.
- **Security:** prefer **Ed25519** keys over RSA-2048; disable `PasswordAuthentication` once keys work.
- **Viva question to expect:** *"What is the difference between `scp` and `sftp`? Which is more secure? Which is more feature-rich?"* — `sftp` is more feature-rich; both share the same security because both run over SSH.
- **Edge case:** If you see *"REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED"*, the destination's host key has been re-generated (e.g., after a VM clone). Remove the stale entry with `ssh-keygen -R 192.168.56.102` on the source.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision mnemonic — "CPAS":**
> **C**onfigure NIC → **P**ing test → **A**uthenticate (SSH) → **S**ecurely copy.
> Memorise these four stages in order and you will never miss a procedural mark.
